Because Sabonete plans to hedge its liabilities in five years, its investment horizon is too short to capture the illiquidity premium that is a critical component of the endowment model.
rationales.B: Sabonete's investment horizon is too short to capture the illiquidity premium that is a critical component of the endowment model. Further, the higher costs of the model are inconsistent with Sabonete's objective to minimize the investment and administrative costs associated with the plan.
rationales.C: The endowment model can be expected to generate higher returns because of the illiquidity premium, which would more than offset the higher costs. However, Sabonete's investment horizon is too short to capture the illiquidity premium inherent in the model. Further, the higher costs of the model are inconsistent with Sabonete's desire to minimize the investment and administrative costs associated with the plan.

questions.stem: Radell's Statement 1 says Option 1 is most consistent with Sabonete's objectives based on Sharpe ratio. DETERMINE if he is correct and JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT = INCORRECT.
Accepted justifications (any ONE):
• Sabonete's stated objectives include minimizing contribution volatility and reaching fully-funded status; a higher Sharpe alone does not address these.
• Option 1 has HIGHER expected contribution volatility per Horvath's note (variable interim contributions), conflicting with the volatility-minimization goal.
• Option 2 better fits the liability-relative objective by lowering contribution variability, even at slightly lower expected return.
• Sharpe ratio is an asset-only metric; DB pension objectives are liability-relative.

PARTIAL: 5=verdict + reasoning; 3=verdict correct, reasoning thin; 0=wrong.

questions.stem: Radell's Statement 2 says the 10% real estate allocation is too high given Sabonete's recent African real estate acquisitions. DETERMINE if correct + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT = CORRECT.
Accepted justifications (any ONE):
• On the economic balance sheet, the firm's €200M African land + planned manufacturing investment concentrate operating exposure to African RE — adding 10% financial RE compounds this concentration.
• The plan sponsor's asset base is already heavily exposed to real estate through operations, so the SPP should hold LESS to diversify total sponsor exposure.
• Correlated risks between sponsor and plan reduce ability to fund shortfalls exactly when the plan needs help.

PARTIAL: 5=verdict + reasoning; 3=verdict correct, weak reasoning; 0=wrong.

questions.stem: Radell's Statement 3 says the 20% emerging-market equity allocation is too high given the firm's revenue sensitivity to emerging markets. DETERMINE + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT = CORRECT.
Accepted justifications (any ONE):
• 60% of Sabonete's revenues are from emerging markets; this is significant sponsor exposure to EM economic conditions.
• Economic balance sheet perspective: SPP's EM equity concentration correlates with sponsor cash flows, reducing diversification.
• EM contribution to global market portfolio is only ~10%; overweight is unjustified given already-high operational EM exposure.
• Correlation between contributions capacity and SPP returns is worsened.

PARTIAL: 5=verdict + reasoning; 3=verdict, weak reasoning; 0=wrong.

questions.stem: Radell says a WIDE rebalancing corridor for global fixed income is appropriate because of its low volatility, low transaction costs, and low correlation with the rest of the portfolio. EVALUATE each of the three rationales.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — evaluate each rationale against the standard corridor-width factors.

===
RATIONALE 1 (LOW VOLATILITY justifies a wide corridor): CORRECT.
• Higher asset-class volatility calls for a NARROWER corridor (large divergences become likely quickly); therefore LOW volatility supports a WIDER corridor.

===
RATIONALE 2 (LOW TRANSACTION COSTS justify a wide corridor): INCORRECT.
• Higher transaction costs → WIDER corridor (rebalancing is expensive, tolerate drift). LOW transaction costs make frequent rebalancing cheap → support a NARROWER corridor, not a wider one.

===
RATIONALE 3 (LOW CORRELATION with the rest of the portfolio justifies a wide corridor): INCORRECT.
• Higher correlation → WIDER corridor (the class moves with the portfolio, so drift matters less). LOW correlation means independent movement changes portfolio risk more → NARROWER corridor.

PARTIAL: full=all three with correct directions; two-thirds=two; one-third=one; 0=none.

cases.title: Vitting (Teddy Brealer)
cases.vignette:
Teddy Brealer is the president of Vitting University (VU). VU just
successfully completed a fundraising campaign of $300 million that
significantly increased the funds in the endowment (Exhibit 1).

**Exhibit 1 Vitting University Endowment Fund Assets and Obligations ($
millions)**

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Immediately available funds                          50.2
-------------------------------------------------------- --------------
**Immediately available funds, restricted to             53.2
scholarships**

Current endowment obligations                        89.5

PV of future endowment obligations                 502.4

**PV of pledged future contributions to endowment,       502.4
unrestricted***

**PV of pledged future contributions, restricted to      87.8
scholarships**
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

These funds may be used for scholarship and non-scholarship items

At a meeting with VU's board of regents, Brealer proposes that the
endowment should fund a new capital improvements project for the
university that will cost $210.3 million. Brealer acknowledges that it
is a large amount that will require another fundraising campaign in the
future, but he states that he has already found a wealthy alumnus, Roger
Clement, who is willing to donate $50 million if the project is
undertaken.

Jennifer Wong, a board member, asks: "If we undertake the project with
Clement's donation but without a new fundraising campaign, how much will
the endowment be underfunded?" Other board members agree that the answer
to this question should be considered before making a decision.
Consequently, the board decides to discuss Brealer's proposal further at
a future meeting.

The board moves on to a discussion about the investment of the endowment
with the recently raised funds. Ronald Black, an investment adviser to
the board, suggests the following:

-   The asset allocation choice should have a heavy emphasis on
fixed-income securities with cash distributions. This type of
allocation will offset the future cash disbursements necessary to
cover costs at the university in excess of tuition revenue.

-   The weightings within the portfolio should be able to deviate within
5% of the target portfolio weights to take advantage of short-term
market opportunities for additional return.

Brealer suggests bringing Black's portfolio allocation ideas to the
University Planning and Priorities Committee (UPPC). The UPPC is
composed of six tenured faculty members and three final-year students.
Three of the faculty members are from the arts area, and three are from
the sciences area. Faculty appointments to the committee are for
two-year, non-renewable terms.

Brealer states that the UPPC desires to have the endowment invested in a
socially responsible manner, which will require developing a new
investment policy statement (IPS). The UPPC intends to draft the new IPS
and present it to the board for approval. Upon receiving board approval,
the UPPC will direct a financial advisory team and the investment
managers to implement the asset allocation indicated in the IPS.
Progress reports and governance audits will be provided to the board
upon request.

The board is agreeable to this plan and assigns Black to be part of the
advisory team after the IPS is drafted and approved.

Following approval of the IPS by the board, the UPPC starts considering
different asset allocations with a target annual return for the
endowment of 5%, while taking into consideration the expected return and
volatility of the given portfolio. Three strategies are presented to the
committee based on socially responsible investing (Exhibit 2). The
committee's goal is to find the allocation that has the highest
probability of meeting the desired return criteria.

Black states that the UPPC should also consider such issues as the cost
associated with rebalancing the portfolio and that the future
distribution of asset returns may not be fully characterized by the
expected return and volatility. Black further states that his preferred
method for dealing with these additional allocation issues is the use of
Monte Carlo simulation.

questions.stem:
Based on an economic balance sheet, the answer to Wong's question
about the underfunding of the endowment (in $ millions)
is closest to:

rationales.A: Correct. An economic balance sheet includes extended assets and liabilities. Assets: immediately available funds + funds restricted to scholarships + PV of pledged future contributions (unrestricted and restricted) + Clement's $50.0 million conditional donation. Liabilities: current endowment obligations + PV of future endowment obligations + the $210.3 million project cost. Liabilities exceed assets by approximately $55.6 million — the underfunding.
rationales.B: Incorrect. This figure omits Clement's $50.0 million donation from the asset side. He has committed to donate if the project is undertaken, so on an economic balance sheet the pledge is an extended asset; excluding it overstates the underfunding by exactly $50 million.
rationales.C: Incorrect. This figure wrongly excludes the scholarship-restricted funds and scholarship-restricted pledged contributions from the asset side while retaining all obligations. The economic balance sheet includes ALL assets and liabilities — restricted assets fund the scholarship component of the endowment's obligations.

questions.stem:
Which asset allocation approach *best* describes the asset
allocation choice suggested by Black to the board?

rationales.A: Correct. Black recommends a heavy fixed-income allocation with cash distributions specifically to OFFSET the university's future cash disbursements — choosing assets in relation to the obligations they must fund. Selecting an asset allocation relative to the investor's liabilities (rather than on asset characteristics alone) is the liability-relative approach.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Mean-variance optimization is an asset-only approach that maximizes return for a level of risk without reference to liabilities. Black's rationale is explicitly built around funding the university's future disbursements.
rationales.C: Incorrect. 'Asset segmentation' is not one of the curriculum's asset allocation approaches (asset-only, liability-relative, goals-based). Black's disbursement-offsetting logic is liability-relative.

questions.stem:
Black's suggestion to the board in regard to the asset weightings in
the endowment portfolio is best described as allowing for an asset
allocation that is:

rationales.A: The ability to deviate from target portfolio weightings for short-term market opportunities is an example of tactical asset allocation, which is an active strategy. Dynamic asset allocation is a long-term active strategy, and indexing is a passive strategy.
rationales.B: Dynamic asset allocation is a long-term active strategy, not a short-term strategy.
rationales.C: The ability to deviate from target portfolio weightings for short-term market opportunities is an example of tactical asset allocation, which is an active strategy and not a passive strategy, such as indexing strategies.

questions.stem:
The process for creating and implementing the investment policy
statement (IPS) by the University Planning and Priorities Committee
(UPPC) most likely follows best governance practices in regard to:

options.B: expertise for developing the asset allocation.
options.C: transparency of decision rights for approving a proposed asset allocation.

rationales.A: Governance audits should be periodic and not on an "as requested" basis.
rationales.B: The committee members have very short terms (two years for faculty and one year for students because they are final year students) and appear to lack financial expertise.
rationales.C: The UPPC must seek the board of regents' approval for any asset allocation the committee proposes to implement. Consequently, the decision rights in regard to the asset allocation process are very transparent, which is consistent with best governance practices. Governance audits being on an "as requested" basis instead of being periodic is inconsistent with best governance practices. The committee members' short terms (two years for faculty and one year for students because they are final-year students) and apparent lack of financial expertise are also inconsistent with best governance practices.

questions.stem:
The asset allocation choice in Exhibit 2 that has the* *highest
probability of meeting the committee's desired return criteria is
allocation:

rationales.A: Incorrect. Allocation 1 has the highest Ratio 1 (Sharpe ratio, (k − R_F)/σ = 0.262), but the Sharpe ratio measures excess return over the RISK-FREE rate per unit of total risk. The committee's criterion is the probability of meeting the 5.0% TARGET return, which Roy's safety-first ratio (Ratio 2) measures.
rationales.B: Correct. The committee wants the allocation with the highest probability of meeting the 5.0% target return. Roy's safety-first criterion selects the portfolio with the highest ratio (k − k_T)/σ — Ratio 2 in Exhibit 2. Allocation 2's Ratio 2 of 0.077 exceeds Allocation 1's 0.067 and Allocation 3's 0.070, so Allocation 2 minimizes the probability of falling below the 5.0% target.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Allocation 3 has the highest Ratio 3 (Treynor measure, (k − R_F)/β = 0.041), but beta-based measures gauge return per unit of systematic risk against the risk-free rate. They say nothing about shortfall probability versus the committee's 5.0% target; Roy's safety-first ratio (Ratio 2) does, and Allocation 2 ranks highest.

questions.stem:
When addressing the University Planning and Priorities Committee,
Black's preferred approach for dealing with the additional allocation
issues is most likely:

options.B: incorrect because it is unable to address rebalancing costs.
options.C: incorrect because it is unable to address distributions that are dependent on parameters other than expected return and volatility.

rationales.A: Correct. Black's preferred method is Monte Carlo simulation, and it is well suited to the additional issues he raises: simulation can incorporate portfolio rebalancing costs and can model return distributions that require more parameters than expected return and volatility (non-normal distributions).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Monte Carlo simulation CAN address rebalancing costs — trading costs can be built into the simulated paths, which is one of its advantages over single-period mean-variance analysis.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Monte Carlo simulation CAN accommodate distributions characterized by parameters beyond expected return and volatility (skewness, fat tails, path dependence) — that flexibility is precisely why Black prefers it for these issues.

questions.stem: Wong asks how underfunded the endowment would be if the $210.3M project is undertaken with Clement's $50M donation but no new fundraising. CALCULATE the underfunded amount. SHOW your work.

rationales.model_answer:
ANSWER: approximately $199.6M underfunded.

CALCULATION (accepted):
Unrestricted assets (economic balance sheet):
  Immediately available funds: $50.2M
  + PV of pledged unrestricted contributions: $502.4M
  + Clement donation: $50M
  = $602.6M assets

Unrestricted liabilities/obligations:
  Current endowment obligations: $89.5M
  + PV of future endowment obligations: $502.4M
  + New project cost: $210.3M
  = $802.2M obligations

Underfunded = $802.2M - $602.6M = $199.6M

PARTIAL CREDIT:
• 5 = correct answer + clear work.
• 4 = correct method, small arithmetic error.
• 3 = correct approach (economic balance sheet), material error.
• 2 = shows partial understanding of assets/liabilities.
• 0 = wrong or blank.
Accepted range: $195-205M for full credit.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two governance concerns with delegating IPS drafting to the University Planning and Priorities Committee (UPPC).

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following earn full credit.

Accepted concerns:
• Faculty members serve 2-year non-renewable terms — insufficient continuity and time to build investment expertise.
• Committee composition (faculty + students) lacks required investment expertise for IPS drafting.
• Student members may lack fiduciary experience or knowledge of long-horizon endowment principles.
• No clear accountability — UPPC drafts, board approves, but implementation is delegated with only 'progress reports on request.'
• Potential conflict between UPPC's socially-responsible mandate and best risk-adjusted returns without documented tradeoffs.
• Committee is not answerable directly to donors or beneficiaries.

PARTIAL: 5=two distinct valid concerns; 3=one valid concern; 0=vague/none.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Ronald Black's two portfolio-allocation suggestions: (i) heavy fixed-income emphasis, and (ii) allowing 5% deviations for short-term market opportunities.

rationales.model_answer:
===
PART i (fixed-income emphasis to match cash disbursements): APPROPRIATE.
Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Liability-matching / cash-flow matching supports fixed income for known future obligations.
• Reduces mismatch between assets and near-term obligations.
• Consistent with liability-relative asset allocation.

===
PART ii (5% deviations for short-term opportunities): NOT APPROPRIATE.
Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Endowment purpose is long-term, perpetual funding — strategic AA should not chase short-term market opportunities.
• Introduces active-management risk and drift from strategic policy.
• Endowments should rebalance to strategic weights, not deviate tactically.
• Increases transaction costs without necessarily improving long-term outcomes.

PARTIAL: 5=both correct + reasoning; 4=both correct, thin on one; 3=one correct with reasoning; 0=both wrong.

questions.stem: Given the three allocations in Exhibit 2 and the 5% target return, DETERMINE which has the highest probability of meeting the target + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT = ALLOCATION 2 (highest Ratio 2 = 0.077).

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Ratio 2 = (k − target)/σ measures probability of meeting a target under safety-first / Roy's criterion; Allocation 2 has highest Ratio 2 (0.077 > 0.070 > 0.067).
• Highest Roy's ratio → lowest probability of shortfall below target.
• Allocation 2 optimizes excess return above target per unit of volatility.

COMMON WRONG ANSWERS TO PENALIZE:
• Allocation 3 (highest return but also highest vol; lower Ratio 2).
• Allocation 1 (highest Sharpe/Ratio 1 vs risk-free, but 5% is not the risk-free rate — target is 5%).

PARTIAL: 5=verdict + Ratio 2 rationale; 3=verdict, weak reasoning; 0=wrong.

cases.title: Windsong (Eunice Fox) Scenario
cases.vignette:

Management, Inc. (WWM). WWM's clients include pension funds,
foundations, sovereign funds, high-net-worth individuals, and family
trusts. Fox is in the process of hiring an asset allocation analyst and
has just completed interviewing two candidates, Ambrose Kelly and
Catherine Trainor, for the position. The interviews were directed around
the case study of Jane Lennon, a fictitious client, described in
Exhibit 1. Fox reviews her interview notes.

Exhibit 1 Case Study of Jane Lennon

Fox told the candidates to assume that Lennon would use sub-portfolios
to achieve her aspirational goals and asked them to identify which of
the sub-portfolios is in the best position to tolerate the greatest risk
exposure.

In reviewing Lennon's risk tolerance, Fox pointed out that Lennon's
prior investment experience clearly indicates some behavioral biases
that would influence her reaction to any asset allocation proposals.

Fox reminded the candidates that in addition to high-net-worth
individuals, the firm's client base also includes various institutional
investors. The candidates made the following statements:

Trainor: A goals-based approach to asset allocation is appropriate
for individual investors, but institutions need to focus either on the
asset or liability side of the balance sheet, depending on the nature of
their business.

Kelly: A typical objective of some institutions is to maximize their
Sharpe ratio for an acceptable level of volatility, and they rely on the
law of large numbers to assist them in modeling their liabilities. Other
institutions behave much like individuals by segmenting general account
assets into sub-portfolios associated with specific lines of business
with their individual return objectives.

Fox mentioned to the candidates that when dealing with strategic asset
allocation, investors often had difficulty understanding the relevant
characteristics of asset classes. They responded:

Kelly: I like to stress to clients that asset classes should have
high within-group correlations but low correlations with other classes.
In addition, because investors need to rebalance to a strategic asset
allocation, asset classes need to have both sufficient liquidity and low
transaction costs.

Trainor: It is important that asset classes should be diversifying.
I always look for low pairwise correlations with other asset classes.

Other general comments were noted about asset classes, but Fox could not
recall their sources:

-   Emerging market equities should not be considered a separate asset
class from global equities.

-   Asset classes differ from strategies in offering a non--skill-based
ex ante expected return premium.

-   Asset classes should be defined in such a way that there is no
overlap in sources of risk.

questions.stem:
Based on the information in Exhibit 1,** **Lennon's economic net
worth (in $ millions) is closest to:

rationales.A: It ignores earnings until retirement (human capital): (21 -- 4.5) -- 15.25 = 1.25
rationales.B: It ignores the restricted shares but keeps everything else: (21 -- 1) -- 15.25 = 4.75
rationales.C: The economic net worth is the difference between the total assets and the total liabilities (21.00 -- 15.25 = 5.75), as calculated in the following economic balance sheet.  Economic Balance Sheet of Jane Lennon (in $ millions)  ------------------------------------------------------------------------- Assets                             Liabilities and Net Worth ---------------------------- --------- ---------------------- ----------- Financial Assets                   Financial Liabilities  Investment Portfolio         8.00      Mortgage: vacation     1.00 property  Restricted shares            1.00  Real estate: residence       2.00      Extended Liabilities:  Real estate: vacation        3.00      Everett's education   1.50 property  Defined contribution pension 2.50      Trust for Marshall     2.00 plan  University endowment   1.75  PV of future           9.00 consumption  Extended Assets:                       Total Liabilities      15.25  Human capital                4.50      Net Worth:  Economic net worth     5.75  Total Assets                 21.00     Total Liabilities and  21.00 Net Worth -------------------------------------------------------------------------

questions.stem:
Which of the sub-portfolios dedicated to Lennon's aspirational goals
is in the best position to tolerate the greatest risk exposure? The
one dedicated to:

rationales.A: Although the special needs trust for Marshall will provide benefits for his entire life, and therefore has a long-time horizon, from Lennon's perspective it requires immediate funding and should be very liquid and bear little risk.
rationales.B: Both of the funds planned for the trust and university endowment represent an imminent need (immediate for the trust and within two years for the endowment). The funding needed for education, however, extends over the longest time horizon, possibly as long as 8 to 10 years. Thus, its sub-portfolio would be in the best position to take on the greatest risk.
rationales.C: Funding of the university endowment involves a short-term time horizon (within two years) so it should bear little risk.

questions.stem:
The *most* appropriate statement in regards to approaches to asset
allocation by institutions is made by:

options.C: Kelly, regarding the Sharpe ratio and modeling of liabilities.

rationales.A: Trainor is incorrect. Some institutions may focus on asset-only allocations, but another approach that can be used is liability-relative, which focuses on the assets in relation to the liabilities.
rationales.B: Kelly's second comment regarding institutions' goals-based allocations is correct. Some institutions (e.g., insurance companies) segment their general account assets into sub-portfolios associated with specific lines of business or blocks of liabilities, with each sub-portfolio having its own return objective.
rationales.C: Kelly's first comment about the Sharpe ratio and the law of large numbers is incorrect. Institutions that maximize their Sharpe ratio for an acceptable level of volatility would be following an asset-only asset allocation approach, and, as such, they would not be concerned with modeling their liabilities.

questions.stem:
In the candidates' responses to Fox regarding the relevant
characteristics of asset classes, the statement that is least accurate
is:

rationales.A: Although Trainor is correct that asset classes should be diversifying, low pairwise correlations with other asset classes is not sufficient. An asset class may be highly correlated with some linear combination of the other asset classes even when pairwise correlations are not high. Both of Kelly's comments are correct: Asset classes should have high within-group correlations but low correlations with other classes. If liquidity and transaction costs are unfavorable for an investment of a size meaningful for an investor, an asset class may not be a suitable investment for that investor.
rationales.B: Kelly's first comment is correct about both the within-group and between class correlations.
rationales.C: Kelly's second comment is correct. The criteria that he is referring to is that asset classes should have the capacity to absorb a meaningful proportion of an investor's portfolio. He is correct in saying that if liquidity and transaction costs are unfavorable for an investment of a size meaningful for an investor, an asset class may not be a suitable investment for the investor.

questions.stem:
In the general comments about asset classes that Fox noted,
the most accurate comment is the one regarding:

options.B: the overlap of sources of risk.
options.C: the return premiums from asset classes.

rationales.A: Emerging markets equities should be considered a distinct asset class as they differ from other equities in terms of diversification potential, informational efficiency, corporate governance, taxation, and currency convertibility.
rationales.B: There will be overlap of sources of risk when asset classes are defined, e.g., US and non-US equities, or even US small and large cap equities will have some risks in common, but there should be as few common risk factors as possible, and they should have only modest correlations.
rationales.C: Asset classes should have a return premium based on an underlying market risk factor (e.g., beta) and not any underlying skill of the investor. Strategies, on the other hand, involve combinations of asset classes with the objective of earning a return based on investment skill.

questions.stem:
The behavioral bias that Lennon's past investment experience
illustrates is best described as:

rationales.A: Self-control bias is a bias in which people fail to act in pursuit of their long-term, overarching goals because of lack of discipline. Lennon does not appear to exhibit this bias because she has taken steps to deal with her specific goals, including saving for her own future and that of her children.
rationales.B: The behavioral bias illustrated in Lennon's past investment experience was loss-aversion bias: Losses are perceived as more painful than the satisfaction of equivalent gains, and assets that have incurred losses but have little chance of recovery are retained because the pain of recognizing the loss is too great. Given the risk of having to give back gains already realized, winning investments are often sold early, resulting in self-imposed limited upside potential.
rationales.C: The mental accounting bias involves setting up separate accounts or buckets for wealth, each with its own risk tolerance and expected return depending on the purpose the investor associates with it. Although one of Fox's comments refers to taking higher risk to achieve one of the goals, this is not being referred to in Exhibit 1, which deals with retaining losers and selling winners.

questions.stem: Fox asked which of Lennon's aspirational sub-portfolios is best positioned to tolerate the greatest risk exposure. DETERMINE which one and JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: the sub-portfolio funding the ENDOWED CHAIR at her alma mater.

Accepted justification (any ONE):
• In a goals-based framework, risk capacity per sub-portfolio is driven by the goal's CRITICALITY: the chair is the purely ASPIRATIONAL goal — failure to fund it fully has no impact on Lennon's or her children's welfare — so it can bear the greatest risk exposure.
• The special needs trust is the LEAST risk-tolerant despite Marshall's long lifetime horizon: it is an essential/critical goal (lifetime benefits for a dependent child) being funded within the year — candidates naming the trust are INCORRECT.
• Everett's education/practice fund is important (partially aspirational for the practice assistance) but supports a dependent's committed path — intermediate risk tolerance.

GRADER NOTE: the chair's short (2-year) funding horizon is a fair caveat and earns credit as supplementary discussion, but criticality dominates the ranking; do not award full credit for the special needs trust under a horizon-only argument.

PARTIAL: full=chair + criticality reasoning; half=identifies an aspirational goal without ranking logic; low=special needs trust; 0=consumption portfolio.

questions.stem: EVALUATE the statements by Trainor and Kelly on how institutional investors differ from individuals in asset allocation.

rationales.model_answer:
===
TRAINOR: 'Goals-based is for individuals; institutions focus on asset OR liability side depending on business.' — CORRECT / MOSTLY CORRECT.
Accepted support:
• Institutions do focus on asset-only (endowments, foundations) OR liability-relative (pensions, insurers).
• Goals-based is more common for individuals with multiple discrete goals.

===
KELLY: 'Institutions may maximize Sharpe for acceptable vol; some use law of large numbers for liabilities; others segment general accounts into sub-portfolios.'
— CORRECT.
Accepted support:
• Insurers use LLN for liability modeling.
• Insurance general accounts often segmented by line of business — analogous to individual sub-portfolios.

PARTIAL: 5=both correctly assessed + support; 3=one correctly assessed; 0=both wrong.

questions.stem: EVALUATE the statements by Kelly and Trainor on asset-class characteristics.

rationales.model_answer:
===
KELLY: 'Asset classes should have high within-group correlations, low correlations across, and sufficient liquidity + low transaction costs.' — CORRECT.
Accepted support:
• Consistent with the standard asset-class criteria (homogeneous, diversifying, investable).
• Liquidity and transaction costs matter for rebalancing to strategic weights.

===
TRAINOR: 'Asset classes should be diversifying — look for low pairwise correlations.' — INCOMPLETE / PARTIALLY CORRECT.
Accepted critique:
• Pairwise correlations alone are insufficient; should also consider factor exposures, liquidity, and non-idiosyncratic risk.
• Low correlation is necessary but not sufficient — asset classes should not overlap in sources of risk.

PARTIAL: 5=both correctly evaluated + support; 3=one correct; 0=both wrong.

questions.stem: EVALUATE each of the three unattributed general comments about asset classes.

rationales.model_answer:
===
COMMENT 1: 'EM equities should not be considered a separate asset class from global equities.' — INCORRECT.
• EM equities have distinct return drivers (idiosyncratic country/political risk, different growth dynamics) and DIVERSIFY vs developed markets — meet criteria for a separate class.
• They typically pass the low-correlation-with-other-classes and material-market-size tests.

===
COMMENT 2: 'Asset classes differ from strategies in offering a non-skill-based ex ante expected return premium.' — CORRECT.
• Asset classes reward broad risk exposure passively (beta); strategies require manager skill.

===
COMMENT 3: 'Asset classes should be defined so there is no overlap in sources of risk.' — CORRECT.
• Non-overlapping sources of risk is a defining criterion for asset classes.

PARTIAL: 5=all three correctly identified + reasoning; 3=two correct; 0=fewer than one.

cases.title: Remington (Preston Remington)
cases.vignette:
Preston Remington is the managing partner of Remington Wealth Partners.
The firm manages high-net-worth private client investment portfolios
using various asset allocation strategies. Analyst Hannah Montgomery
assists Remington.

Remington and Montgomery's first meeting of the day is with a new
client, Spencer Shipman, who recently won $900,000 in the lottery.
Shipman wants to fund a comfortable retirement. Earning a return on his
investment portfolio that outpaces inflation over the long term is
critical to him. He plans to withdraw $54,000 from the lottery winnings
investment portfolio in one year to help fund the purchase of a vacation
home and states that it is important that he be able to withdraw the
$54,000 without reducing the initial $900,000 principal. Montgomery
suggests they use a risk-adjusted expected return approach in selecting
one of the portfolios provided in Exhibit 1.

Exhibit 1 Investment Portfolio One-Year Projections

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Return      Standard Deviation
--------------------------- --------------- ----------------------------
Portfolio 1                 10.50%          20.0%

Portfolio 2                 9.00            13.0

Portfolio 3                 7.75            10.0

All data are tax adjusted
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Remington and Montgomery discuss the importance of strategic asset
allocation with Shipman. Remington states that the firm's practice is to
establish targeted asset allocations and a corridor around the target.
Movements of the asset allocations outside the corridor trigger a
rebalancing of the portfolio. Remington explains that for a given asset
class, the higher the transaction costs and the higher the correlation
with the rest of the portfolio, the wider the rebalancing corridor.
Montgomery adds that the higher the volatility of the rest of the
portfolio, excluding the asset class being considered, the wider the
corridor.

Remington and Montgomery next meet with client Katherine Winfield. The
firm had established Winfield's current asset allocation on the basis of
reverse optimization using the investable global market portfolio
weights with further adjustments to reflect Winfield's views on expected
returns.

Remington and Montgomery discuss with Winfield some alternative asset
allocation models that she may wish to consider, including resampled
mean--variance optimization (resampling). Remington explains that
resampling combines mean--variance optimization (MVO) with Monte Carlo
simulation, leading to more diversified asset allocations. Montgomery
comments that resampling, like other asset allocation models, is subject
to criticisms, including that risker asset allocations tend to be
under-diversified and the asset allocations inherit the estimation
errors in the original inputs.

Montgomery inquires whether asset allocation models based on heuristics
or other techniques might be of interest to Winfield and makes the
following comments:

1.  The 60/40 stock/bond heuristic optimizes the growth benefits of
equity and the risk reduction benefits of bonds.

2.  The Norway model is a variation of the endowment model that actively
invests in publicly traded securities while giving consideration to
environmental, social, and governance issues.

3.  The 1/N heuristic allocates assets equally across asset classes
with regular rebalancing without regard to return, volatility, or
correlation.

Finally, Remington and Montgomery discuss Isabelle Sebastian. During a
recent conversation, Sebastian, a long-term client with a $2,900,000
investment portfolio, reminded Remington that she will soon turn age 65
and wants to update her investment goals as follows:

Goal 1: Over the next 20 years, she needs to maintain her living
expenditures, which are currently $120,000 per year (90% probability of
success). Inflation is expected to average 2.5% annually over the time
horizon, and withdrawals take place at the beginning of the year,
starting immediately.

Goal 2: In 10 years, she wants to donate $1,500,000 in nominal
terms to a charitable foundation (85% probability of success).

Exhibit 2 provides the details of the two sub-portfolios, including
Sebastian's allocation to the sub-portfolios and the probabilities that
they will exceed the expected minimum return.

**Exhibit 2 Investment Sub-Portfolios & Minimum Expected Return for
Success Rate**

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Sub-Portfolio                   BY             CZ
----------------------------------- ------------------ -----------------
Expected return (%)                 5.70               7.10

Expected volatility (%)             5.10               7.40

Current portfolio allocations (%)   40                 60

Probability (%)                 **Minimum Expected
Return (%)**

Time Horizon: 10 years

99                                  2.90               2.50

90                                  3.40               2.80

85                                  3.60               3.00

Time horizon: 20 years

95                                  5.10               5.40

90                                  5.20               5.70

85                                  5.60               5.90

*Assume 0% correlation between the
time horizon portfolios*
------------------------------------------------------------------------

questions.stem:
Which of the portfolios provided in Exhibit 1 has the highest
probability of enabling Shipman to meet his goal for the vacation home?

rationales.A: Portfolio 1 was chosen because it has the highest projected return.
rationales.B: Portfolio 2 has the highest probability of enabling Shipman to meet his goal for the vacation home. All three of the portfolios' expected returns over the next year exceed the 6.0% (see calculations below) required return threshold to avoid reducing the portfolio. However, on a risk-adjusted basis, Portfolio 2 (probability ratio of 0.231) has a higher probability of meeting and surpassing the threshold than either Portfolio 1 (probability ratio of 0.225) or Portfolio 3 (probability ratio of 0.175).    +------+---------------------------------------------------------------+ | Step | Calculate the required return threshold: 54,000 ÷ 900,000 =   | | 1    | 0.06 = 6.0%.                                                  | +======+===============================================================+ | Step | To decide which allocation is best for Shipman, calculate the | | 2    | probability ratio:                                            | |      |                                                               | |      |                                                               | +------+---------------------------------------------------------------+  > [E(R~P~) -- R~L~] ÷ σ~P~, where > > R~P~ = The return for the portfolio > > R~L~ = The required return threshold > > σ~P~ = The standard deviation of the portfolio > > > > Portfolio 1: (10.50% -- 6.0%) ÷ 20.0% = 4.50% ÷ 20.0% = 0.225. > > > > Portfolio 2: (9.00% -- 6.0%) ÷ 13.0% = 3.00% ÷ 13.0% = 0.231. > (Highest) > > > > Portfolio 3: (7.75% -- 6.0%) ÷ 10.0% = 1.75% ÷ 10.0% = 0.175.
rationales.C: Portfolio 3 was chosen because it has the lowest projected standard deviation.

questions.stem:
When discussing asset allocation corridors with Shipman, which of
Remington's and Montgomery's statements is the least accurate? The one
regarding:

rationales.A: The statement regarding volatility is the least accurate. The higher the volatility of the rest of the portfolio, excluding the asset class being considered, the more likely a large divergence from the strategic asset allocation becomes, which should point to a narrower optimal corridor, all else being equal.
rationales.B: The higher the correlation of an asset class with the rest of the portfolio, the wider the optimal corridor. When asset classes move in sync, further divergence from target weights is less likely.
rationales.C: The higher the transaction costs, the wider the optimal corridor. High transaction costs set a high hurdle for rebalancing benefits to overcome.

questions.stem:
The model on which Winfield's current asset allocation is based
is best characterized as:

rationales.A: Winfield's current asset allocation is most likely based on the Black--Litterman model. Black--Litterman starts with the excess returns produced from reverse optimization, which commonly uses the observed market-capitalization value of the assets or asset classes of the global opportunity set. It then alters the reverse-optimized expected returns that reflect an investor's own distinctive views yet still behaves well in an optimizer.
rationales.B: Reverse optimization takes as its inputs a set of asset allocation weights that are assumed to be optimal and, with covariances and the risk aversion coefficient, solves for expected returns. The starting weights are commonly the observed market-capitalization value of the assets or asset classes of the global opportunity set. The asset allocation using reverse optimization would not take into account the investor's own views.
rationales.C: Asset allocations using mean--variance optimization tend to be concentrated in a subset of the available asset classes. Winfield's portfolio will be allocated to all or most of the asset classes through the reverse-optimization process followed by adjustments reflecting the investor's views.

questions.stem:
In Remington and Montgomery's discussion with Winfield on
resampling, Montgomery's comment is most likely:

options.C: incorrect regarding diversification of asset allocations.

rationales.A: Risker asset allocations are over-diversified, not under-diversified. However, the asset allocations do inherit the estimation errors in the original inputs.
rationales.B: Risker asset allocations are over-diversified, not under-diversified. However, the asset allocations do inherit the estimation errors in the original inputs.
rationales.C: Montgomery's comment about the criticisms of resampling is incorrect regarding diversification of asset allocations. Riskier asset allocations are over-diversified, not under-diversified. The comment is correct with regard to estimation errors because the asset allocations do inherit the estimation errors in the original inputs.

questions.stem:
In describing heuristics and other modeling techniques, Montgomery
is most accurate with respect to:

rationales.A: Incorrect. Comment 1 claims the 60/40 stock/bond split OPTIMIZES the growth benefits of equity and the risk-reduction benefits of bonds. A heuristic is a rule of thumb — it captures those benefits roughly but performs no optimization for any particular investor's objectives or constraints.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Comment 2 mischaracterizes the Norway model, which is defined by largely PASSIVE investment in publicly traded securities (subject to ESG considerations) — the diametrical CONTRAST to the endowment model's active management and heavy alternatives allocations, not a variation of it.
rationales.C: Correct. Comment 3 accurately describes the 1/N heuristic: wealth is allocated equally across the N available asset classes and rebalanced regularly, with no regard to expected returns, volatilities, or correlations — all assets are treated as indistinguishable in those dimensions.

questions.stem:
Using Exhibit 2, which of the sub-portfolio allocations is *most
likely* to meet both of Sebastian's goals?

options.B: A 43% allocation to sub-portfolio BY and a 57% allocation to sub-portfolio CZ
options.C: A 37% allocation to sub-portfolio BY and a 63% allocation to sub-portfolio CZ

Goal 2 is realized.

By adjusting the allocations to 37% × $2,900,000 = $1,073,000 in BY and 63% × $2,900,000 = $1,827,000 in CZ, she will be able to achieve both of her goals based on the confidence intervals.    Goal 1: Sebastian needs to maintain her current living expenditure of $120,000 per year over 20 years with a 90% probability of success. Inflation is expected to average 2.5% annually over the time horizon.    Sub-portfolio CZ should be selected because it has a higher expected return (5.70%) at the 90% probability for the 20-year horizon. Although sub-portfolio CZ has an expected annual return of 7.10%, based on the 90% probability of success requirement, the discount factor is 5.70%.    Goal 1: k = 5.70%; g = 2.50%. Although sub-portfolio BY has an expected annual return of 5.70%, based on the 85% probability of success requirement, the discount factor is 3.60%.    Goal 2: k = 3.60%. questions.stem: Using Roy's safety-first criterion, DETERMINE which portfolio in Exhibit 1 is most appropriate for Shipman and JUSTIFY. Show any calculations.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: PORTFOLIO 2.

Calculation of Roy's ratio (E[R] - MAR)/σ where MAR = $54,000/$900,000 = 6.0%:
  Portfolio 1: (10.5 - 6.0)/20.0 = 0.225
  Portfolio 2: (9.0 - 6.0)/13.0 = 0.231 ← highest
  Portfolio 3: (7.75 - 6.0)/10.0 = 0.175

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Highest Roy's ratio minimizes shortfall probability below the required 6% withdrawal rate.
• Portfolio 2 has the best trade-off of excess return over MAR per unit of risk.

PARTIAL: 5=Portfolio 2 + Roy's ratio work; 4=Portfolio 2, incomplete work; 3=Portfolio 2 with different justification; 2=Portfolio 3 with 'safest' logic (recognizes goal but wrong metric); 0=Portfolio 1.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Remington's statement that higher transaction costs and higher correlation warrant WIDER rebalancing corridors, and Montgomery's statement that higher volatility of the REST of the portfolio warrants a WIDER corridor.

rationales.model_answer:
===
REMINGTON: higher tx costs + higher correlation → WIDER corridor.
• Higher tx costs → WIDER: CORRECT (expensive to rebalance, tolerate more drift).
• Higher correlation with rest of portfolio → WIDER: CORRECT (asset drift is less consequential when it moves with the rest of the portfolio).

===
MONTGOMERY: higher volatility of REST of portfolio → WIDER corridor. — INCORRECT.
• Higher volatility of the REST of the portfolio → NARROWER corridor: because unbalanced weights amplify the volatile portfolio's already-high risk.
• (Note: higher volatility of the ASSET ITSELF also → narrower.)

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly; 4=two correct; 3=one correct; 0=none.

questions.stem: EVALUATE each of Montgomery's three statements about heuristic asset allocation methods.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STATEMENT 1 (60/40 optimizes growth + risk reduction): INCORRECT.
• 60/40 is a HEURISTIC, not an optimization — no formal maximization occurs.
• The term 'optimizes' is inaccurate.

===
STATEMENT 2 (Norway model — active public + ESG): INCORRECT.
• The Norway model is largely PASSIVE (indexed public equities with some active tilts), not actively invested.
• The ESG component is correct.

===
STATEMENT 3 (1/N equal-weight without regard to return/vol/correlation): CORRECT.
• Defining feature of 1/N: equal weight, no input from expected metrics.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: For Sebastian's two goals in Exhibit 2 sub-portfolio detail, IDENTIFY which sub-portfolio (BY or CZ) is most appropriate for each goal + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
===
GOAL 1 (20-year, 90% success, ongoing consumption): match sub-portfolio BY if lower required return; sub-portfolio CZ if higher required return.
Accepted reasoning: Goal 1 has 20-year horizon and 90% probability requirement. Compare required return to '20-year 90% min return' for each sub-portfolio: BY 5.20% vs CZ 5.70%. The CHOICE depends on Sebastian's calculated required return; the sub-portfolio meeting the required return with adequate safety margin wins.
Full credit for: correct framework (match required return to '20-year 90%' figures) AND correct choice given the required return.

===
GOAL 2 (10-year, 85% success, $1.5M nominal): match sub-portfolio using '10-year 85% min return' figures: BY 3.60% vs CZ 3.00%.
Sebastian needs to grow enough to fund $1.5M in 10 years. Given the SUCCESS MINIMUM returns available and the required return, choose the one that meets or exceeds requirement with safety margin.
Accepted framework: matches minimum-return-for-success-probability to required return; picks sub-portfolio with lowest excess vol that still meets requirement.

PARTIAL: 5=correct framework + both goals matched; 3=framework correct, one match off; 2=framework unclear but reasonable answer; 0=random or wrong metric.

cases.title: Tina Swan (Tina Swan) Scenario
cases.vignette:
Tina Swan heads a consulting practice that advises large funds and
high-net-worth individuals about portfolio asset allocation and
portfolio performance. Swan and two junior advisers, Stephanie Gruber
and Monica Morrison, are meeting with a client, XTR Funds (XTR), to
select an appropriate mean--variance-optimized (MVO) portfolio
combination that meets various restrictions. Three possible portfolios
meet XTR's criteria; each portfolio's expected performance is higher
than XTR's target return of 5.7% (Exhibit 1). Consequently, each
prospective portfolio can be combined with a risk-free security to
generate XTR's target return.

Exhibit 1 Mean--Variance-Optimized (MVO) Portfolios

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
MVO           Expected    Return Standard  Portfolio Weight in
Portfolio     Return (%)  Deviation (%)**    Risk-Free Security
(%)***
--------------- ------------- ------------------ ----------------------
A               9.5           18.69              49.35

B               8.2           14.95              39.06

C               7.8           14.18              35.00

Risk-free
security
expected
return: 1.8%

XTR target
return: 5.7%

**This is the
proportional
investment in
the risk-free
security that
in combination
with the
associated MVO
portfolio
produces the
target expected
return of
5.7%.*
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

After meeting with XTR, Swan, Gruber, and Morrison discuss some of the
criticisms of MVO portfolios.

Swan: MVO portfolios are diversified with respect to risk factors
such as value, size, and quality.

Gruber: MVO portfolios are more sensitive to measurement errors in
the expected return than to measurement errors in correlation and risk.

Morrison: Some of the issues with MVO can be corrected by using
reverse optimization to solve for risk parameters based on inputs for
expected return and correlation.

Swan then asks Gruber about the issues associated with incorporating
illiquid assets into a portfolio allocation. Gruber makes the following
statements:

1.  Illiquid assets offer both diversification benefits and an expected
return discount relative to similar liquid assets as compensation
for illiquidity.

2.  Easily tracked indexes in asset classes similar to that of an
illiquid asset often do not represent the non-idiosyncratic risk of
the illiquid asset very accurately.

3.  Unfortunately, there are no low-cost passive investment vehicles
available to allow one to closely track the aggregate performance of
less liquid asset classes.

Morrison presents a portfolio allocation for another client, Terry
Williams, based on the information in her current Investment Policy
Statement (IPS) using an asset-only asset allocation (Exhibit 2).

Exhibit 2 Optimized Output for Asset Classes within a Portfolio

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
Asset     Expected Return  Portfolio       Beta Relative to
Class     (%)              Weight (%)      Overall Portfolio
----------- ------------------ ----------------- ------------------------
A           10.2               63.34             1.091

B           8.4                10.00             0.857

C           8.9                20.00             0.922

D           6.3                6.66              0.584

Risk-free

expected
return:
1.80%

expected
return:
9.50%

Standard

of the
portfolio
return:
21.63%
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

Finally, the team discusses a third client, Roberta Harmon. Harmon just
made a cash deposit of $679,000 with a 10-year goal of achieving
$775,000. She expressed the desire to meet this goal with 90% certainty
without any excess risk. Her IPS allows for the use of equities and
fixed-income securities. Exhibit 3 summarizes her investment goal and
the characteristics of the investments contained in her IPS.

**Exhibit 3 Summary of Harmon's Investment Goal and Investment
Characteristics**

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Investment Goal
---------------------------------- ------------------------------------
Current balance                $679,000

Investment goal                $775,000

Investment horizon             10 years
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
**Investment
Characteristics**
---------------------- ------------ ---------------- ------------------
Expected   Annual         **Annual Return
Annual       Volatility (%)** with 90% Success
Return (%)                  Rate (%)

Equity portfolio       5.75         10.00            1.70

Fixed-income portfolio 1.95         2.25             1.04
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

The following comments are made:

Gruber: It would be safest for Harmon to invest 100% in fixed income
to meet her goal.

Morrison: I disagree. To meet her goal, Harmon's portfolio will have
to contain some equity investments, but no further optimization will be
required.

Swan: I agree that the portfolio will require equity exposure, but
optimization will also be required.

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 1, the *most *appropriate* *portfolio that meets
the requirements of XTR Funds is:

rationales.A: Portfolio A may be selected because it has the lowest weight invested in a risky portfolio.
rationales.C: Portfolio C may be selected because it is the least risky of the three MVO portfolios.

questions.stem:
In the discussion of the criticisms of MVO portfolios,
the most accurate statement is made by:

rationales.A: MVO portfolios are based on market risk only
rationales.B: Gruber is correct. MVO portfolios are more sensitive to measurement errors in the expected return than to measurement errors in correlation and risk.
rationales.C: Reverse optimization uses inputs for risk and correlation (or covariance) to solve for expected retur

questions.stem:
Which of Gruber's statements regarding illiquid assets
is most accurate?

rationales.A: Statement 1 is incorrect because an illiquid asset usually has a premium associated with its return due to the illiquidity.
rationales.B: Statement 2 is incorrect because easily tracked indexes for an asset class usually do not capture the idiosyncratic risk component of less liquid assets.
rationales.C: Statement 3 is the most accurate. Low-cost passive vehicles for tracking performance exist for publicly traded liquid assets but do not exist for illiquid assets.

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 2, the marginal contribution to total risk (MCTR)
of the portfolio from asset class A is closest to:

rationales.A: because 13.7% is the portfolio weight multiplied by the portfolio standard deviation: 0.6334 × 0.2163 = 0.1370.
rationales.B: because 14.9% is the weighted MCTR, which is the portfolio weight multiplied by the MCTR: 0.6334 × 0.2360 = 0.14948.
rationales.C: The marginal contribution to total risk (MCTR) is the beta relative to the portfolio multiplied by the standard deviation of the portfolio: 1.091 × 0.2163 = 0.2360 = 23.6%.

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 2, the ratio of the excess return to the marginal
contribution to total risk (MCTR) of the portfolio is closest to:

rationales.A: Because the portfolio is optimal, the ratio of the excess return to the marginal contribution to total risk (MCTR) is equal to the Sharpe ratio for the portfolio:  Share ratio =  where,  > k~p~ = portfolio expected return > > R~F~ = risk-free rate > > σ~p~ = standard deviation of portfolio return
rationales.B: because 0.439 is the Sharpe ratio calculated with the portfolio expected return (k~p~) rather than the return premium (k~p~^ ^-- R~F~): 0.095/0.2163 = 0.439.
rationales.C: because 0.077 is the Treynor measure of the portfolio (the portfolio beta is equal to 1.0): (0.095 -- 0.018)/1.0 = 0.077.

questions.stem:
In the discussion of Harmon's portfolio asset allocation (Exhibit
3), the most accurate comment is made by:

rationales.A: Swan's comment is most accurate. The annual return required to achieve Harmon's goal is 1.33%:  The fixed-income portfolio can provide only a 1.04% return with 90% certainty, so some equity exposure is required to increase the portfolio return. However, various combinations of the fixed-income and equity portfolios will need to be determined to find the risk/return results for alternative weightings. The correlation between the two portfolios will have a strong influence on the degree of risk reduction that can be achieved. The optimized portfolio will be the one that achieves the 1.33% return with 90% certainty and has the lowest level of risk.
rationales.B: The fixed-income portfolio alone will not generate the required annual return of 1.33% with 90% certainty.
rationales.C: Further optimization will be needed depending on the correlation between the equity portfolio and the fixed-income portfolio.

questions.stem: DETERMINE which MVO portfolio (A, B, or C) is most appropriate for XTR to combine with the risk-free security to achieve the 5.7% target + JUSTIFY. Show calculations.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: PORTFOLIO B.

Calculation of Sharpe ratio (E[R] - Rf)/σ, Rf = 1.8%:
  Portfolio A: (9.5 - 1.8)/18.69 = 0.412
  Portfolio B: (8.2 - 1.8)/14.95 = 0.428 ← highest
  Portfolio C: (7.8 - 1.8)/14.18 = 0.423

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• On a two-fund efficient frontier with a risk-free asset, the highest-Sharpe risky portfolio dominates as the tangency portfolio.
• Combining tangency portfolio (highest Sharpe = Portfolio B) with risk-free asset gives the lowest volatility for the 5.7% target return.
• Any portfolio on the efficient frontier can achieve 5.7%, but the tangency portfolio does so with minimum vol.

PARTIAL: 5=Portfolio B + Sharpe ratio calculations; 4=Portfolio B, calculations incomplete; 3=Portfolio B without formal Sharpe; 0=A or C.

questions.stem: EVALUATE the three statements by Swan, Gruber, and Morrison about MVO criticisms.

rationales.model_answer:
===
SWAN: 'MVO portfolios are diversified with respect to risk factors.' — INCORRECT.
• MVO diversifies across ASSETS, not risk factors. Factor exposures may be concentrated in an MVO portfolio.

===
GRUBER: 'MVO more sensitive to E[R] errors than to correlation/risk errors.' — CORRECT.
• Well-established: MVO input sensitivity is dominated by expected return errors; correlation and volatility errors are secondary.

===
MORRISON: 'Reverse optimization solves for risk parameters based on E[R] and correlation inputs.' — INCORRECT.
• Reverse optimization solves for IMPLIED EXPECTED RETURNS given weights and covariance — the reverse direction to Morrison's claim.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly + reasoning; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Gruber's three statements on illiquid assets.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STATEMENT 1: 'Illiquid assets offer diversification benefits AND an expected return DISCOUNT relative to similar liquid assets.' — INCORRECT.
• Illiquid assets offer an expected return PREMIUM (illiquidity premium), NOT a discount — compensation for accepting illiquidity.

===
STATEMENT 2: 'Easily-tracked indexes in similar asset classes do not represent the non-idiosyncratic risk of the illiquid asset.' — INCORRECT.
• Indexes typically capture systematic (non-idiosyncratic) risk reasonably well; what they miss is idiosyncratic/manager-specific risk of the illiquid asset.

===
STATEMENT 3: 'No low-cost passive investment vehicles for less liquid asset classes.' — CORRECT.
• Private equity, private real estate, hedge funds generally lack low-cost passive proxies; investors bear active fees.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed + reasoning; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: For Harmon (goal: grow $679,000 to $775,000 in 10 years with 90% probability), DETERMINE which of Gruber, Morrison, or Swan is correct + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: SWAN is correct.

Required return calculation:
  (775/679)^(1/10) - 1 ≈ 1.32% (nominal annualized)
  Wait — actual: (775,000/679,000)^(1/10) - 1 = (1.1414)^0.1 - 1 ≈ 1.33%
  So required annualized return ≈ 1.33% (unadjusted for probability).

Assessment given '90% success' return figures:
  Fixed-income only 90%-success return: 1.04% < 1.33% required — INSUFFICIENT.
  Equity only 90%-success return: 1.70% ≥ 1.33% — sufficient but excessive vol.
  Mixed portfolio needed → optimization required.

Accepted reasoning (any ONE for SWAN correct):
• Fixed income alone (Gruber) does not meet the 90%-success return requirement.
• Some equity exposure is needed; but the exact mix requires optimization to minimize excess risk, so Morrison's 'no further optimization' claim is wrong.
• Swan correctly recognizes both need for equity and need for optimization.

PARTIAL: 5=Swan + correct reasoning showing FI insufficient; 3=Swan without quantitative reasoning; 2=Morrison (partially right on equity need); 0=Gruber.

cases.title: Olivinia (Olivinia)Case
cases.vignette:
Olivinia is an oil-rich state in the country of Puerto Rinaldo, which
uses the US dollar as its official currency of exchange. In 1981, the
state's legislature created the Olivinia Heritage Fund (OHF) to collect
a portion of the state's non-renewable resource revenue and invest it on
behalf of future generations. James Lafferty, the managing director of
the fund, is one of the keynote speakers at the Global Wealth Creation
Conference. He begins his presentation with a brief overview of OHF's
history (Exhibit 1).

Exhibit 1 An Overview of the Olivinia Heritage Fund

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Phase 1 (1981--1991)                                              |
|                                                                       |
| -   The fund was given an initial allocation of $1 billion by the    |
|     state. The fund was to receive 10% of all state revenues arising  |
|     from taxes on oil and gas production and extraction. The fund was |
|     given a 20-year accumulation period over which no distributions   |
|     were allowed and the fund was forecasted to grow to $10 billion. |
|     Income earned following the accumulation period was to be used to |
|     provide for public works and other public infrastructure within   |
|     the state. Investments were restricted to cash and                |
|     investment-grade bonds.                                           |
+=======================================================================+
| Phase 2 (1991--2001)                                              |
|                                                                       |
| -   By 1991, after being in existence for 10 years, the fund value    |
|     had grown to $2.2 billion. At this time, transfers of state      |
|     revenues from taxes on oil-related resources was halted and the   |
|     government began to use income generated by the fund for direct   |
|     economic development and social investment purposes. In addition  |
|     to cash and investment-grade bonds, the investment mandate for    |
|     the fund was expanded to include investments in private and       |
|     public companies, real estate, and infrastructure investments.    |
|     Management of cash and bond investments was performed in-house.   |
|     For the higher-risk component of the portfolio, the fund hired    |
|     external managers in an effort to increase return and             |
|     correspondingly lower the incidence of negative performance.      |
|     These managers were hired or retained if they had outperformed    |
|     other active managers in their sectors in at least the prior two  |
|     years. The fund value at the end of this period was $6 billion.  |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Phase 3 (2001--2014)                                              |
|                                                                       |
| -   Strong reform legislation related to the original intent of the   |
|     fund was introduced in 2001. It reinstated transfers of           |
|     oil-related taxes to the fund, increasing them to 35% of oil- and |
|     gas-related state revenues. In addition, the fund was mandated to |
|     have 50% in public equities through passive index funds and 10%   |
|     in cash and investment-grade bonds. The remainder was to be       |
|     divided equally between high-yield bonds, real estate, private    |
|     equity, and hedge funds and would continue to be managed          |
|     externally. All investments were to be made outside the country   |
|     to avoid overheating the national economy. Investments managed by |
|     individual external managers was limited to approximately $75    |
|     million. A two-thirds majority in both the upper and lower        |
|     legislative bodies was required to change any future legislation  |
|     related to the fund. By the end of this phase, the fund was worth |
|     $28 billion.                                                     |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Phase 4 (2014--Present)                                           |
|                                                                       |
| -   The fund's management felt that the significant decline in oil    |
|     prices since mid-2014 and lowered production levels were likely   |
|     to persist through several business cycles, requiring a change in |
|     strategy to maintain the long-term objectives of the funds. They  |
|     sought government approval for lower withdrawals from the fund,   |
|     higher equity exposure, and the flexibility to vary asset class   |
|     policy weights by as much ±5% for each asset class from the       |
|     static weights that had previously existed. The government        |
|     reaffirmed its commitment to the fund given in Phase 3, and       |
|     legislative approval was received for these changes, including    |
|     the ability to increase public equity exposure to 65% and reduce  |
|     investment-grade bond exposure to as little as 7.5%. Of the       |
|     remaining authorized assets, no one asset class could have a      |
|     weight in excess of 10%.                                          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Lafferty states that ever since the fund was given the authority to vary
asset class policy weights from their strategic levels, it has actively
engaged in tactical asset allocation (TAA) using a variety of
proprietary short-term forecasting tools that have been developed
in-house. He provides the data in Exhibits 2 and 3 to illustrate the
results of one such shift in the fund's asset allocation following a
signal from its TAA model.

Exhibit 2 Example of a Short-Term Shift in Asset Allocation

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Asset Class    Current       Policy     TAA       Period
Weights     Weights    Weights   Returns
------------------ --------------- ------------ ----------- ------------
Investment-grade   12%             15%          10%         1.75%
bonds

High-yield bonds   8               6.25         10          3.0

Public equity      63              60           65          7.0

Private equity     5               6.25         6.25        4.5

Real estate        6.25            6.25         2.5         --4.0

Infrastructure     6.25            6.25         6.25        2.5

* Current weight
refers to the
weighting in
effect just prior
to when the TAA
signal occurred.
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Error! Filename not specified.

Lafferty concludes the morning portion of his presentation at the
conference by comparing the relative performance of the three portfolios
(from Exhibit 2) utilizing a graph (Exhibit 3) of the efficient frontier
derived from the asset classes used by the fund.

questions.stem:
During Phase 1, the *most* significant constraint on OHF's asset
allocation choices was the result of:

rationales.A: The fund started at $1 billion and was worth $2.2 billion at the end of Phase 1; such a size is considered in the low range for a large institutional portfolio.
rationales.B: During Phase 1, OHF was restricted to investing in only cash and high-grade debt instruments.
rationales.C: During Phase 1, OHF had no liquidity constraints. No outflows were expected for 20 years, all income was to be reinvested, and the nature of the portfolio was quite liquid by regulation, consisting of cash and high-grade bonds.

questions.stem:
During Phase 2,** **OHF's external manager selection and retention
policy was most likely susceptible to which of the following biases?

rationales.A: The endowment bias is the bias that individuals ascribe more value to things merely because they already own them.
rationales.B: Loss aversion is a bias in which individuals tend to strongly prefer avoiding losses as opposed to achieving gains. The basis for hiring and retaining managers was not based on gains or losses but, rather, was performance relative to that of their peers.
rationales.C: Representative (or recency) bias is the tendency to overweight the importance of the most recent observations and information compared with information arising from a longer-term set of observations. For this situation, it implies that those managers who outperformed their peers in at least the prior two years would continue to do so in future periods.

questions.stem:
In Phase 3,** **the *most likely*** **change in the constraints
facing OHF's ability to undertake asset allocation arose from an
increased need for:

rationales.A: The liquidity requirements of the fund had not changed; tax revenues were again flowing into the fund, and the fund held substantial (10%) bond and cash investments.
rationales.B: Phase 3 indicates no change in risk from Phase 2. In Phase 2, the fund was allowed to increase its risk exposure; it could invest in public equites, high-yield bonds, private equity, hedge funds, and real estate. In Phase 1, investments were very low risk---cash and high-grade bonds.
rationales.C: In Phase 3, 40% of assets were to be invested in high-yield bonds, real estate, private equity, and hedge funds, which were to be managed externally. Each external manager was limited to approximately $75 million of the fund's assets. As indicated in the table below, the number of external managers required grew from about 30 to almost 150 by the end of the period. This would have placed substantial demands on the governance resources of the fund to allow for identification of suitable managers and to monitor their performance.    ------------------------------------------------------------------------ Start of Phase   End of Phase 3 3 ---------------------------------- ------------------ ------------------ Portfolio value                    $6b               $28b  Externally managed funds @ 40%     $2.4b             $11.2b  Managers needed with $75m limit   32                 149 ------------------------------------------------------------------------

questions.stem:
The changes that were allowed in OHF's strategic asset allocation in
Phase 4 are best classified as relating to changes in:

rationales.A: Although business conditions have changed because of lower oil prices and reduced production, there is no indication that the main goal of the fund has changed; indeed, the government reaffirmed its commitment to the fund given in the latter part of Phase 2.
rationales.B: In Phase 4, lower oil prices and reduced production would have resulted in substantial declines in cash inflows that management perceived to be long term in nature. To compensate for these changes, management sought both lower withdrawals and increased equity exposure to bolster its declining cash inflows.
rationales.C: A change in constraints would arise from a change in time horizon, liquidity needs, asset size, or regulatory constraints, none of which are indicated in Phase 4.

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 2, compared with the strategic asset allocation,
the incremental return added to the fund through tactical asset
allocation was closest to:

rationales.C: By underweighting investment-grade bonds and real estate and overweighting public equity and high-yield bonds, the TAA strategy added 0.53% to the return of the fund, as shown below.    --------------------------------------------------------------------------- (1)       (2)       (3)      (4) = [(2) -- (1)] × (3) ------------------ ------------- ------------- ------------ --------------- Policy      TAA         Period     Incremental Weights     Weights     Returns      Returns (%) (%)  Investment-grade   0.1500        0.1000        1.75         --0.0875 bonds  High-yield bonds   0.0625        0.1000        3.00         0.1125  Public equity      0.6000        0.6500        7.00         0.3500  Real estate        0.0625        0.0250        --4.00       0.1500  Total from TAA 0.8750        0.8750                     0.5250    The following had no impact on the incremental return  Private equity     0.0625        0.0625        4.50         0.0000  Infrastructure     0.0625        0.0625        2.50         0.0000  0.1250        0.1250                     0.0 ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

questions.stem:
The *most* appropriate conclusion that can be drawn from Exhibit 3
is that:

options.A: the current portfolio is a corner portfolio.
options.B: the Sharpe ratios for the policy portfolio and the TAA portfolio are the same.
options.C: management's risk--return objectives may not have been achieved with the TAA portfolio.

rationales.A: Corner portfolios are efficient portfolios and represent a portfolio where an asset weight changes from zero to positive or positive to zero. No such behavior in weights is indicated for the current portfolio allocation in Exhibit 2. It is also an inefficient portfolio.
rationales.B: The Sharpe ratio is the slope of the line drawn from the risk-free rate to a particular portfolio. The two portfolios of interest are the policy portfolio and the TAA portfolio because both are indicated as being efficient. The diagram to the right indicates that the policy portfolio/risk-free combination has a higher slope than the TAA/risk-free combination.
rationales.C: The Sharpe ratio is the slope of the line drawn from the risk-free rate to a particular portfolio. The two portfolios of interest are the policy portfolio and the TAA portfolio because both are indicated as being efficient. The diagram to the right indicates that the policy portfolio/risk-free combination has a higher slope than the TAA/risk-free combination. Even though the TAA portfolio has a higher return than the policy portfolio, the additional return requires too much additional risk. In addition, the TAA portfolio may exceed management's risk tolerance.

questions.stem: Using Exhibit 2 policy weights, TAA weights, and period returns, DETERMINE whether the TAA shift added value + JUSTIFY. Show calculations.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: YES — TAA shift added approximately 53 basis points (~0.53%) of value.

Calculations:
Policy return = 0.15(1.75) + 0.0625(3.0) + 0.60(7.0) + 0.0625(4.5) + 0.0625(-4.0) + 0.0625(2.5) = 4.84%

TAA return = 0.10(1.75) + 0.10(3.0) + 0.65(7.0) + 0.0625(4.5) + 0.025(-4.0) + 0.0625(2.5) = 5.36%

Value added = 5.36% - 4.84% ≈ 0.52% (accept 50-55 bps range).

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Overweighting public equity (65% vs 60%) captured the strong 7% period return.
• Underweighting real estate (2.5% vs 6.25%) avoided the -4% loss.
• Overweighting high-yield bonds (10% vs 6.25%) captured 3% return.
• All shifts moved toward winning asset classes.

PARTIAL: 5=YES + calculated value-add + reasoning; 4=YES + directional reasoning; 3=YES with weak reasoning; 0=NO/wrong.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two governance strengths of the OHF structure across its four phases.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following earn full credit.

Accepted strengths:
• Two-thirds legislative supermajority required for changes — protects long-term policy stability from short-term political pressure.
• $75M cap per external manager (Phase 3) — limits concentration and manager risk.
• Foreign-only investment mandate — avoids overheating the domestic economy and provides diversification against national economic conditions.
• Phased evolution with legislative approval — deliberate, transparent policy changes.
• Reinstatement of oil-revenue transfers (Phase 3) — restored the original purpose.
• Independent management structure with defined governance layers.
• Explicit multi-generational mandate for future generations aligns incentives.

PARTIAL: 5=two distinct valid strengths + brief reasoning; 3=one strength; 0=vague.

questions.stem: EVALUATE the Phase 2 practice of hiring or retaining external managers based on their two-year track record of outperformance.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: WEAK PRACTICE — subject to well-known selection biases.

Accepted critiques (any ONE for pass, TWO for full credit):
• Recent-performance bias / hot-hand fallacy — 2 years is insufficient to distinguish skill from luck.
• Return-chasing systematically buys managers near cyclical peaks; likely to experience subsequent mean reversion (underperformance).
