• Does not adjust for risk — a high-return, high-risk manager may look better than a lower-risk, better-risk-adjusted manager.
• Ignores style drift, capacity, fees, and consistency.
• Ignores fundamental process assessment — outperformance criterion is purely retrospective.
• 'Correspondingly lower the incidence of negative performance' is dubious — past winners do not systematically avoid future losses.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies practice as weak + two accepted critiques; 3=weak + one critique; 1=recognizes issue without articulation; 0=defends the practice.

questions.stem: DISCUSS how the three Phase 4 changes (lower withdrawals, higher permitted equity exposure up to 65%, ±5% weight flexibility) address the persistent oil-price decline described in the vignette.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — one accepted rationale per change earns full credit.

===
LOWER WITHDRAWALS:
• Reduces drawdown pressure during periods of low oil-related contributions.
• Preserves capital for future generations given reduced revenue inflow.

===
HIGHER EQUITY EXPOSURE (up to 65%):
• Raises long-term expected return to compensate for lower oil-revenue inflow.
• Long-horizon capital can absorb equity volatility to achieve higher expected returns.

===
±5% WEIGHT FLEXIBILITY:
• Enables tactical asset allocation to exploit short-term dislocations.
• Provides operational flexibility to manage through market volatility while staying within strategic bounds.

PARTIAL: 5=one accepted rationale per change; 4=covers 2 of 3; 3=covers 1 well; 0=general/off-topic.

cases.title: Overview of FI - Danny Moynahan (Danny)
cases.vignette:
Danny Moynahan, CFA, is a fixed-income portfolio manager at Reagan
Investment Advisory (Reagan). His wife, Abigail Boyle, is a professor at
a local university not far from their home. She is currently teaching an
investments class. Over dinner one evening, she asks her husband if he
will come and talk to her class about managing fixed-income portfolios.
She believes it will be a useful experience for her students to hear
from someone working in the investment industry. He agrees, and they
plan for him to make his presentation the following week.

The next day at his office, with permission from his superior, Tom
Gayle, Moynahan works on his presentation to the class. He plans to put
together six pages for his discussion. He reviews the presentation
materials he previously used at a conference to see if any of it would
be useful. He decides page 1 should discuss the benefits of including
fixed-income securities in a portfolio and highlights the following
three points:

Point A: Adding fixed-income securities to a portfolio is an
effective way of obtaining the benefits of diversification, especially
because fixed-income correlations with other asset classes are low.

Point B: The regular nature of fixed-income cash flows enables
investors to fund future obligations, unless there is a credit event.

Point C: Fixed-income securities can always provide a hedge for
inflation, which results in superior risk-adjusted real portfolio
returns.

On page 2, Moynahan decides to outline the three total return approaches
he utilizes to manage Reagan's fixed-income portfolios. He puts together
the following exhibit:

Exhibit 1 Features of Total Return Portfolios

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Benchmark   Portfolio   Portfolio   **Portfolio
1           2           3**
----------------------- --------------- ------------- ------------- -------------
Quality:

AAA/AA/A (% of          76.0            74.9          75.8          76.3
portfolio)

BBB/BB (% of portfolio) 24.0            25.1          24.2          23.7

Average                 AA--            AA--          AA--          AA

Key Rate Duration:

1--5 years              2.5             2.4           2.4           2.5

5--10 years             1.8             1.9           1.9           1.8

10--15 years            1.5             1.4           1.5           1.5

Credit Spread Duration  1.45            1.55          1.43          1.50

Turnover (%)                            8%            5%            6%

Country Exposure:

Developed Markets       90.0            86.4          91.2          87.0

Emerging Markets        10.0            14.0          9.8           13.0

Securities Lending      N/A             Not Allowed   Allowed       Not Allowed
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Moynahan titles page 3, "Liquidity in the Fixed-Income Market." He wants
to ensure that the class appreciates the differences in liquidity
between fixed-income and equity securities. He stresses that liquidity
across fixed-income securities varies greatly and that compared to
equities, fixed-income markets are generally less liquid. Also,
liquidity influences fixed-income pricing, but illiquidity enhances the
portfolio's yield to maturity. Lastly, dealers will narrow bid--ask
spreads on thinly traded securities as a consequence of their
illiquidity.

Tom Gayle, Moynahan's superior, stops by Moynahan's office. Moynahan
shares his presentation with Gayle, who suggests that page 4 include a
discussion about expected returns. They decide to outline an example of
a recent bond trade where they bought a $100 par value bond at a
premium. Moynahan presents a decomposition of the bond's expected
returns detailing various components and focuses on roll down return. He
adds the following footnote: "The roll down return demonstrates how the
price of a bond typically moves closer to par regardless of yield curve
changes over the strategy horizon."

Moynahan and Gayle continue their discussion about the presentation and
debate several potential subjects to include on page 5. Gayle suggests
assessing the use of leverage in the portfolios. They decide to present
a scenario where the portfolio is fully invested, but given their
outlook for a decline in interest rates, they want to increase the
portfolio's investment exposure. The portfolio and the benchmark both
currently have the same duration.

On page 6, the final page of his presentation, Moynahan plans to discuss
the tax implications of fixed-income investing. He wants the class to
understand that the management of taxable portfolios is more complicated
than that of tax-exempt portfolios. He outlines the following key
considerations for managing taxable fixed-income portfolios:

A.  Minimize interest income relative to capital gains.

B.  Minimize capital gains relative to capital losses.

C.  Forego attractive trading opportunity because of tax implications.

questions.stem:
Which of the points outlined on page 1 of Moynahan's presentation
is least likely correct?

rationales.A: because the statement regarding diversification benefits of fixed-income securities is accurate.
rationales.B: because the statement regarding fixed-income cash flows is accurate.
rationales.C: Point C of Moynahan's presentation is incorrect. Some fixed-income securities, such as inflation-linked bonds, provide a hedge for inflation that results in superior risk-adjusted real portfolio returns. However, not all fixed-income securities act as a hedge against inflation.

questions.stem:
How should Moynahan most likely label the management approaches for
each of the portfolios described in Exhibit 1 on page 2 of his
presentation?

options.A: Portfolio 1 = Active Management, Portfolio 2 = Pure Indexing, Portfolio 3 = Enhanced Indexing
options.B: Portfolio 1 = Active Management, Portfolio 2 = Enhanced Indexing, Portfolio 3 = Pure Indexing
options.C: Portfolio 1 = Enhanced Indexing, Portfolio 2 = Pure Indexing, Portfolio 3 = Active Management

rationales.A: Moynahan should label the portfolios on page 2 as follows: Portfolio 1 = Active Management, which allows for larger risk factor mismatch to the benchmark. Portfolio 2 = Pure Indexing, which involves attempting to replicate a bond index as closely as possible. Portfolio 3 = Enhanced Indexing, which is closely linked to the benchmark but attempts to generate a modest amount of outperformance versus the benchmark.  ------------------------------------------------------------------------- Portfolio 1  Portfolio 2 Portfolio 3 ----------------------- ---------------- --------------- ---------------- Quality                 Enhanced         Pure Indexing   Enhanced Indexing or                      Indexing or Active                           Active Management                       Management  Duration                Active           Pure Indexing   Enhanced Management       or Enhanced     Indexing or Indexing        Active Management  Credit Spread           Active           Pure Indexing   Enhanced Management                       Indexing  Turnover                Active           Pure Indexing   Enhanced Management                       Indexing  Country Exposure        Active           Pure Indexing   Enhanced Management                       Indexing  Securities Lending      No Impact        No Impact       No Impact  Determination           Active           Pure Indexing   Enhanced Management -------------------------------------------------------------------------
rationales.B: because the ordering of portfolios given is incorrect. The correct ordering is: Portfolio 1 = Active Management, Portfolio 2 = Pure Indexing, Portfolio 3 = Enhanced Indexing.
rationales.C: because the ordering of portfolios given is incorrect. The correct ordering is: Portfolio 1 = Active Management, Portfolio 2 = Pure Indexing, Portfolio 3 = Enhanced Indexing.

questions.stem:
Are Moynahan's comments regarding fixed-income liquidity *most
likely* correct?

options.B: No, with respect to the bid--ask spread.
options.C: No, with respect to fixed-income pricing and yield to maturity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Moynahan's liquidity discussion contains an error, so his comments cannot be affirmed as correct in full.
rationales.B: Correct. Moynahan claims dealers will NARROW bid–ask spreads on thinly traded securities as a consequence of their illiquidity. The relationship runs the other way: dealers WIDEN bid–ask spreads on thinly traded bonds to compensate for the inventory and search risk of holding illiquid positions.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The pricing and yield-to-maturity comment is accurate: liquidity does influence fixed-income pricing, and less liquid bonds compensate investors with higher yields to maturity (an illiquidity premium), enhancing portfolio YTM.

questions.stem: Is the footnote Moynahan includes on page 4 most likely correct?

options.B: No, with respect to bond prices.
options.C: No, with respect to roll down return.

rationales.A: Moynahan's footnote regarding the yield curve is not accurate.
rationales.B: Moynahan's footnote with respect to bond prices is accurate.
rationales.C: The footnote Moynahan includes on page 4 is incorrect with respect to roll down return. The roll down return is equal to the bond's percentage price change assuming an unchanged yield curve over the strategy horizon, not regardless of yield curve changes as stated in the footnote. The roll down return results from the bond "rolling down" the yield curve as the time to maturity decreases. As time passes, a bond's price typically moves closer to par.

questions.stem:
What trades can Moynahan most likely make to accomplish the objective
outlined on page 5 of his presentation?

options.B: Enter into a fixed-rate payer swap contract

rationales.A: To accomplish Moynahan's objective of increasing the investment exposure of a fully invested portfolio, he would buy long bond futures. Futures contracts embed significant leverage because they permit the counterparties to gain exposure to a large quantity of the underlying asset without having to actually transact in the asset.
rationales.B: because entering into a fixed-rate payer swap contract would not increase the portfolio's investment exposure.
rationales.C: because selling an overnight repurchase agreement would not increase the portfolio's investment exposure.

questions.stem:
Which of the considerations outlined by Moynahan on page 6 of the
presentation is least likely correct?

rationales.A: When managing taxable fixed-income portfolios, Moynahan would want to minimize interest income relative to capital gains because capital gains are typically taxed at a lower effective tax rate.
rationales.B: When managing taxable fixed-income portfolios, Moynahan should not minimize capital gains relative to capital losses because capital losses are generally only used to offset capital gains.
rationales.C: When managing taxable fixed-income portfolios, Moynahan may want to dismiss attractive relative value trades due to tax implications.

questions.stem: EVALUATE each of Moynahan's three points (A, B, C) about the benefits of adding fixed-income to a portfolio.

rationales.model_answer:
===
POINT A (FI diversification benefits from low correlation): CORRECT.
• Fixed income generally has low correlation with equities, providing diversification.
• Especially valuable in balanced portfolios.

===
POINT B (regular cash flows fund future obligations, unless credit event): CORRECT / MOSTLY CORRECT.
• Predictable coupon and principal cash flows are useful for liability-matching.
• Credit events (default) do disrupt this — statement acknowledges the caveat.

===
POINT C (FI can ALWAYS provide inflation hedge): INCORRECT.
• Nominal fixed-income does NOT hedge inflation — it is HURT by unexpected inflation.
• Only INFLATION-LINKED bonds (TIPS, linkers) provide an inflation hedge.
• The word 'always' makes the statement unambiguously wrong.

PARTIAL: 5=all three correct + reasoning; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: Based on Exhibit 1, DETERMINE which of Portfolio 1, 2, or 3 most closely tracks the benchmark + JUSTIFY briefly.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: PORTFOLIO 3.

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Portfolio 3 has quality distribution (76.3% AAA/AA/A, 23.7% BBB/BB) closest to benchmark (76%/24%).
• Key rate durations match benchmark exactly (2.5 / 1.8 / 1.5).
• Credit spread duration 1.50 close to benchmark 1.45.
• Country exposure 87.0% DM / 13.0% EM slightly off but similar to benchmark 90/10 compared to alternatives.

COMPARISON:
• P1 has bigger EM overweight (14% vs 10%) and mismatched key rate durations.
• P2 has better country match but security lending affects total return.
• P3 provides best overall benchmark-tracking on key metrics.

PARTIAL: 5=P3 + reasoning; 3=P3 without specifics; 0=P1 or P2.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Moynahan's four statements on page 3 about liquidity in fixed-income markets.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STATEMENT 1 (liquidity varies greatly across FI): CORRECT.
• FI liquidity is highly heterogeneous — Treasuries very liquid, credit less so.

===
STATEMENT 2 (FI generally less liquid than equity): CORRECT.
• Bond markets are largely OTC dealer-driven; equity exchanges provide continuous auction liquidity.

===
STATEMENT 3 (illiquidity enhances YTM): CORRECT.
• Illiquid bonds command an illiquidity premium — higher yields for equivalent credit.

===
STATEMENT 4 (dealers NARROW bid-ask on thinly traded securities): INCORRECT.
• Dealers WIDEN bid-ask on thinly traded securities — compensation for taking inventory risk on illiquid names.
• Narrow spreads apply to actively traded liquid securities.

PARTIAL: 5=all four assessed correctly; 3=three correct; 0=fewer than two.

questions.stem: Moynahan wants to explain why liability-based approaches differ from total-return management. DISCUSS two key differences.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted differences:
• Liability-based benchmark is the LIABILITY (interest-rate-sensitive PV), whereas total return uses a market bond index.
• LDI focuses on immunizing/matching duration and convexity to liabilities; total return focuses on outperforming the index.
• LDI success measured by funded status stability; total return by alpha vs index.
• LDI accepts return concentration for hedging accuracy; total return diversifies.
• Risk metric: LDI uses hedge ratio and duration gap; total return uses tracking error.
• LDI often uses derivatives to precisely match liability sensitivities.

PARTIAL: 5=two distinct differences + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Elbe Society (The Elbe Society)
cases.vignette:
The Elbe Society is an organization dedicated to documenting and
preserving the pre-20th century artistic culture of eastern Germany. The
work of the Elbe Society is supported entirely by the Elbe Society
Endowment, which has a portfolio worth approximately €360 million. The
portfolio's current asset allocation approximately matches its model
portfolio of 45% in publicly traded equities, 45% in investment-grade
fixed-income securities, and 10% in publicly traded real estate
investment trusts (REITs). The model portfolio was chosen using
mean--variance optimization to meet a target real return (in excess of
inflation) of 4% with minimum return volatility. Spending for each year
is set at 4% of the average value of the portfolio over the previous
three years. The portfolio must be rebalanced each year to be close to
the model portfolio allocation.

Elias Neumann, chair of the board of trustees of the Elbe Society, which
also oversees the endowment, is meeting with Luisa Zimmermann, chief
investment officer of the endowment. Neumann begins by informing
Zimmermann that the board would like the spending rate to be increased
to 5% of the average portfolio value within two years. He recommends
substituting a portion of the investment-grade fixed-income securities
with a 25% allocation to private equity, noting that the resulting
portfolio will have similar return volatility, improved diversification,
and a higher expected return than the current portfolio. The trustees
would like the other allocation and spending rules to remain the same.

Zimmermann responds that there are other risk considerations that must
be examined when replacing fixed income with private equity. She makes
three statements:

Statement 1: The reported return volatility of investment-grade
fixed income is likely more accurate than that of private equity.

Statement 2: The risk of not meeting long-term investment goals is
higher for investment-grade fixed income than for private equity.

Statement 3: The reported correlation of investment-grade fixed
income with public equity is likely less accurate than the reported
correlation of private equity with public equity.

Zimmermann tells Neumann, "As we move toward an increased allocation to
alternative investments--particularly private assets--we may want to
move away from our current traditional asset allocation approach to a
risk-factor-based approach. Traditional approaches have the advantage of
being easier to communicate relative to risk-factor-based approaches.
However, traditional approaches often commingle assets with very
different risk characteristics in the same asset classes, resulting in
portfolios with the same asset allocation but very different risks.
Unlike the traditional approach, systematic risk factors do equally well
at explaining the risk and return patterns of public and private
assets."

Zimmermann, discussing liquidity with Neumann, tells him, "If the fund
adds the private equity allocation you recommend, it will be infeasible
to rebalance to our model allocation on an annual basis. However,
because we will experience much higher returns from private equity than
we've been getting from the fixed-income securities being replaced, we
should have no problem increasing our current spending rate, as
requested. If we experience an economic downturn, a private equity
position improves our overall liquidity because privately held firms are
subject to fewer market pressures than publicly traded ones."

questions.stem:
Is Neumann's assessment of the portfolio impact of private equity
investment most likely correct?

options.B: No, he is incorrect with respect to portfolio return.
options.C: No, he is incorrect with respect to portfolio return volatility.

rationales.A: Replacing investment-grade fixed-income securities with private equity will increase expected return but also increase portfolio return volatility.
rationales.B: "B" is investment-grade fixed income provides steady but more moderate returns. Replacing investment-grade fixed-income securities with private equity will increase the portfolio's expected return, as desired.
rationales.C: Replacing investment-grade fixed-income assets, which have a strong potential for diversifying public equity, with private equity, which has a moderate potential to diversify public equity and higher standalone volatility than fixed income, will almost certainly increase portfolio return volatility.

questions.stem: Which of Zimmermann's statements is *least likely *correct?

rationales.A: Returns from private equity are based on infrequent reporting and typically use appraisal values, resulting in a smoothing of returns and less accurate return volatilities. In comparison, investment-grade fixed-income returns are based on transaction prices, resulting in more accurate return volatilities.
rationales.B: Private equity has a higher expected return than investment-grade fixed income. Therefore, given the desire to increase the endowment's long-term return, investment-grade fixed-income portfolios or portfolios with higher allocations to investment-grade fixed income have a greater risk of not meeting that goal than private equity portfolios or portfolios with higher allocations to private equity.
rationales.C: Private equity returns are based on infrequent reporting and typically use appraisal values, which results in lower return correlations with other asset classes (including public equity) than would be evident from actual transaction prices. In comparison, investment-grade fixed-income returns are based on transactions prices, resulting in a more accurate return series and more accurate return correlation estimates.

questions.stem:
Which aspect of Zimmermann's discussion of the traditional approach
to asset allocation versus a risk-factor-based approach is *least
likely* correct?

options.A: Commingling of assets into asset classes
options.B: Explaining the risk and returns of various assets
options.C: The relative ease of communicating the two approaches

rationales.A: Zimmermann is correct that traditional approaches often commingle assets with very different risk characteristics into the same broad asset classes---for example, when high-yield fixed-income securities and government bonds are both included in a portfolio's fixed-income allocation.
rationales.B: Zimmermann is incorrect about the ability of risk-factor approaches to explain both public and private asset behavior. Systematic risk-factor approaches typically explain most or all of the risk and return patterns of public assets but far less of those patterns for private assets. This is due to the widespread use of appraisal-based valuation for private assets and the idiosyncratic risks present in individual funds.
rationales.C: Zimmermann is correct that it is easier to explain traditional approaches to asset allocation than to explain risk-based approaches.

questions.stem:
In her discussion of liquidity issues, Zimmermann is *most
likely *correct regarding:

options.C: the impact of an economic downturn.

rationales.A: Private equity requires a long-term commitment of capital with very uncertain drawdowns of that commitment and return of invested capital. Early in the investment cycle, only a portion of the committed capital will be called by the general partner and the timing of future calls is uncertain. Later in the investment cycle, the timing of a fund's sale of investments and return of capital to investors is uncertain. The investment itself is very illiquid (hard to sell in the secondary market); thus, rebalancing to a model allocation on an annual basis will be infeasible.
rationales.B: Early in the private equity investment cycle, committed capital will be held in relatively liquid assets with lower expected returns to fund capital drawdowns that have uncertain timing. Further, little return will be realized from early-stage investments, particularly on the venture capital side of private equity. Also, bad investments tend to fail earlier than good investments succeed. Overall, private equity is subject to a J-curve, with low or even negative returns early on and high returns later on. For these reasons, the portfolio's overall return will be lower in early years, reducing the endowment's available spending according to its current spending rules and making it difficult to increase the spending rate in two years.
rationales.C: In an economic downturn, capital calls for a newer private equity fund can accelerate because depressed market prices produce good investment opportunities precisely when the endowment portfolio's public asset values are depressed. This can require either selling portfolio assets for negative returns to fund capital calls or abandoning the investment in private equity at a total loss. Further, in an economic downtown, the general partner of a more mature private equity fund will extend the fund's life and avoid selling holdings at depressed prices, thereby reducing cash flows the endowment portfolio had anticipated.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Zimmermann's three statements about substituting fixed income with private equity.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STMT 1 (reported vol of IG FI more accurate than PE): CORRECT.
• Public FI has continuous market pricing → accurate return series → accurate vol.
• PE has infrequent valuations, smoothing → REPORTED vol UNDERSTATES true risk.
• Il-liquidity smoothing effect makes PE appear less volatile than it is.

===
STMT 2 (risk of not meeting long-term goals HIGHER for IG FI than PE): CORRECT.
• IG FI typically has LOWER expected return; may not achieve 4% real target.
• PE has HIGHER expected return, better positioned to meet long-term goals.
• Higher chance of shortfall with FI-heavy allocation.

===
STMT 3 (correlation of IG FI with public equity LESS accurate than PE's correlation with public equity): INCORRECT.
• REVERSED: PE correlation with public equity is UNDERSTATED due to stale valuations.
• IG FI correlations are measurable from actual public market data → MORE accurate.
• PE correlations reported are typically 0.3-0.5 but TRUE correlations may be 0.7-0.8.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly + reasoning; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Zimmermann's statement about traditional asset allocation vs risk-factor-based approaches.

rationales.model_answer:
===
CORRECT ASPECTS:
• Traditional approaches easier to communicate (naming asset classes vs abstract risk factors). ✓
• Traditional approaches often COMMINGLE assets with different risk characteristics in the same class. ✓ (E.g., 'alternatives' bucket includes PE, HFs, RE — different risks.)
• Portfolios with 'same asset allocation but very different risks' — TRUE. ✓

===
INCORRECT ASPECT:
• 'Systematic risk factors do equally well at explaining risk and return patterns of public AND private assets' — OVERSTATED.
• Risk-factor models struggle with private assets due to: infrequent pricing, smoothed returns, illiquidity premium, manager-specific effects.
• Traditional factors (market, size, value, momentum, quality) do NOT fully capture PE returns.
• Some factors are relatively good (market) but idiosyncratic PE returns are less well-explained.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies both correct and incorrect aspects; 3=one aspect; 0=fully agrees or fully rejects.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two liquidity considerations Zimmermann should raise given the proposal to add 25% PE (replacing IG FI) and increase spending to 5%.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
• CAPITAL CALL RISK: PE commitments require unpredictable capital calls (over 3-5 years); IG FI is fully invested — PE creates liquidity uncertainty.
• DENOMINATOR EFFECT: in market downturns, public assets decline faster than PE (due to stale pricing) → PE % of portfolio INCREASES unintentionally.
• INCREASED SPENDING (5% vs 4%) requires MORE liquid distributions; 25% PE (less liquid) is problematic.
• REBALANCING CHALLENGE: cannot easily rebalance illiquid PE positions to target.
• DISTRIBUTIONS TIMING: PE cash flows lumpy and unpredictable.
• SECONDARY MARKET DISCOUNTS: if forced to sell PE for liquidity, typical 15-30% discount from NAV.
• ANNUAL REBALANCING requirement conflicts with PE illiquidity.
• ENDOWMENT MODEL success requires perpetual horizon and low withdrawal — not guaranteed with 5% spend.

PARTIAL: 5=two clear considerations + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two RISK considerations beyond volatility that should be examined when substituting IG fixed income with private equity.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
• ILLIQUIDITY RISK: PE cannot be easily sold; 25% allocation is substantial.
• J-CURVE EFFECT: early years of PE commitments have negative cash flows and returns; spending must be funded from other sources.
• VINTAGE-YEAR RISK: PE returns highly dependent on entry year; single-vintage concentration is risky.
• MANAGER SELECTION RISK: PE returns disperse widely across managers; poor selection = poor returns.
• LEVERAGE / GEARING: PE portfolios typically use significant leverage — hidden risk factor.
• VALUATION LAG: reported valuations lag public markets → understated volatility.
• FEE DRAG: 2/20 fee structure erodes returns.
• REGULATORY / TAX CHANGES affecting PE.
• CORRELATION CHANGE in CRISIS: PE correlations to public equity increase in stress.
• DUE-DILIGENCE COMPLEXITY.

PARTIAL: 5=two clear considerations + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Gambier Advisory (Lucas Thompson)
cases.vignette:
Lucas Thompson is a senior investment adviser working for Gambier
Advisory Group, located in Adelaide, Australia. Thompson works primarily
with not-for-profit institutions. He has two meetings with clients
scheduled for today.

His first meeting is with Isla Nguyen, the chief financial officer of
Mungo University, also located in Adelaide. Nguyen manages the Mungo
Building Fund, currently holding $A130 million. Tuition revenue,
donations, and government support allow the university to add annually
to the fund, which helps pay for construction or major renovations of
existing facilities. Over the past 10 years, the average value of the
fund was $A160 million and the minimum value was A$75 million. The
fund's asset allocation is approximately 60% broad fixed income and 40%
publicly traded equity. Nguyen is considering reallocating half of the
fixed-income allocation to alternative assets.

Nguyen tells Thompson, "I've reviewed the past 15 years of data. Based
on that analysis, it is my understanding that reallocating some of the
fixed income to public real estate would result in a more diversified
portfolio, given real estate's lower correlation with equities and lower
equity beta. Furthermore, public real estate investments are highly
liquid and would not require the long-term commitment of other
alternative investments, such as private equity and hedge funds."

Thompson offers some advice to Nguyen, "If you proceed with plans to add
alternative assets to the fund, I would recommend a risk-based approach
to asset selection. Relative to traditional approaches, there are a
number of benefits to a risk-based approach."

These benefits include the following:

Benefit 1: It doesn't group assets together based on a few shared
characteristics.

Benefit 2: It does equally well accounting for risk levels of
alternative assets as it does for publicly traded assets.

Benefit 3: It identifies a set of common risk factors and estimates
sensitivities to these factors for each asset being analyzed that are
stable over time.

Thompson's second meeting is with Evie Taylor, chief investment officer
for the Nullarbor Foundation, whose mission is to provide social
services to Aboriginal Australians. Its work is supported by income from
the foundation's A$260 million investment portfolio. Historically, the
portfolio has been invested in a mixture of publicly traded equity and
fixed-income securities using low-cost index funds, along with real
estate assets using REITs. Three years ago, a plan to shift some of the
portfolio's assets into private alternative assets was implemented with
the goal of replacing half of the equity investments with private equity
and half of the fixed-income investments with private credit over a
six-year period. Private equity investment is to be in the nonventure
sector, and private credit in the direct lending sector.

Taylor tells Thompson, "I recently met with the foundation's investment
committee to review our progress on shifting to alternative investments
and the materials we've received from general partners in whose funds
we've invested."

The investment committee wants:

-   each fund to identify and provide the qualifications of those who
have the responsibility and authority to make investment decisions;

-   outside experts brought in to provide quarterly updates on market
conditions and expectations, with an indication of what sectors of
the private equity and private credit markets are most likely to
benefit from those expectations; and

-   to know what assets are held by each fund at the end of each quarter
and how those assets are valued for reporting purposes.

Thompson reminds Taylor that they have not yet finalized the
foundation's evaluation process for alternative investment performance.
He tells her, "You've decided to measure the performance of private
equity investments against a broad equity market index and private
credit against an investment-grade fixed-income index, both having a
goal of an excess of benchmark return after fees of 200 basis points
(bps). These are in keeping with industry practices. We will measure
return and return volatility using reported values from each fund's
quarterly reporting and market values for the indexes. A difference
between the publicly traded and alternative asset portions of the
portfolio is the need for the foundation to develop procedures to
monitor alternative investment managers and processes."

questions.stem:
Are Nguyen's comments to Thompson regarding the results of a
potential reallocation from fixed income to real estate *most
likely* correct?

options.B: No, she is incorrect regarding the liquidity.
options.C: No, she is incorrect regarding the diversification benefits.

rationales.A: Nguyen is incorrect regarding the diversification benefits.
rationales.B: Public real estate investment is highly liquid.
rationales.C: Public real estate has had a fairly high, positive correlation with equities, as well as a high, positive equity beta. In contrast, fixed income has broadly had a negative correlation with equity and a small but negative equity beta. Switching from fixed income to real estate will likely decrease portfolio diversification and increase return volatility.

questions.stem:
When recommending a risk-based approach to asset selection, Thompson
is most likely correct regarding which of the following?

rationales.A: Traditional approaches to defining the investment opportunity set tend toward broad asset classes that can contain assets with very divergent risk levels. For example, fixed income can contain government bonds with little or no default risk and high-yield bonds that have very high levels of the default risk. A risk-based approach seeks to measure the sensitivity of each asset to a specific set of risk factors and would treat government bonds and high-yield bonds very differently because of their different sensitivities to default risk or credit spreads and to liquidity, inflation, and potentially other risk factors.
rationales.B: Risk-based approaches generally do a very good job of accounting for the overall risk of publicly traded assets, with R^2^ values of 0.8--1.0. They do less well at accounting for the overall risks of alternative assets, with R^2^ values of 0.3--0.7. This level of unexplained risk is due to the use of appraisals for valuation, heavier reliance on manager choices, and other idiosyncratic risks.
rationales.C: It is true that risk-based approaches identify a single set of common risk factors and estimate the sensitivities of each asset to these factors. Risk factors, however, are sensitive to the historical period examined for the estimation procedure; therefore, risk factor sensitivities are not stable over time

questions.stem:
The Nullarbor Foundation's investment committee's requests
are least likely realistic with regard to:

rationales.A: It is reasonable to expect the general partner to explain who has investment decision-making authority or responsibility and to make clear that person has the knowledge, time, and capacity to make good decisions.
rationales.B: Investments in private equity and private credit require investors to believe that markets are less than efficient in specific ways from which alternative investments can profit. It is reasonable and appropriate for the investment committee to want to understand how market conditions are likely to favor certain alternative investment strategies.
rationales.C: Although it is reasonable to expect a private equity or private credit general partner to explain how assets are being valued for reporting (e.g., using independent administrators), full transparency of holdings cannot be expected. Investors in these markets must, to some extent, be comfortable with buying into a blind pool of assets

questions.stem:
Thompson's comments to Taylor regarding the evaluation of
alternative investments are least likely appropriate with regard to:

options.B: valuation and risk of alternative investments.
options.C: the need to monitor managers and investment processes

rationales.A: Although no benchmark for alternative investments is perfect, the ones identified by Taylor are in keeping with industry practice and appropriate given the nature of the investments (nonventure for private equity and direct lending for private credit).
rationales.B: Infrequent pricing (in this case, quarterly) can make it difficult to measure volatility and correlation because too few observations are available. Furthermore, stale pricing and the use of appraisals for valuation lead to smoothing effects (swings in market values are muted), which in effect understates return volatility and reduces measured return correlations with publicly traded assets, making the diversification benefits of alternative assets seem better than they actually are. Efforts must be made to correct for these biases when measuring the performance of alternative investments.
rationales.C: With alternative investments, investors do need to pay attention to numerous aspects of the investment process, including key-person risk, conflicts of interest between the general partner and the investor, style drift, client and asset turnover, reliability of service providers, and so on. In general, investors do not need to monitor the manager or the investment process when investing in publicly traded assets because these tend to be more transparent and more highly regulated.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Nguyen's statement about reallocating half of Mungo's fixed income to PUBLIC REAL ESTATE.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — Nguyen's claims contain errors.

===
CLAIM 1 (public RE has LOWER correlation with equities): PARTIALLY CORRECT.
• Public REITs typically have HIGHER correlation with equities than private RE.
• REITs trade like stocks with correlation ~0.5-0.7 to broad equity.
• The claim of 'lower correlation' is misleading for PUBLIC REITs.

===
CLAIM 2 (lower equity beta): PARTIALLY CORRECT.
• REITs have moderate beta (~0.7-0.9) but NOT dramatically lower.
• Overstated as a diversification benefit.

===
CLAIM 3 (public RE 'highly liquid'): TRUE relative to private RE but misleading in stress.
• Under normal conditions, REITs trade continuously.
• In market crises, REITs are correlated with equity sell-offs.
• 'Highly liquid' overstates the case.

===
CLAIM 4 (avoid long-term commitment of private assets): OVERSIMPLIFIED.
• Private RE and PE do require commitments, but that's compensated by illiquidity premium.
• Avoiding long-term commitments means missing that premium.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies multiple errors + reasoning; 3=one error; 0=agrees.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Thompson's three claimed benefits of the risk-based approach to asset selection.

rationales.model_answer:
===
BENEFIT 1 (doesn't group assets based on few shared characteristics): CORRECT.
• Risk-based approach identifies factor exposures rather than lumping assets into broad classes.
• Genuine benefit over traditional asset allocation.

===
BENEFIT 2 (equally well for alt assets as public): INCORRECT / OVERSTATED.
• Risk-factor models DO NOT capture private asset returns as well as public.
• Illiquidity premium, manager-specific returns, private-market dynamics are poorly explained by public factor models.
• Same critique as Zimmermann's statement in Elbe case.

===
BENEFIT 3 (identifies factors AND sensitivities that are STABLE OVER TIME): INCORRECT.
• Factor sensitivities (betas) are NOT stable over time — they vary with market regime, business cycle, and manager style.
• Historical factor loadings may not persist.
• Overstated claim.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: For Nullarbor Foundation's shift to alternatives, DISCUSS two considerations regarding the choice of NONVENTURE PE and DIRECT LENDING for private credit.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
===
NONVENTURE PE:
• LARGER, more mature companies → less concentration risk than venture.
• More predictable cash flows and exits.
• Less J-curve than venture (positive returns earlier).
• BUT: higher deal valuations reduce return potential.
• Requires substantial due diligence and manager selection.

===
DIRECT LENDING (private credit):
• Floating rates → hedge against rate increases.
• Higher yields than public IG credit due to illiquidity premium.
• BUT: concentrated exposure to specific borrowers.
• CREDIT LOSSES in economic downturns can be substantial.
• Fee drag (management fees) reduces net returns.
• Liquidity constraint — cannot exit positions easily.
• Ties up capital during commitments.

PARTIAL: 5=at least one consideration per each of PE and DL; 3=one; 0=vague.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two governance / monitoring considerations for Nullarbor's ongoing management of the alternative investment allocation.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
• PACING PLAN: manage capital commitments across vintages to avoid over-commitment and undercommitment in any single vintage.
• LIQUIDITY BUDGETING: monitor uncalled commitments vs available liquidity for capital calls.
• MANAGER MONITORING: regular review of GP performance, style drift, key person changes.
• DIVERSIFICATION across managers, vintages, sectors, geographies.
• BENCHMARK RESULTS: appropriate benchmark selection for illiquid investments (private market indexes, PME).
• EXPENSE and FEE MONITORING.
• RISK REPORTING: understanding hidden leverage, concentration, correlation across the portfolio.
• EXIT PLANNING: managing secondary sales if liquidity needed.
• GOVERNANCE PROVISIONS in LP agreements (side letters, MFN clauses).

PARTIAL: 5=two clear governance/monitoring items + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Ava Chan (Ava Chan)
cases.vignette:
Ava Chan, age 52, is a high-net-worth investor residing in Vancouver,
Canada. Four years ago, the logistics firm she co-founded had an initial
public offering. One year ago, Chan retired when her obligation to work
for the company and to maintain ownership of its stock ended, to spend
time with her family and to manage her investment portfolio, valued at
approximately Can$425 million. Her investment adviser, Ethan Gill, has
extensive experience working with publicly traded equity and
fixed-income portfolios. With Gill's assistance, Chan sold her
concentrated stock holdings and created a well-diversified portfolio
with approximately 70% in global equities, 20% in real estate, and 10%
in investment-grade fixed income. The equity and fixed-income portions
of the portfolio consist entirely of index exchange-traded funds (ETFs),
and the real estate portfolio consists of a well-diversified selection
of REITs.

Chan's investment plans for the coming year are threefold. A Can$25
million portfolio is intended to provide an annual retirement income of
Can$1 million for her and her spouse. Trusts of Can$50 million each
are to be established for her two children. The remainder of her wealth
will be used to establish a charitable foundation to provide annual
grants in support of environmental preservation causes.

Chan meets with Gill to discuss potential changes to her investment
strategy and portfolio---specifically, the addition of more alternative
investments in the charitable foundation. She tells him, "As we separate
my current portfolio into the three trusts and the foundation later this
year, my intention is for the trust portfolios to have the same asset
allocation as my current portfolio but to shift approximately 50% of the
charitable foundation's portfolio into alternative investments, leaving
the remainder in the existing asset allocation. It seems that the growth
in the number of managers of these assets has made it much easier for
investors like me to access top-tier managers. I understand that
shifting the foundation portfolio into alternative investments will
drastically reduce the liquidity of those positions, but I don't believe
this will be a concern, given the long-term horizon of the foundation.
Investing in these assets will certainly require you to spend more time
conducting due diligence, but based on the advisory work you've done for
me in the past and your experience, I'm confident of your ability to do
this."

Gill responds, "It will be particularly important for the foundation
that I look for managers who offer full transparency regarding their
holdings and strategies, so the foundation's investment committee can
best understand the risk and return characteristics of the entire
portfolio. Also, because the current portfolio is appropriate for an
investor who believes financial markets are efficient, it will be
important for the investment committee and me to identify markets we
believe are not completely efficient and match those views to
alternative investment products and strategies that are most likely to
benefit from those perceived inefficiencies."

Gill also reminds Chan, "We have used a traditional mean--variance
approach to optimize your current portfolio allocation, but this will
not be appropriate for a portfolio that includes substantial allocations
to alternative investments. Returns to traditional asset classes are
normally distributed, whereas returns to alternative investments tend to
exhibit non-normality. For alternative investments, even the most basic
estimate of risk, the standard deviation of returns, is likely to be
measured incorrectly, because of the way returns are calculated and
reported. A common approach to portfolio optimization when alternative
assets are involved is a two-step process that first uses a
mean--variance analysis for the traditional asset classes and then
incorporates alternative assets using more sophisticated techniques,
such as Monte Carlo simulation."

Chan tells Gill, "My studies over the past year have made me realize
investments in alternative investments will require different kinds of
monitoring from the traditional asset classes in which I'm currently
invested. Regarding performance benchmarks, we will likely have to rely
on peer comparisons, which are good substitutes for the market indexes
we use for traditional asset classes. For many alternative investment
classes, particularly private equity and direct real estate investment,
we will not be able to assess the performance until the program is fully
invested, which can take many years. Furthermore, unlike traditional
asset classes, for alternative investments, we need to worry about how
the actions of other investors affect the performance of the investments
we make."

questions.stem:
In her discussion with Gill about shifting foundation portfolio
assets into alternative investments, Chan is most likely correct
about:

rationales.A: It is true that investment in private equity, hedge funds, direct real estate, and similar investments will require substantial due diligence efforts that were not required for investments in ETFs and REITs. Chan, however, has no evidence that Gill has the ability to conduct such efforts because his work with her in the past has involved trading and building portfolios of liquid, publicly traded securities.
rationales.B: A shift into private equity, hedge funds, direct real estate, and similar investments will reduce the liquidity of the portfolio, but 50% of the charitable foundation portfolio will remain in other, more liquid asset classes. The foundation is intended to provide ongoing annual grants, so these liquid investments should be sufficient to meet ongoing spending needs.
rationales.C: It is true that the number of providers of private equity, hedge funds, direct real estate, and similar investments has grown substantially over time; however, access to top-tier managers is quite limited because managers of alternative investments tend to be capacity constrained and many alternative investment strategies have limited market volume.

questions.stem:
Is Gill's response to Chan regarding transparency and market
efficiency most likely correct?

options.B: No, it is incorrect with regard to transparency.
options.C: No, it is incorrect with regard to market efficiency.

rationales.A: The statement regarding transparency is incorrect.
rationales.B: The statement regarding transparency is incorrect. Investors in alternative investments, such as private equity, direct real estate, and hedge funds, should not expect transparency in holdings or strategies; rather, investors in these asset classes must be comfortable with a lack of transparency in specific strategies and a "blind pool" of assets. If full transparency is desired or required, alternative investments will not be suitable.
rationales.C: The statement regarding market efficiency is correct. Many alternative investment asset classes, including private equity, direct real estate, and many hedge fund strategies, are predicated on the notion that markets are inefficient in specific ways. Chan's current portfolio follows a well-diversified, low-cost indexing strategy indicative of a belief in market efficiency. In making the switch, it will be important for Chan and Gill to identify specific markets they believe to be inefficient and invest in alternative investment products and strategies that are most likely to take advantage of those specific perceived inefficiencies.

questions.stem:
In his comments on portfolio optimization, Gill is *least
likely* correct regarding the:

options.B: accuracy of risk estimates for alternative investments.
options.C: common way portfolio optimization can incorporate alternative investments.

rationales.A: Despite its limitation, the assumption of normality is assumed for both traditional and alternative asset classes. The returns to traditional assets classes are not normally distributed; however, mean--variance techniques assuming normality have persisted because no standard approach for working with non-normally distributed returns exists. Many alternative asset classes exhibit more substantial skewness and kurtosis than traditional assets, partly because of high leverage, sensitivity to liquidity events, and asymmetric performance fees, making the assumption of normality more problematic, but these concerns also exist for some traditional assets (e.g., speculative grade bonds, microcap equities).
rationales.B: Returns to alternative investments are often based on appraisal data rather than market transactions, which results in artificial smoothing and underestimation of the standard deviation of returns. Infrequent reporting also makes accurate measurement of standard deviation difficult because of limited observations.
rationales.C: It is common for portfolio optimization that includes alternative assets to be performed in two stages--the first following a traditional mean--variance analysis and the second using Monte Carlo simulation or other more advanced techniques to account for the non-normality of returns of alternatives.

questions.stem:
Which of Chan's comments on monitoring alternative investments
is most likely correct? Those regarding:

options.C: the impact of other investors' actions on performance.

rationales.A: Peer evaluations are not a good substitute for market price indexes because it is difficult to identify a peer group with the same investment strategy as the manager being evaluated. Furthermore, the lack of standards for constructing such benchmarks results in the same manager's performance being assessed in very different ways by different evaluators.
rationales.B: Although it is difficult to assess the performance of an investment program that is not fully invested (such as a private equity investment or a direct real estate investment), investors must still monitor market conditions to ensure the investment program remains appropriate.
rationales.C: Many alternative investments use limited partnership or similar structures in which each investor owns a fairly large portion of the fund. Strategies often involve investment in illiquid assets. If a few investors pull out of a fund, managers can be forced to sell positions at disadvantageous times, resulting in poor performance for all investors. Remaining positions might also be particularly illiquid.

questions.stem: For Ava Chan's charitable foundation portfolio (Can$225M after allocations), EVALUATE her plan to shift 50% to alternatives given the foundation's long-term horizon.

rationales.model_answer:
APPROPRIATENESS: LARGELY APPROPRIATE with important caveats.

===
APPROPRIATE ASPECTS:
• Long horizon (foundation is permanent) supports illiquid investments.
• 50% shift keeps the other 50% liquid for annual grant distributions.
• Foundation status supports allocation to alternatives that provide higher long-term returns.

===
CAVEATS (Chan's assumptions to challenge):
• 'Easier to access top-tier managers due to growth in managers' — MISLEADING.
  → More managers = more mediocrity; top-tier remains hard to access.
  → Best PE managers oversubscribed; access requires established relationships.
• 'Long-term horizon of the foundation' — CORRECT but ANNUAL GRANTS still require liquidity.
• 'Confident of Gill's ability to do due diligence' — Gill has NO alt experience; this is a MAJOR CONCERN.
• DUE DILIGENCE is significantly more complex for alts than for public investments.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies appropriateness + at least 2 caveats; 3=general assessment; 0=uncritical.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Gill's response about looking for managers with FULL TRANSPARENCY of holdings and strategies.

rationales.model_answer:
===
APPROPRIATENESS: PARTIALLY VALID.

===
SUPPORTIVE reasoning:
• Transparency helps the investment committee understand risk and return.
• Enables proper due diligence and risk aggregation across the portfolio.
• Allows the foundation to detect strategy drift.

===
CRITIQUE:
• Requiring FULL TRANSPARENCY may SCREEN OUT MANY SKILLED ALTERNATIVE MANAGERS.
• Many top hedge funds and PE managers PROTECT TRADE SECRETS and holdings.
• Insisting on transparency may relegate the foundation to lower-quality managers.
• Best practice: seek REASONABLE transparency with appropriate NDAs, not FULL position disclosure.
• Manager selection is a trade-off between transparency and access to skill.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies both benefits AND drawbacks; 3=one-sided; 0=uncritical.

questions.stem: For Chan's Can$25M retirement income sub-portfolio needing Can$1M annually, DETERMINE the required return (real terms, assuming perpetuity) and DISCUSS asset allocation appropriateness.

rationales.model_answer:
REQUIRED RETURN CALCULATION:
For perpetuity: required RETURN = Withdrawal / Principal = $1M / $25M = 4%.

REAL vs NOMINAL:
• If Chan's $1M is desired in REAL terms (inflation-adjusted), required REAL return = 4%.
• If $1M is nominal fixed, real return needs to be higher to maintain purchasing power (need to keep $25M principal growing with inflation).
• Assuming Chan wants inflation-adjusted income: 4% real return needed.

ALLOCATION APPROPRIATENESS:
• Current allocation 70% equities + 20% RE + 10% FI = high risk.
• A 4% real return is achievable with moderate risk allocation (60/40 equity/bond).
• The retirement sub-portfolio should prioritize STABILITY OF INCOME.
• Current 70% equity + 10% FI is TOO AGGRESSIVE for a retirement income portfolio.
• Recommend: 40-60% FI + moderate equity for growth + inflation hedge.

PARTIAL: 5=4% required return + allocation critique; 3=return only; 0=wrong.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two considerations for the DIFFERENT allocations of the two children's trusts (Can$50M each) vs the charitable foundation and retirement portfolio.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO considerations.

Accepted considerations:
===
TIME HORIZON:
• Children's trusts have HORIZONS extending decades (beyond Chan's lifetime).
• Foundation is PERPETUAL.
• Retirement is a fixed period.
• Different horizons → different risk tolerances.

===
BENEFICIARY NEEDS:
• Children's trusts: unknown future needs; balance growth and stability.
• Foundation: specific mission (environmental grants); need for regular distributions.
• Retirement: known needs; focus on income stability.

===
TAX TREATMENT:
• Children's trusts and foundation may have different tax structures.
• Foundation may be tax-exempt; children's trusts may face beneficiary taxation.

===
GOVERNANCE:
• Trusts have beneficiary rights and trustee obligations.
• Foundation has board governance and grantmaking priorities.

===
LIQUIDITY:
• Children's trusts: possible distributions at various life events.
• Foundation: annual grants require regular liquidity.
• Retirement: monthly income needs.

PARTIAL: 5=two considerations covering multiple dimensions; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Rothhaven Foundation (Alt Investments Pathway)
cases.vignette:
The Rothhaven Foundation is a $1.2 billion charitable foundation established
in 2015 to support medical research in rare pediatric diseases. Rothhaven's
current asset allocation is 55% publicly-traded equity (broad global mandate),
30% investment-grade fixed income, 10% publicly-traded REITs, and 5% cash.
The foundation targets a 5% real return net of fees to support its annual
grantmaking of approximately 4% of a rolling three-year average of portfolio
value, with the remaining return supporting long-term real capital growth.

Elaine Kohls, the foundation's chief investment officer, is meeting with
consultant Marcus Redland to discuss adding alternative investments to the
portfolio. Redland has proposed the following asset allocation shift:

Exhibit 1: Proposed Asset Allocation Change

Asset Class            Current    Proposed    Change
Global Equity          55%        40%         -15%
IG Fixed Income        30%        15%         -15%
REITs                  10%        10%          0%
Cash                   5%         5%           0%
Private Equity         0%         15%         +15%
Private Real Estate    0%         10%         +10%
Hedge Funds            0%         5%          +5%

Redland provides the following supporting analysis:
- The proposed private equity allocation targets 60% buyouts, 20% growth
  equity, and 20% venture capital, invested across four vintages over four years.
- Private real estate allocation is direct commercial property (office/industrial
  mix) in gateway US cities.
- Hedge fund allocation targets 40% long-short equity, 40% global macro, and
  20% event-driven.
- Redland claims: "The proposed allocation improves expected return by
  approximately 150 basis points, reduces total portfolio volatility by 200
  basis points due to lower correlations, and provides genuine downside
  protection through hedge fund exposure."

Kohls raises three concerns:

Concern 1: The foundation has grantmaking obligations that require predictable
liquidity. Under the proposed allocation, 30% of the portfolio would be
illiquid, and capital calls could exceed the foundation's ability to fund
without disrupting grantmaking.

Concern 2: The foundation's investment committee lacks deep experience in
alternative investment due diligence, particularly for private equity fund
manager selection.

Concern 3: Rothhaven's current benchmarking methodology uses public market
indexes and would need substantial revision to properly assess alternative
investment performance.

Redland responds: "For liquidity, we can pace commitments to match expected
distributions from mature vintages. For due diligence expertise, I recommend
retaining a specialist consultant for manager selection. For benchmarking, we
should use public market equivalent (PME) methodology for private equity and
hedge fund composite benchmarks."

questions.stem: EVALUATE Redland's claim that the proposed allocation improves return by ~150 bps, reduces volatility by ~200 bps, and provides downside protection through hedge funds.

rationales.model_answer:
===
RETURN IMPROVEMENT (~150 bps): PLAUSIBLE but overstated.
• Private equity historical premium over public: 200-400 bps net of fees.
• Private RE premium: 100-200 bps.
• Hedge fund alpha: often negative net of fees post-2010.
• Aggregate 150 bps improvement plausible if manager selection succeeds.

===
VOLATILITY REDUCTION (~200 bps): OVERSTATED.
• Reported low volatility for PE/RE is due to STALE PRICING and SMOOTHING.
• True underlying vol of PE ≈ 25% (comparable to equity).
• Correlation understated similarly.
• Genuine vol reduction is modest, maybe 50-100 bps.

===
DOWNSIDE PROTECTION VIA HEDGE FUNDS: OVERSTATED.
• Hedge fund correlations to equity RISE in crisis (2008, 2020 examples).
• Long-short equity strategies do provide some downside cushion.
• Global macro can provide crisis alpha but with high dispersion across managers.
• 'Genuine downside protection' is a strong claim not supported empirically.

PARTIAL: 5=critiques all three claims + reasoning; 3=critiques one or two; 0=agrees.

questions.stem: DISCUSS whether Kohls's Concern 1 about liquidity is valid + IDENTIFY appropriate liquidity management strategies for the proposed allocation.

rationales.model_answer:
===
VALIDITY: Concern 1 IS VALID.

Accepted reasoning:
• 30% illiquid (PE + private RE) is significant given 4% annual grantmaking.
• Capital calls in year 1-3 can exceed distributions from mature vintages, creating negative cash flow.
• Denominator effect in downturns: public assets decline faster than PE marks, inflating alt % of portfolio and constraining rebalancing.
• Grants must be paid regardless of illiquidity conditions.

===
LIQUIDITY MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES (any TWO):
• PACING PLAN: commit to PE across multiple vintages (e.g., 3-4% per year over 4-5 years) to smooth capital calls.
• LIQUIDITY BUFFERS: hold 3-5% cash + short-duration IG bonds explicitly for capital calls and grants.
• BRIDGE FINANCING: subscription lines of credit to smooth capital calls.
• SECONDARY MARKET: use PE secondaries market for interim liquidity.
• DIVERSIFICATION across vintages so distributions from mature funds fund calls of new funds.
• STRESS TESTING: model liquidity needs under adverse scenarios.

PARTIAL: 5=validity + two strategies + reasoning; 3=validity + one strategy; 0=vague.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Redland's proposed benchmarking approaches: PME for PE and hedge fund composite benchmarks.

rationales.model_answer:
===
PME for PRIVATE EQUITY: APPROPRIATE.
• Public Market Equivalent (PME) properly compares PE returns to what would have been earned in an equivalent public market investment.
• Kaplan-Schoar PME and Direct Alpha are widely accepted PE benchmarks.
• Accounts for timing of capital calls and distributions — critical for irregular cash flows.
• Superior to IRR-only or time-weighted return comparisons.

===
HEDGE FUND COMPOSITE BENCHMARKS: PARTIALLY APPROPRIATE.
• Hedge fund indices (HFRI, Credit Suisse) have well-known issues:
  - SURVIVORSHIP BIAS: failed funds drop out.
  - BACKFILL BIAS: successful funds added retroactively.
  - SELF-SELECTION: only funds choosing to report are included.
• Composite benchmarks understate true universe average returns.
• Better: strategy-specific benchmarks (e.g., HFRX equity long-short for long-short equity component) with awareness of biases.

PARTIAL: 5=both correctly assessed + reasoning; 3=one correctly; 0=vague.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two considerations for Rothhaven's mission alignment when selecting specific alternative investment managers.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
• ESG / IMPACT ALIGNMENT: Rothhaven's mission is medical research; consider impact investing in life-sciences PE or biotech venture funds.
• MISSION-RELATED INVESTING (MRI): allocations to health-focused managers may align portfolio with foundation purpose.
• REPUTATIONAL RISK: avoid managers investing in sectors that could reflect poorly on the foundation (e.g., tobacco, opioid manufacturers).
• GRANT-RECIPIENT CONFLICTS: due diligence on managers' portfolio companies vs. Rothhaven's grantees.
• GOVERNANCE STANDARDS: managers with robust ESG frameworks align with foundation values.
• PROGRAM-RELATED INVESTMENTS (PRI): possible use of PRI category to align investment with mission tax-efficiently.
• LIQUIDITY OF EXITS: PE portfolios may include health-tech companies whose exits could benefit Rothhaven's grantees.

PARTIAL: 5=two clear considerations + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Chattahoochee (Demarcus Miller)
cases.vignette:
Demarcus Miller is a principal at Chattahoochee Advisers, a firm that
provides investment management for institutional clients in the
southeastern United States. Miller is holding an orientation meeting
with Kalisha Browne, a new member of Chattahoochee's sales team.

Miller tells Browne, "Chattahoochee provides its services to relatively
small institutional investors, typically those with assets of USD20
million--USD100 million. Your primary goal is to attract new clients in
our three primary target areas: insurance companies, defined benefit
pension funds, and endowments and foundations."

Miller continues, "The asset portfolios of our clients can vary widely.
How well do you understand the differences between the investment
approaches used by various institutions?"

Browne replies, "I'm familiar with a number of them. For our clients
with long-term horizons and relatively low liquidity needs, we could
recommend the Canadian model, which entails a high exposure to
alternative investments. Given their primary investment objectives,
pension funds and insurance companies are usually well suited to the
liability-driven investing model."

Browne asks Miller for some advice on developing a proposal for a
potential client, the defined benefit pension plan for Tucker
Manufacturing. Tucker is a publicly traded firm whose stock is a part of
the Russell 2000 Index. The firm has been in business for 82 years and
has a Fitch credit rating of BBB+.

The plan, which does not require any contributions from employees, has
160 retirees and 280 active employees. Employees vest fully after six
years of service. The average age of active employees is 54 years, their
average length of service is 26 years, and employees may retire and
begin to collect benefits after 30 years of service. Benefits are based
on years of service and the average salary during the last five years of
service. Retirees receive a cost-of-living adjustment based on changes
in the consumer price index. Prior to retiring, employees can choose to
receive a lump sum payment in place of future pension benefits.

The asset allocation of the portfolio is:

-   37% publicly traded equity, mostly small-cap growth equities, about
half of which is in Tucker common stock, which is considered a
growth stock and pays no dividends;

-   29% private equity, invested in two recently launched funds; and

-   34% directly owned real estate, consisting of vacant land that is
expected to be developed in 15--20 years.

The current value of plan assets is USD109 million. Values are
calculated using market prices where available and third-party
appraisals or valuations where market prices are unavailable or stale.
The value of plan liabilities is USD93 million, which is calculated by
discounting projected benefit payments using a discount rate equal to
the average total return the current asset allocation would have earned
over the last 15 years.

Browne tells Miller that Tucker Manufacturing's management is happy the
value of plan assets exceeds the value of plan liabilities by USD16
million. The company anticipates recording an asset on its balance sheet
called "plan surplus" in this amount. She asks him whether he sees any
problems with this idea.

Miller reminds Browne that her proposal should indicate any risks
present in the Tucker pension fund that would be reduced by the
Chattahoochee investment proposal. Browne responds, "I have been
evaluating a number of potential risk sources and have identified some
concerns that I will raise."

Browne also shares her proposal for a revised investment objective for
the Tucker pension:

The primary objectives of the Tucker Manufacturing pension plan are to
maintain an asset portfolio that is duration matched to plan liabilities
and over the long term earns a return equal to the inflation rate plus
the growth rate in salaries of Tucker Manufacturing employees. The
secondary objectives of the plan are to prevent being underfunded and
avoid increasing required sponsor contributions above their historical
level.

questions.stem:
Which characteristic would *least likely* affect Browne's ability to
attract new clients from his universe of target institutional investors?

rationales.A: Small institutional investors, such as those in Browne's USD20 million--USD100 million universe, typically outsource investment management operations. They lack sufficient scale to hire adequate resources for an investment function. Insurance companies have investment horizons that are different from those of endowments and foundations and defined benefit plans. Each of these types of institutional investors face different regulatory requirements.
rationales.B: Insurance companies have investment horizons that are different from those of endowments and foundations and defined benefit plans.
rationales.C: Each of these types of institutional investors face different regulatory requirements.

questions.stem:
Is Browne *most likely* correct in her discussion of investment
approaches?

options.B: No, she is incorrect regarding the Canadian model.
options.C: No, she is incorrect regarding the liability-driven investing model

rationales.A: She is incorrect regarding the Canadian model.
rationales.B: The Canadian model does entail a large allocation to alternatives, which can be value enhancing for institutions with long-term horizons and low liquidity needs; however, it requires these institutions to develop the expertise to manage alternative investments in house. Small institutional investors do not have the scale to hire or develop this expertise. Further, Chattahoochee provides investment management for clients and would be unlikely to recommend an approach that leads the client to manage assets in house.
rationales.C: She is correct that defined benefit pension plans and insurance companies, which focus on generating returns sufficient to pay liabilities, are well suited to using the liability-driven investing model.

questions.stem:
The stakeholders of the Tucker pension plan would* least
likely* have concerns about the:

options.B: plan's ability to meet liquidity needs.
options.C: imbalance between the maturity of plan assets and plan liabilities.

rationales.A: Given the age and average tenure of current employees, the vast majority are vested; therefore, there is no uncertainty regarding the benefit due. Further, should non-vested employees leave, their accumulated benefit would remain in the portfolio, increasing its funded status, to the benefit of beneficiaries and the plan sponsor. However, the plan's portfolio is invested in assets that produce little cash flows on their own. A large portion of these assets are illiquid (private equity and undeveloped real estate). Therefore, having sufficient liquidity to meet cash outflow needs is a concern, particularly because much of the current workforce is close to, if not at, the age of retirement eligibility and can choose a lump sum cash payment. In addition, given the way changes in interest rates can affect the value of plan liabilities, many defined benefit pension plans have adopted a liability-driven approach in which the duration of plan assets is chosen to match the duration of plan liabilities. The Tucker plan portfolio contains only equities and alternative assets with an unclear duration, leading to concerns about the imbalance between liabilities and assets and the resulting interest rate risk
rationales.B: The plan's portfolio is invested in assets that produce little cash flows on their own. A large portion of these assets are illiquid (private equity and undeveloped real estate). Therefore, having sufficient liquidity to meet cash outflow needs is a concern, particularly because much of the current workforce is close to, if not at, the age of retirement eligibility and can choose a lump sum cash payment. Therefore, stakeholders would have legitimate concerns about the plan's ability to meet potential liquidity needs.
rationales.C: Given the way changes in interest rates can affect the value of plan liabilities, many defined benefit pension plans have adopted a liability-driven approach in which the duration of plan assets is chosen to match the duration of plan liabilities. The Tucker plan portfolio contains only equities and alternative assets with an unclear duration. Although this asset allocation may provide strong expected returns, it does lead to legitimate concerns about the imbalance between liabilities and assets and the resulting interest rate risk.

questions.stem:
How should Miller *most likely* respond to Browne's question about
Tucker's anticipated pension surplus asset?

options.A: The value of the surplus is likely overstated.
options.B: US accounting rules do not permit a sponsor to claim a pension surplus on the balance sheet.
options.C: There do not appear to be any problems with the value or anticipated accounting treatment of the surplus.

rationales.A: The pension plan is incorrectly using a discount rate based on the returns to its current asset allocation, which includes small-cap equity, private equity, and illiquid real estate investments. These returns are historically much higher than the returns to high-grade bond yields averaged over the last 25 years, which is the maximum discount rate permitted in the United States. Therefore, the value of liabilities is being calculated with a higher-than-allowed discount rate and has likely been understated. This means the value of the surplus is likely overstated.
rationales.B: US GAAP not only permits but requires firms to report the value of the overfunded or underfunded pension plan on the balance sheet as an asset or a liability, respectively.
rationales.C: The value of the surplus is likely overstated because the discount rate being used to calculate the value of plan liabilities is too high.

questions.stem:
Which of the following would Browne *least likely* consider a source
of risk for the Tucker pension plan?

options.C: Common risk exposures between the sponsor and the fund

rationales.A: There are a substantial number of retirees, and the active employees are older, with long service records, suggesting many are nearing retirement. The current asset allocation is both illiquid in nature (newly launched private equity funds and directly owned undeveloped real estate) and volatile in value (small-cap equities). Given the workforce characteristics, the fund should likely be moving to a more current income-producing allocation and one that would reduce surplus volatility.
rationales.B: The plan sponsor holds an investment-grade credit rating, and there are no indications of declining financial health.
rationales.C: because 18­%--19% of the fund's assets are invested in the sponsor's common stock. If the sponsor's financial health declines, the value of fund assets will decline at precisely the same time the sponsor is required to make additional contributions. Because it is less likely to be able to do so when it is in poor financial health, this situation creates a greater risk of insolvency than if that allocation were in different assets.

options.B: No, the secondary objectives are incompatible.
options.C: No, the proposed required return is inappropriate.

rationales.A: The proposed required return is inappropriate.
rationales.B: The secondary objectives are not incompatible. It is common for a plan that is currently overfunded to want to maintain status quo, and it is common to do so by engaging in ways of minimizing the volatility of the surplus. Most funds also act to minimize the likelihood the plan sponsor would have to increase its contributions; indeed, many will take on additional asset risk to increase plan asset expected returns and decrease sponsor expected contributions. Although these objectives are compatible, they will have to be balanced against one another.
rationales.C: The proposed required return is inappropriate. For any defined benefit plan, the required return should take into account the anticipated change in future liability cash flows and the change in valuation of those cash flows. The cash flows are affected by changes in active employee salaries, changes in active employee longevity, and the rate of inflation (retired employees). The valuation of liability cash flows will change as market interest rates change. In general, the required return for plan assets should exceed the discount rate on plan liabilities. The required return specified in the proposal does not reflect appropriate market interest rates used to discount liability cash flows (investment-grade long-term bond yields) and double counts inflation, which is a primary factor in active employee salary increases.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Browne's statements about institutional investment models. IDENTIFY which statements are correct and which are incorrect.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STMT 1 (Canadian model for long-horizon, low-liquidity clients — high alt exposure): CORRECT.
• Canadian model is characterized by direct investment in real assets, private equity, and alternatives; high-alt allocation; internal management.
• Appropriate for long-horizon investors with limited liquidity needs.

===
STMT 2 (pension funds AND insurance companies both suited to LDI): PARTIALLY CORRECT.
