IMF and the European Central Bank, and

3.  the US balance of trade deficit with euro-based countries had
continued to decline in the past several years and was expected to
continue to decline.

The Spanish investment involved Testa acquiring 200,000 shares of a
packaging company at EUR90 per share. He decided to fully hedge the
position with a six-month USD/EUR forward contract. Details of the euro
hedge at initiation and three months later are provided in Exhibit 1.
Three months after the purchase, the shares had increased to EUR100
each, but Testa, believing that a still higher price was likely,
maintained the position. He also indicated that he did not anticipate
having to roll the hedge forward at its maturity. Both he and Fournier
believed that further appreciation of the euro was quite likely, and the
increase in the notional size of the position was hedged using currency
options. They based their choices on the information provided in Exhibit
2.

**Exhibit 1 2009 Spot and Forward USD/EUR Quotes (Bid-Offer) and
Annualized MRR Rates**

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Maturity         At            Three         **At
Initiation     Months          Maturity
Later**
-------------------- ---------------- ---------------- ----------------
Spot (USD/EUR)       1.3935/1.3983    1.4106/1.4210    1.4189/1.4289

3-month forward      --8.1/--7.6      --21.6/--21.0

6-month forward      --19.0/--18.3                     --27.0/--26.2

USD MRR                               1.266%

EUR MRR                               1.814%
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

In 2014, Testa notified Fournier that he anticipated taking a position
in a plastics producer located in India. Fournier warned him that the
Indian rupee (INR) was a restricted currency and that currency
management would not be as simple as in the other transactions handled
previously. Fournier said that non-deliverable forwards (NDFs) on the
rupee were available, as they were for the currencies of other
developing countries. When asked how non-deliverable forwards differed
from the contracts they had used in the past, Fournier responded:

-   NDFs are cash settled in the non-controlled currency of the currency
pair,

-   NDFs have greater credit risk associated with them than outright
forward contracts because the central banks in most developing
countries are not as strong as they are in developed countries, and

-   the pricing of NDFs may differ from what is expected on the basis of
arbitrage conditions.

In 2015, Testa informed Fournier that he had taken large positions in
both a New Zealand firm and an Australian packaging firm. The positions
were roughly equal in size in terms of the US dollar. Fournier informed
Testa that the correlation between USD/AUD and USD/NZD was approximately
0.85. Given the size of the positions, Testa indicated that he wished to
minimize any foreign exchange exposure.

questions.stem:
In terms of the objectives and constraints that were incorporated
into Testa's IPS, the one that best explains the initial euro exposure
of the Spanish investment in 2009 is the one related to his:

rationales.A: HNW's assessment was that the euro was likely to appreciate against the US dollar within the next six months. Reason 1, higher real rates in euro-based countries, is consistent with an appreciation of the euro. Higher euro rates will attract "foreign" investors and drive up demand for the euro as they acquire those investments.
rationales.B: The main goal of Testa's investment program is the realization of returns based on his perceived superior ability to discover merger and acquisition targets. The position was fully hedged even though both he and his adviser believed that the euro was likely to appreciate over the investment horizon; there was no attempt to exploit that belief either with futures or options (although the use of options was restricted to strong market views at the time of rebalancing of an already winning position).
rationales.C: Testa's investment program did not impose any liquidity or income needs on the position.

questions.stem:
Which of Testa's reasons for the future course of the USD/EUR
exchange rate in 2009 is most consistent with HNW's assessment?

rationales.A: Correct. HNW forecasts euro appreciation against the US dollar. Higher REAL interest rates in euro-based countries attract capital flows toward euro assets, supporting euro appreciation — Reason 1 is consistent with HNW's assessment under the economic fundamentals framework.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Potential defaults of euro-based countries from excessive debt are euro-NEGATIVE fundamentals; IMF/ECB support measures are crisis responses, not appreciation drivers. Reason 2 does not support a stronger euro.
rationales.C: Incorrect. A DECLINING US trade deficit with euro-based countries means improving US external balances — a US-dollar-supportive fundamental that argues for euro depreciation, the opposite of HNW's view.

questions.stem:
Using Exhibit 1, if the Spanish shares had been sold after three
months, the cash outflow (in US dollars) required to close out the
forward contract would have been closest to:

rationales.A: In implementing the hedge, euros (the base currency) must be sold against the US dollar. The base currency is selling at a discount and thus would "roll up the curve" as the contract approaches maturity. Settlement of the forward contract would entail buying euros at a higher price---that is, selling low and buying high---resulting in a negative roll yield. Since the euro has appreciated by the time the hedge needs to be extended, this tends to further increase the cost of euros to settle the original contract and makes the roll yield even more negative---that is, sell low, buy even higher.
rationales.B: The initial foreign asset position was EUR18 million: 200,000 shares × EUR90/share. The six-month forward contract would have been sold using the bid of the base currency (euro) at an all-in forward rate of 1.3935 -- 19/10,000 = 1.3916 USD/EUR.    If the position had been closed in three months, a three-month forward contract would have to be purchased at the offer of the base currency at an all-in forward rate of 1.4210 -- 21/10,000 = 1.4189 USD/EUR.    The cash outflow at settlement would have been EUR18 million × (1.4189 -- 1.3916) USD/EUR = USD491,400. This amount needs to be discounted by three months at the US dollar MRR rate: 491,400/(1 + 0.01266 × 90/360) = USD489,850.
rationales.C: It uses the settlement cash flow, ignoring any discounting: USD491,400.

questions.stem:
If the 2009 forward hedge had been rolled forward at its maturity, using
Exhibit 1, the roll yield would most likely have been:

options.A: negative, but the currency change made it less negative.
options.B: positive, but the currency change reduced some of this effect.
options.C: negative, and the currency change made it even more negative.

rationales.A: The euro MRR rate is used to discount the settlement cash flow: 491,400/(1 + 0.01814 × 90/360) = USD489,182.
rationales.B: In implementing the hedge, euros (the base currency) must be sold against the US dollar. The base currency is selling at a discount and thus would "roll up the curve" as the contract approaches maturity. Settlement of the forward contract would entail buying euros at a higher price--that is, selling low and buying high--resulting in a negative roll yield. Since the euro has appreciated by the time the hedge needs to be extended, this tends to further increase the cost of euros to settle the original contract and makes the roll yield even more negative--that is, sell low, buy even higher.
rationales.C: In implementing the hedge, euros (the base currency) must be sold against the US dollar. The base currency is selling at a discount and thus would "roll up the curve" as the contract approaches maturity. Settlement of the forward contract would entail buying euros at a higher price--that is, selling low and buying high--resulting in a negative roll yield. Since the euro has appreciated by the time the hedge needs to be extended, this tends to further increase the cost of euros to settle the original contract and makes the roll yield even more negative--that is, sell low, buy even higher.

questions.stem:
Which of Fournier's 2014 comments about non-deliverable forwards
(NDFs) is least accurate? The one concerning:

rationales.A: Fournier's statement regarding settlement of NDFs is correct. Non-deliverable forwards exist in situations involving capital controls on one of the currencies. The controlled currency cannot be physically settled (i.e., not delivered or received), but instead it is cash settled in the non-controlled currency.
rationales.B: Fournier's statement regarding credit risk is incorrect. The credit risk does not relate to the central bank of the developing country but, rather, the counterparty risk faced in the contract. The credit risk underlying an NDF is lower than an outright forward contract since the notional size of the contract is not exchanged at settlement, but only the non-controlled currency amount by which the notional size of the controlled currency has changed over the life of the contract---that is, the change in the controlled currency times the notional size converted to the non-controlled currency at the spot rate on the settlement date.
rationales.C: Fournier's statement regarding settlement of NDFs is correct. Non-deliverable forwards exist in situations involving capital controls on one of the currencies. The controlled currency cannot be physically settled (i.e., not delivered or received), but instead it is cash settled in the non-controlled currency.

questions.stem:
The *most* appropriate hedging strategy for the 2015 positions, in
keeping with Testa's wishes, is based on a:

options.A: direct hedge on each currency separately.
options.B: cross-hedge of the two currencies in the portfolio.
options.C: minimum-variance hedge of the two currencies in the portfolio.

rationales.A: A direct hedge on each currency is the most appropriate strategy for the long positions in the Australian and New Zealand dollar. The high correlation between the currencies does not help here because the investor will be using forward contracts to sell both of these currencies. The high correlation between the currencies could have been exploited with a cross-hedge or a minimum-variance hedge if one of the foreign assets was held long and the other short.
rationales.B: The high correlation between the currencies could have been exploited with a cross-hedge or a minimum-variance hedge if one of the foreign assets was held long and the other short.
rationales.C: The high correlation between the currencies could have been exploited with a cross-hedge or a minimum-variance hedge if one of the foreign assets was held long and the other short. Although a minimum hedge portfolio can be constructed without simultaneous long and short positions, the greatest risk reduction (which Testa desires) would arise if that were to occur.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Testa's three reasons for expecting the euro to appreciate against the USD.

rationales.model_answer:
===
REASON 1 (higher real interest rates in euro-based countries): CORRECT.
• Higher real rates attract capital inflows → currency appreciation.
• Standard real interest rate differential theory.

===
REASON 2 (potential defaults leading to IMF/ECB support): PARTIALLY CORRECT.
• Strong central bank support can stabilize but does not necessarily strengthen the currency.
• If underlying issues remain, monetary support may be seen as debasement.
• Ambiguous impact on currency value.

===
REASON 3 (declining US trade deficit with euro-based countries): CORRECT.
• Reduced trade deficit means less USD supply on foreign exchange market.
• Fundamental support for USD strength (or EUR weakness) — REASON IS BACKWARDS.
• If US deficit is DECLINING (US buying less from Europe), euro DEMAND decreases → EUR should DEPRECIATE, not appreciate.
• Testa's causality is REVERSED.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly + reasoning; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: EVALUATE the appropriateness of Testa's 6-month forward hedge given the IPS constraints and HNW's forecast.

rationales.model_answer:
APPROPRIATE for the given constraints.

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• IPS constraint: 'currency exposure would usually not extend beyond a six-month period' → 6-month forward matches.
• IPS constraint: 'cost of hedging should be minimized' → forward has minimal cost (only the forward premium/discount).
• Full hedge (rather than option) is appropriate given HNW's positive currency view — hedge locks in the expected currency gain.
• Wait: full hedge ELIMINATES both upside and downside. Since HNW expects EUR APPRECIATION, hedging FORFEITS the expected gain.

COUNTERPOINT: Given HNW's positive EUR view, the FORWARD HEDGE is SUBOPTIMAL — it locks in the forward premium/discount and eliminates upside.
• A better choice given positive currency view: PARTIAL hedge or NO hedge.
• Options (call on EUR) would preserve upside — but IPS restricts options use.

VERDICT: Forward is appropriate for IPS compliance but SUBOPTIMAL for capturing positive view.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies tension between IPS + view; 3=addresses only one aspect; 0=fully approves.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Fournier's three statements about non-deliverable forwards (NDFs) on the Indian rupee.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STMT 1 (NDFs cash settled in non-controlled currency): CORRECT.
• NDFs settle in the freely-convertible currency (USD in this case), not in INR.
• The difference between contracted rate and fixing rate is paid in USD.

===
STMT 2 (NDFs have GREATER credit risk because developing country central banks are weaker): INCORRECT / MISLEADING.
• NDF credit risk is COUNTERPARTY risk (banks, dealers), not central bank risk.
• Weakness of developing central banks affects currency stability but not NDF settlement mechanism specifically.
• Modern NDFs are often centrally cleared or collateralized.

===
STMT 3 (NDF pricing may differ from arbitrage conditions): CORRECT.
• Restricted currencies have limited arbitrage between spot and NDF market.
• Capital controls prevent free flows that would enforce interest rate parity.
• NDF forward rates can deviate significantly from theoretical values.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: For Testa's paired AUD and NZD positions (equal USD size, correlation 0.85), DISCUSS the appropriate hedging strategy given his desire to minimize FX exposure.

rationales.model_answer:
APPROACH: PROXY HEDGE using AUD forward (or NZD forward) to hedge BOTH positions.

Accepted reasoning:
• With 0.85 correlation, movements in AUD and NZD are largely aligned.
• Rather than execute both AUD forward AND NZD forward (2 contracts, 2 sets of costs), use ONE hedge to cover BOTH exposures.
• Proxy hedge: use AUD forward at 2× notional to cover both positions.
• Basis risk of ~15% (1 - 0.85) remains.

ALTERNATIVE APPROACH — CROSS-HEDGE:
• Hedge NZD position using AUD forward (or vice versa).
• Effectively uses AUD as a proxy for NZD.

REJECTED:
• Full separate hedges — higher cost, but eliminates basis risk.
• Given Testa wants to MINIMIZE FX exposure but also implicitly minimize hedging cost, proxy hedge is the balanced choice.

PARTIAL: 5=proxy hedge + reasoning + basis risk acknowledgment; 3=identifies proxy concept; 0=full separate hedges.

cases.title: Currency-Sabanai (Marina Campos)
cases.vignette:
Marina Campos is a senior portfolio manager for Sabanai Investimentos in
Sao Paulo, Brazil. Sabanai provides investment management and advisory
services for high-net-worth and institutional clients. She is assisted
by two portfolio analysts, Fabiana Traldi and Pedro Peixaria. Campos is
meeting with Traldi and Peixaria to discuss the portfolios of three
clients.

The first client is Gilvan Araujo Dias, a high-net-worth client who has
given Sabanai responsibility for managing his foreign investments, which
consist of equity investments in the United Kingdom and Germany. His
other assets consist of equity and corporate bond investments in Brazil.
Exhibit 1 summarizes information on Dias's foreign portfolio holdings
and exchange rates.

**Exhibit 1 Gilvan Araujo Dias, Information on Foreign Asset Holdings
and Exchange Rates**

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
UK Assets  German        Spot Exchange
Assets        Rates
---------- -------------- --------------- --------------- ---------------
Date   Value in     Value in      BRL/GBP     BRL/EUR
GBP          EUR

1/1/2013   83,400,000     55,000,000      3.8729          3.0359

1/1/2014   86,000,000     51,000,000      4.1025          3.5142
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

Dias has asked whether it would be appropriate for him to hedge his
foreign currency exposure. Campos raises the issue with Traldi and
Peixaria. Traldi responds, "In the short run, if the correlation between
foreign asset returns and foreign currency returns is negative, then
there may be a need to hedge all foreign currency exposure.
Alternatively, one could implement a currency overlay program in which
the currency exposure is fully hedged and currency alpha is generated
separately. This currency overlay strategy will only be successful in
adding value to the portfolio if the currency alpha has a high
correlation with Brazilian equities and corporate bonds."

The second client, BC Fundos de Pensao (BC), manages pension funds for
numerous local companies and has currency exposure to the USD, the EUR,
and the GBP. BC wants Sabanai to provide guidance on using active
currency management strategies for the portfolios they manage. Peixaria
has been assigned this task and has collected information on one-year
yield levels in the United States, United Kingdom, and Eurozone, as well
as one-year implied volatility for various currency pairs extracted from
option pricing models. This information is provided in Exhibit 2.

Exhibit 2 One-Year Yield Levels and Implied Volatilities

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Panel A
---------------------------------- ------------------------------------
Country                        One-Year Yield

United States                      0.05%

United Kingdom                     0.40%

Eurozone                           0.11%

Panel B

Currency Pair                  One-Year Implied Volatility

USD/GBP                            5.50%

GBP/EUR                            7.50%

USD/EUR                            9.50%
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Peixaria indicates that his research suggests that the USD/EUR currency
pair will become more volatile over the near term. He recommends that BC
implement an options-based strategy using USD/EUR options to profit from
the expected increase in volatility.

The third client is Fundo do Brasil (FB), a Brazilian sovereign wealth
fund. FB has long equity positions in Australian and Swiss equities.
Spot and forward market currency information for AUD and CHF is provided
in Exhibit 3. FB managers have asked Campos for advice on whether it
would be appropriate to hedge the currency exposure with forward
contracts in AUD and CHF. Campos indicates that she will examine the use
of forward contracts to hedge currency exposure.

Exhibit 3 Spot and Forward Rates for AUD and CHF

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Currency Pair     Currency    Six-Month         **Six-Month
Spot Rate    Forward Rate       Forecast Spot
Rate**
--------------------- -------------- -------------------- --------------
BRL/AUD               2.1046         2.1523               2.0355

BRL/CHF               2.5309         2.4641               2.5642
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Traldi suggests that the use of put options might be a better way to
hedge currency exposure. Campos responds that there are better
options-based strategies that can exploit market views and reduce
hedging costs. She suggests the following strategies:

Strategy 1: For AUD exposure, the appropriate strategy is to be long
put options at a strike price of 2.1046, short put options with a strike
price 2.1006, and short call options with a strike price of 2.1456.

Strategy 2: For CHF exposure, the appropriate strategy is to be long
put options at a strike price of 2.5309, short put options with a strike
price 2.5049, and short call options with a strike price of 2.5669.

questions.stem:
Based on the information provided in Exhibit 1, the domestic
currency value of Dias's foreign investments most likely:

options.A: decreased because of changes in the domestic currency value of foreign asset holdings.
options.B: increased because of changes in the domestic currency value of foreign asset holdings.
options.C: increased because of changes in the domestic currency value of UK assets but decreased because of changes in the domestic currency value of German assets.

rationales.A: The domestic currency value of the portfolio increased, and the domestic currency value of UK and German investments increased.
rationales.B: The domestic currency value of Dias's portfolio of foreign assets most likely increased because of changes in the domestic currency value of foreign asset holdings. The domestic currency return of the portfolio of foreign assets is:    > R~DC~ = w~1~(1 + R~FC~~,GBP~)(1 + R~FX~~,GBP~) + w~2~(1 > + R~FC~~,EUR~)(1 + R~FX~~,EUR~) -- 1 > > > >  = 0.659(86,000,000/83,400,000)(4.1025/3.8729) + > > 0.341(51,000,000/55,000,000)(3.5142/3.0359) > >  = 0.659(1.0312)(1.0592) + 0.341(0.9273)(1.1575) > >  = 0.659(1.0923) + 0.341(1.0734) > >  = 0.0858  The calculations show that the domestic currency value of the portfolio of foreign assets increased because of changes (i.e., increases) in the domestic currency value of UK and German equity investments. Note:  > w~1~ = 322,999,860/489,974,360 = 0.659  and  > w~2~ = 166,974,500/489,974,360 = 0.341
rationales.C: The domestic currency value of the portfolio increased because of increases in the domestic currency value of UK and German investments. It is the foreign currency value of German equity investments that declined.

questions.stem:
In her response regarding hedging foreign currency exposure in
Dias's portfolio, Traldi is most likely:

options.A: correct about the correlations and the currency overlay program.
options.B: incorrect about the correlations and the currency overlay program.
options.C: incorrect about the correlations, but correct about the currency overlay program.

rationales.A: Traldi is incorrect with regard to correlations and the currency overlay program
rationales.B: Traldi is incorrect about the correlations and the currency overlay program. In the short run, if the correlation between foreign currency asset returns and foreign currency returns is negative, then there may be no need to hedge all foreign currency exposure because some currency exposure is desirable from a portfolio diversification perspective. Regarding the currency overlay program, it will add value to the portfolio only if the currency alpha has a low correlation with other asset classes in the portfolio (i.e., Brazilian equities and corporate bonds).
rationales.C: Traldi is incorrect with regard to correlations and the currency overlay program.

questions.stem:
Based on the information in Exhibit 2, it would be *best *for
Sabanai to implement a carry trade for BC by borrowing in:

rationales.A: An appropriate active currency management strategy that may add value to BC's portfolios would be to borrow in USD and invest in GBP. The spread in yields is widest between the United Kingdom and the United States, and the USD/GBP currency pair has the lowest implied volatility, which is better for a carry trade.
rationales.B: UK interest rates are the highest, so the GBP would be an appropriate currency to invest, not to borrow. US interest rates are the lowest, so the USD would be an appropriate funding currency, not an investment currency.
rationales.C: The Eurozone has higher one-year yield levels compared to the US, so this would not be the appropriate funding currency. Furthermore, one-year implied volatility for the Eurozone is the highest, which does not benefit a carry trade.

questions.stem:
In regard to using USD/EUR options, Peixaria is *least likely* to
recommend a strategy to go:

options.A: long an equal number of 25-delta puts and calls.
options.B: long an equal number of 50-delta puts and calls.
options.C: short an equal number of 15-delta puts and calls.

rationales.A: A long 25-delta strangle is appropriate if you expected increased volatility. This strategy is a cheaper than the 50-delta strangle because 50-delta calls and puts are at-the-money options and are more expensive than out-of-the-money options such as 25-delta calls and puts (a 25-delta strangle).
rationales.B: A long 50-delta strangle (long 50-delta calls and puts) would be appropriate if you expected high volatility.
rationales.C: A short strangle (short an equal number of 15-delta calls and puts) would only be appropriate if volatility is expected to be low. The expectation is for increased volatility, so the long strangles would be more appropriate. A strategy of taking long positions on an equal number of 50-delta calls and 50-delta puts (i.e., a 50-delta straddle) is an appropriate way to take advantage of expected increased volatility in the USD/EUR currency pair. However, 50-delta calls and puts are at-the-money options and are more expensive than out-of-the-money options, such as 25-delta calls and puts (a 25-delta strangle).

questions.stem:
Based on the information provided in Exhibit 3,
the most appropriate risk neutral strategy is for FB to:

options.A: over-hedge AUD and not hedge CHF.
options.C: under-hedge CHF and not hedge AUD.

rationales.A: Because of equity investments in Australia and Switzerland, FB has long currency exposure to AUD and CHF. The appropriate risk-neutral strategy is to over-hedge (hedge ratio > 1) AUD and not hedge CHF. The AUD is selling at a forward premium of 2.27%, which means that the expected roll yield for a short hedge in AUD is 2.27%. Furthermore, the AUD is expected to depreciate by 3.28%, which means the short position in the AUD gains 3.28%. Thus, a short hedge of the AUD is appropriate. The CHF is at a forward discount of 2.64%, which means that the expected roll yield for a short hedge of CHF is --2.64%. The CHF is expected to appreciate 1.32%, which means that a short position in CHF would lose 1.32%. Thus, in this instance it would not be appropriate to hedge the CHF.  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Currency   Current   Six-Month   Six-Month   Forward            Spot Pair       Spot Rate Forward       Forecast Spot Premium/Discount   Appreciation/Depreciation Rate        Rate ------------ ----------- ------------- ------------- -------------------- ----------------------------- BRL/AUD      2.1046      2.1523        2.0355        2.27%                --3.28%  BRL/CHF      2.5309      2.4641        2.5642        --2.64%              1.32% -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
rationales.B: The appropriate risk-neutral strategy is to over-hedge (hedge ratio > 1) AUD and not hedge CHF.
rationales.C: The appropriate risk-neutral strategy is to over-hedge (hedge ratio > 1) AUD and not hedge CHF.

questions.stem:
Is Campos *most likely *correct that Strategy 1 and Strategy 2 will
accomplish the goals of exploiting market views and reducing hedging
costs?

options.B: No, she is incorrect about reducing hedging costs.
options.C: No, she is incorrect about exploiting market views.

rationales.A: It is true that both strategies help reduce hedging costs through premiums collected on short call and put, but they both do not accommodate the market view on the currencies.
rationales.B: It is true that both strategies help reduce hedging costs through premiums collected on short call and put.
rationales.C: Campos suggests that both strategies help reduce hedging costs and allow the manager to exploit a market view. While it is true that both strategies help reduce hedging costs through premiums collected on short calls and puts, they both do not exploit the market view on the currencies, specifically, Strategy 1 does not. Exhibit 3 indicates that the expectation is for the AUD to depreciate to BRL/AUD 2.0355 and for the CHF to appreciate to BRL/CHF 2.5642. Strategy 1, the short seagull on the AUD, only provides downside protection to BRL/AUD 2.1006 (when the short put kicks in and neutralizes the hedge), not BRL/AUD 2.0355. Under Strategy 2, the expectation is for an appreciation to BRL/CHF 2.5642; here the option premium is pocketed and because the option is written with a strike of BRL/CHF 2.5669, it will expire worthless if the rate never gets to BRL/CHF 2.5669.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Traldi's statement about currency overlay programs.

rationales.model_answer:
===
CORRECT ASPECTS:
• When correlation between foreign asset returns and foreign currency returns is NEGATIVE, currency depreciation partially offsets asset gains → REDUCES the need for hedging in the short run. Actually WAIT — reread.
• Traldi says: 'if correlation is negative, then there MAY be a need to hedge all foreign currency exposure' — this is BACKWARDS. Negative correlation reduces need to hedge (natural hedging occurs).
• Positive correlation increases need to hedge (currency and asset move together, amplifying downside).
• Traldi is WRONG on the hedging direction.

===
CURRENCY OVERLAY VALUE-ADD CONDITION:
• Traldi says: 'currency overlay will only be successful if currency alpha has a HIGH correlation with Brazilian equities and corporate bonds' — this is WRONG.
• Currency alpha is BEST when it has LOW correlation with existing portfolio — diversification benefit.
• High correlation would mean adding currency alpha adds no diversification.

PARTIAL: 5=both errors identified with correct reasoning; 3=one error; 0=agrees.

questions.stem: Using Exhibit 1, CALCULATE the currency contribution to Dias's total return (BRL basis) for the UK and German equity positions over 2013.

rationales.model_answer:
===
UK POSITION:
Value: GBP 83,400,000 → 86,000,000 (Return in GBP = +3.12%).
Exchange rate BRL/GBP: 3.8729 → 4.1025 (BRL strengthened relative to GBP... wait, BRL/GBP RISING means more BRL per GBP = GBP STRENGTHENED).
% change in BRL/GBP = (4.1025 − 3.8729) / 3.8729 = 5.93%.

Total BRL return = (1 + 0.0312)(1 + 0.0593) − 1 = 1.0924 − 1 = 9.24%.
Currency contribution = 5.93% + 0.0312 × 0.0593 = ~5.93% (mostly).
Roughly: Currency contribution ≈ 5.93% × (1.0312) = 6.11%.

===
GERMAN POSITION:
Value: EUR 55,000,000 → 51,000,000 (Return in EUR = -7.27%).
Exchange rate BRL/EUR: 3.0359 → 3.5142 (EUR strengthened).
% change in BRL/EUR = (3.5142 − 3.0359) / 3.0359 = 15.76%.

Total BRL return = (1 − 0.0727)(1 + 0.1576) − 1 = 0.9273 × 1.1576 − 1 = 7.35%.
Currency contribution ≈ 15.76% × (1 − 0.0727) = 14.62%.

===
SUMMARY:
• UK: 5.93% currency contribution.
• Germany: 15.76% currency contribution.
• Both currencies strengthened significantly against BRL, benefiting Dias.

PARTIAL: 5=both currency contributions + method; 4=method + arithmetic minor; 3=one currency; 0=wrong.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Peixaria's recommendation for BC (options-based strategy on USD/EUR expecting VOLATILITY increase). IDENTIFY the appropriate strategy.

rationales.model_answer:
APPROPRIATE STRATEGY: LONG STRADDLE or LONG STRANGLE on USD/EUR.

Accepted reasoning:
• Peixaria expects VOLATILITY (not direction) to increase → volatility trade.
• LONG STRADDLE: Buy ATM call + ATM put on USD/EUR — profits from any large move or IV increase.
• LONG STRANGLE: Similar but cheaper — buy OTM call + OTM put.

IMPLICIT CHECK — is EUR/USD IV overpriced or underpriced?
• Exhibit 2 shows USD/EUR IV = 9.50%, higher than USD/GBP 5.50% and GBP/EUR 7.50%.
• Somewhat elevated already — a further rise is Peixaria's forecast.

REJECTED ALTERNATIVES:
• Long forward: takes directional bet, not vol.
• Short strangle: WRONG DIRECTION — profits from LOWER vol.
• Straddle better than strangle if strong directional break is likely.

PARTIAL: 5=long straddle/strangle + reasoning; 3=identifies long vol; 0=wrong direction.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Campos's proposed Strategy 1 and Strategy 2 for hedging FB's AUD and CHF exposures using options.

rationales.model_answer:
STRATEGIES ARE SEAGULL SPREADS (three-legged option strategies).

===
STRATEGY 1 for AUD (long put 2.1046, short put 2.1006, short call 2.1456):
STRUCTURE: LONG PUT SPREAD (down to 2.1006) + SHORT CALL (above 2.1456).
• Protects against BRL/AUD falling below 2.1046 (AUD weakening) DOWN to 2.1006.
• Financed by selling upside above 2.1456 (giving up gains if AUD strengthens).
• Reduces cost of pure long put via TWO short legs.
• Appropriate hedge with directional view (AUD may weaken but not sharply).

===
STRATEGY 2 for CHF (long put 2.5309, short put 2.5049, short call 2.5669):
STRUCTURE: SAME SEAGULL SPREAD structure.
• Protects BRL/CHF between 2.5049 and 2.5309.
• Cost financed by short call above 2.5669.

===
APPROPRIATENESS:
• Both strategies are LEGITIMATE seagull spreads that reduce cost vs pure put.
• Trade off: LIMITED downside protection (only to lower put strike) AND GIVEN UP UPSIDE (short call).
• Appropriate if directional view is moderate.
• Effective when investor believes currency won't move drastically in either direction.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies seagull structure + evaluates both; 3=structure identified; 0=misunderstands.

cases.title: Bristol Advisors (Options Pathway)
cases.vignette:
Bristol Advisors is a $600 million private wealth management firm. Portfolio
manager Ethan Colenso is meeting with client Diana Ashcroft to discuss hedging
strategies for her concentrated position in KEND Corporation, a mid-cap
industrial company. Ashcroft holds 500,000 shares of KEND acquired through a
family inheritance, valued at $28 million (KEND currently trades at $56.00
per share). The concentrated position represents 42% of Ashcroft's total
investable wealth.

Ashcroft's objectives:
- Reduce concentration risk without triggering an immediate taxable sale.
- Maintain some upside participation in KEND.
- Generate income where possible to fund current lifestyle expenses.
- Willingness to accept a maximum loss on the KEND position of ~15% ($8.40/share)
  over the next 12 months.

Current option data for KEND:

Exhibit 1: Option Data (KEND at $56.00, 12 months to expiration)

Strike    Call Premium    Put Premium    Call Delta    Put Delta
$45       $12.80          $1.20          0.89          -0.11
$50       $8.50           $2.20          0.75          -0.25
$55       $5.20           $4.30          0.55          -0.45
$60       $2.90           $7.20          0.35          -0.65
$65       $1.50           $10.50         0.20          -0.80

Colenso presents three potential strategies:

Strategy 1: Sell 5,000 call contracts at strike $60 (out-of-the-money covered
call) — generates $2.90 × 500,000 = $1.45 million premium income; caps upside
at $60 per share.

Strategy 2: Buy 5,000 put contracts at strike $50 (protective put) — costs
$2.20 × 500,000 = $1.10 million premium; provides downside protection below $50.

Strategy 3: Zero-cost collar — buy 5,000 put contracts at strike $50 and sell
5,000 call contracts at strike $60. Net premium: -$2.20 + $2.90 = +$0.70 per
share credit, generating $350,000 income.

Ashcroft mentions: "I'm also considering the tax implications. My cost basis
in KEND is $32 per share, so I have significant embedded gains. I'd prefer
strategies that don't trigger constructive sale rules under US tax law."

Colenso responds: "Constructive sale rules under IRC Section 1259 apply to
transactions that eliminate substantially all risk of loss and opportunity
for gain. A collar with strikes not too far from current price could
potentially trigger constructive sale treatment, so we need to structure
carefully. Deep in-the-money puts combined with tight OTM calls raise the
highest concern."

questions.stem: EVALUATE Strategy 3 (zero-cost collar at $50 put / $60 call) against Ashcroft's stated objectives + JUSTIFY whether it meets her requirements.

rationales.model_answer:
===
OBJECTIVE 1 (reduce concentration risk without immediate sale): MET.
• Collar limits downside without triggering an actual sale.
• Effective downside protection below $50 (10% loss from $56).

===
OBJECTIVE 2 (maintain upside participation): PARTIALLY MET.
• Upside capped at $60 (7% gain from $56).
• Reasonable participation for 12-month horizon.

===
OBJECTIVE 3 (generate income): MET.
• Net credit of $0.70/share = $350k income.
• Meaningful income for lifestyle needs.

===
OBJECTIVE 4 (max loss ~15% or $8.40/share): NEARLY MET.
• Downside limited to $50 (-$6/share = -10.7% loss).
• Better than Ashcroft's tolerance.

===
CONSTRUCTIVE SALE CONCERN:
• Put at $50 and call at $60: strike range of $10 vs current $56 = 17.9% band.
• Not overly narrow — likely SAFE from constructive sale (typically triggered at ~5-10% bands with strikes close to current price).
• Reasonable protection, income, participation, and tax-safety balance.

===
VERDICT: Strategy 3 IS APPROPRIATE.

PARTIAL: 5=all four objectives + constructive sale evaluated; 3=three items; 0=one-sided.

questions.stem: COMPARE Strategy 2 (protective put) vs Strategy 1 (covered call) for a client focused on downside protection. IDENTIFY key differences in risk/reward.

rationales.model_answer:
===
PROTECTIVE PUT (Strategy 2):
• BOUGHT 5000 put contracts at $50, cost $1.10M.
• Below $50: put payoff offsets stock decline (near dollar-for-dollar).
• Above $50: full upside participation (unlimited).
• Downside: floor is (stock loss to $50) + premium = 10.7% + 3.9% = 14.6% max loss.
• Upside: no cap, but reduced by premium paid.

===
COVERED CALL (Strategy 1):
• SOLD 5000 call contracts at $60, receive $1.45M premium.
• Above $60: called away at $60 (max profit = $4 + $2.90 = $6.90/share).
• Below $60: keep stock + premium.
• Downside: no protection — stock can go to $0.
• Upside: capped at $60 + $2.90 premium.

===
KEY DIFFERENCES:
• PROTECTIVE PUT provides DOWNSIDE PROTECTION but costs money.
• COVERED CALL provides INCOME but no downside protection.
• For DOWNSIDE FOCUS: Protective Put clearly better.
• For INCOME FOCUS: Covered Call better.
• For BOTH: Collar (Strategy 3) combines features.

PARTIAL: 5=full comparison + protective put chosen for downside focus; 3=direction; 0=confused.

questions.stem: CALCULATE the payoff on the collar (Strategy 3) at KEND prices of $40, $50, $56, $60, $70 at expiration. Compare to the unhedged stock position.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — full payoff table:

COLLAR: LONG 5000 puts @ $50 + SHORT 5000 calls @ $60 + LONG 500,000 shares.
Net premium received: $0.70/share × 500,000 = $350,000.

===
AT $40 (unhedged stock loss $8M):
• Stock: 500k × ($40-$56) = -$8,000,000.
• Put payoff: 500k × ($50-$40) = +$5,000,000.
• Call payoff: 0 (out-of-the-money).
• Net premium: +$350,000.
• TOTAL COLLAR P&L: -$2,650,000 (-9.5% on $28M position).

===
AT $50 (breakeven of put):
• Stock: -$3,000,000.
• Options: 0 payoff both sides.
• Net premium: +$350,000.
• TOTAL: -$2,650,000 (-9.5%).

===
AT $56 (current):
• Stock: 0.
• Options: 0 payoff both sides.
• Net premium: +$350,000.
• TOTAL: +$350,000 (+1.25%).

===
AT $60 (call strike):
• Stock: +$2,000,000.
• Options: 0 payoff both sides.
• Net premium: +$350,000.
• TOTAL: +$2,350,000 (+8.4%).

===
AT $70:
• Stock: +$7,000,000.
• Call cost: -500k × ($70-$60) = -$5,000,000.
• Net premium: +$350,000.
• TOTAL: +$2,350,000 (+8.4% — capped).

===
UNHEDGED COMPARISON:
• Downside unlimited; upside unlimited.
• Collar: floor -9.5%, cap +8.4%.

PARTIAL: 5=all 5 prices + comparison; 4=four correct; 3=three; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two additional tax considerations Ashcroft should consider with the collar strategy beyond constructive sale rules.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
• HOLDING PERIOD SUSPENSION: entering a collar SUSPENDS the holding period on the underlying stock during the hedge — can convert long-term to short-term gains.
• STRADDLE RULES: IRC Section 1092 straddle rules can defer losses on option positions until the offsetting position is closed.
• WASH SALE RULES: closing a collar and reopening could trigger wash sale.
• MARK-TO-MARKET (Section 1256): some options and index options are marked to market for tax purposes, creating different treatment than equity options.
• CAPITAL GAINS on OPTIONS: closing puts and calls generates capital gains/losses that must be reported.
• GIFT/ESTATE PLANNING: could combine collar with charitable trust to defer/eliminate embedded gains.
• EXCHANGE FUND: alternative structure that pools diversification exchanges tax-free.
• PREPAID VARIABLE FORWARD: alternative to collar for concentrated positions.
• STATE TAX: state tax treatment may differ from federal.
• AMTs and TAX RATES: consider AMT impact on collar cash flows.
• DOCUMENTATION: proper documentation to support tax positions.

PARTIAL: 5=two considerations + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Aventine Pension (Swaps/Futures Pathway)
cases.vignette:
Aventine Corporation's defined benefit pension plan has $3.2 billion in
assets. CIO Ronan Fitzsimmons is meeting with fixed-income overlay specialist
Beatrix Kaminski to discuss enhancements to the plan's LDI (liability-driven
investing) strategy.

Current plan characteristics:
- Plan assets: $3.2 billion; plan liabilities (PBO): $3.6 billion (funded status 88.9%).
- Fixed-income allocation: 55% ($1.76B).
- Fixed-income modified duration: 8.5 years; PV01: $1,496,000.
- Liability modified duration: 15.2 years; PV01: $5,472,000.
- Duration gap: liability PV01 - asset PV01 = $3,976,000 (assets under-duration).

Fitzsimmons wants to close 60% of the current duration gap ($2,385,600 of PV01)
using derivative overlays. Kaminski recommends using Treasury futures and
interest rate swaps:

Option A: Treasury bond futures (long)
- Contract price: 148-16 (=$148,500 per $100,000 face).
- Contract PV01: $105 per contract.
- Cheapest-to-deliver bond has PV01 of $110 per $100 face; conversion factor 0.90.
- Effective PV01 for hedging: $105 (contract PV01).

Option B: Receive-fixed 10-year interest rate swap
- Notional required for $1M PV01: approximately $110M.
- Receive-fixed increases plan asset PV01.
- Requires ISDA agreement and initial margin.

Kaminski also discusses tactical opportunities:

Situation 1: Fitzsimmons believes the US yield curve will steepen over the
next 6 months as the Fed begins cutting short-term rates while long rates
remain anchored by inflation concerns.

Situation 2: The plan has $150 million invested in Japanese Government Bonds
(JGBs) held for interest rate diversification. Fitzsimmons is concerned that
the Bank of Japan may reduce its yield curve control, causing JGB yields to
rise sharply.

Situation 3: The plan holds $200 million in emerging markets equity exposure
via ETF but does not hedge the associated currency exposure. Kaminski suggests
implementing a systematic currency hedge using forward contracts.

Kaminski makes the following claim: "For closing the LDI gap, receive-fixed
swaps are superior to long Treasury futures because swaps provide more
precise duration matching, avoid basis risk from CTD bond selection, and
require no rolling every quarter. However, futures require less operational
setup and no counterparty credit risk exposure."

questions.stem: CALCULATE the number of Treasury futures contracts required to close 60% of Aventine's duration gap. Show work.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — full calculation:

GAP TO CLOSE (60%):
• Full duration gap: $3,976,000 (liability PV01 - asset PV01).
• 60% target: $3,976,000 × 0.60 = $2,385,600 PV01 to add.

===
TREASURY FUTURES CONTRACTS NEEDED:
• Contract PV01: $105.
• Number of contracts = Required PV01 / Contract PV01.
• = $2,385,600 / $105
• = 22,720 contracts LONG.

===
ADJUSTMENT for CTD BASIS:
• If using CTD-adjusted BPV: Contract PV01 = ($110 / 0.90) × contract face size effect.
• Alternative BPV = $110 / 0.9 = $122.22 per $100 face.
• Per contract ($100,000 face): $122.22 (or use $105 direct contract BPV).

===
RESULT: 22,720 contracts LONG.

Accepted range: 22,000-23,000 with reasonable rounding.

PARTIAL: 5=~22,720 LONG + work; 4=direction correct + arithmetic minor; 3=direction correct; 0=SHORT or wrong.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Kaminski's claim that receive-fixed swaps are SUPERIOR to Treasury futures for closing the LDI gap.

rationales.model_answer:
===
CLAIMS FOR SWAPS:
• More precise duration matching: TRUE — swaps can be constructed for exact duration/curve exposures.
• Avoid basis risk from CTD bond selection: TRUE — swaps price off swap curve, not government bond curve; CTD basis risk avoided.
• Require no rolling every quarter: TRUE — swaps have single maturity; futures must be rolled quarterly.

===
CLAIMS FOR FUTURES:
• Less operational setup: TRUE — no ISDA agreement, no initial margin negotiation.
• No counterparty credit risk: TRUE for exchange-cleared futures (via CCP); FALSE for OTC bilateral (not applicable here).

===
MISSING CONSIDERATIONS Kaminski overlooked:
• SWAP CURVE BASIS RISK: liability discount curve is corporate/AA; swap curve is different — creates a basis risk not present in Treasury futures (which have Treasury basis risk).
• LIQUIDITY: Treasury futures are highly liquid (deep, transparent markets); swap markets are OTC-driven with counterparty-specific pricing.
• REGULATORY: Dodd-Frank clearing requirements for swaps.
• MARGIN: swaps require ongoing variation margin plus IM.
• ACCOUNTING: hedge accounting treatment may differ.

===
CONCLUSION: Kaminski's claim is MOSTLY VALID for pension LDI but overstates case. Best practice often uses BOTH.

PARTIAL: 5=validates claims + identifies missing considerations; 3=validates; 0=uncritical.

questions.stem: For Situation 1 (expected yield curve steepening), DESCRIBE an appropriate derivative overlay strategy to profit from the view WITHOUT compromising the LDI hedge.

rationales.model_answer:
STRATEGY: STEEPENER using different-maturity derivative positions.

===
APPROACH 1: FUTURES CURVE TRADE.
• LONG 2-year Treasury note futures (short duration).
• SHORT 10-year (or 30-year) Treasury futures.
• Profits if 2-10 or 2-30 spread widens (curve steepens).
• DV01-neutral: size positions so they have equal but opposite duration impact.

===
APPROACH 2: SWAP SPREAD TRADE.
• RECEIVE-FIXED on 2-year swap.
• PAY-FIXED on 10-year swap.
• Profits from same steepening view.

===
LDI CONSIDERATIONS:
• Must be OVERLAY on top of existing LDI hedge — not substituting for it.
• Sizing must not disrupt the primary duration match.
• Should be sized modestly (e.g., 5-10% of total PV01 exposure).
• Requires monitoring — tactical position, not permanent.

===
RISKS:
• If curve FLATTENS instead, losses on both legs.
• Basis risk between swap and Treasury steepeners.
• Correlated to macroeconomic developments not always predictable.

PARTIAL: 5=steepener strategy + LDI-preserving sizing; 3=strategy identified; 1=vague; 0=wrong direction.

questions.stem: For Situation 3 (unhedged EM equity currency exposure via ETF), DISCUSS the appropriate hedging approach + potential drawbacks.

rationales.model_answer:
===
APPROACH: STRATEGIC CURRENCY HEDGE via ROLLING FORWARDS.
• Estimate underlying currency exposure from ETF holdings (typically weighted average of EM basket currencies).
• Sell forward each currency against USD to hedge exposure.
• Roll forwards quarterly (typical) as they approach maturity.
• Rebalance as ETF composition changes and currency movements alter exposures.

===
SIZING:
• Full hedge (100%) or partial hedge (typically 50-75%) depending on plan's views on EM currency premium.
• EM currencies have historical positive expected returns from carry — full hedging forfeits this.

===
DRAWBACKS/CHALLENGES:
• MULTIPLE EM CURRENCIES: not all hedgeable via liquid forwards (INR, CNY often require NDFs).
• BASKET CHANGES: ETF composition changes force hedge adjustments.
• TRANSACTION COSTS: rolling multiple forward positions has meaningful cost drag.
• REBALANCING: hedge ratio drifts as currency and asset returns move.
• BASIS RISK: proxy hedging using G10 currencies (e.g., AUD proxy for EM Asia).
• CURRENCY CARRY LOSS: EM currencies typically have positive carry (higher rates); hedging forfeits this premium.
• EMERGING MARKET EQUITY RETURNS: often correlated positively with currency returns — natural hedge partially reduces the need for full explicit hedging.

PARTIAL: 5=hedging approach + drawbacks; 3=one aspect; 0=vague.

cases.title: Maravella Global (Currency Pathway)
cases.vignette:
Maravella Global Advisors manages $2.1 billion in multi-currency portfolios
for institutional clients. Portfolio manager Sabine Voort is meeting with
consultant Rafael Duarte to discuss currency management strategy for the
Continental Investment Trust, a €400 million European institutional client
with a global equity mandate.

Continental's portfolio allocation and current exposures:

Exhibit 1: Continental Investment Trust Global Equity Portfolio

Region              Weight   Local Currency
Eurozone            35%      EUR (base)
United States       28%      USD
United Kingdom      12%      GBP
Japan               10%      JPY
Emerging Markets    10%      Various
Switzerland         5%       CHF

Continental's current hedging policy:
- USD exposure: 50% hedged via 3-month forwards, rolled quarterly.
- GBP exposure: 100% hedged.
- JPY exposure: unhedged.
- Emerging markets: unhedged.
- CHF exposure: unhedged.

Duarte reviews current market rates and provides analysis:

Exhibit 2: Currency Market Data

Currency Pair    Spot Rate     6-Month Forward    Forward Points    1-Year IV
EUR/USD          1.0450        1.0510             +60 pips          8.5%
EUR/GBP          0.8620        0.8595             -25 pips          6.2%
EUR/JPY          160.20        157.80             -240 pips         9.8%
EUR/CHF          0.9420        0.9385             -35 pips          5.5%

Voort proposes several strategy modifications:

Modification 1: "Given the positive USD/EUR forward premium (USD trading at
premium to EUR in forwards), we should consider reducing our USD hedge from
50% to 25% — we're paying a cost to eliminate an exposure that has favorable
carry."

Modification 2: "For JPY exposure, given the sharp forward discount (JPY
trading at premium to EUR in forwards, meaning we would receive positive
carry from being long JPY forward), we should consider a 50% hedge to
protect against sudden JPY weakness while maintaining some carry benefit."

Modification 3: "Rather than roll 3-month forwards for USD, we should shift
to 6-month forwards to reduce transaction costs. The additional term risk
is minimal for a large institutional portfolio."

Duarte adds: "For the Emerging Markets 10% allocation, we should consider
using proxy hedging via more-liquid G10 currencies. For example, hedging
Asian EM exposure using JPY or AUD forwards, and Latin American exposure
using USD forwards for carry-adjustment."

Voort also expresses concern: "Continental's investment committee has asked
about the impact of currency correlations on total portfolio risk. Our
regression analysis shows USD returns and eurozone equity returns have -0.35
correlation over the past 10 years — currency losses tend to offset equity
gains."

questions.stem: EVALUATE Voort's Modification 1 (reduce USD hedge from 50% to 25% based on forward premium).

rationales.model_answer:
===
ANALYSIS:
• EUR/USD spot 1.0450, 6-month forward 1.0510 → forward EUR premium (USD discount at 6-month vs spot).
• 60 pip forward premium on EUR/USD annualized: (0.0060 / 1.0450) × 2 ≈ 1.15%.
• Hedging USD (sell USD forward) means Continental locks in this forward premium.
• Voort's claim: forward premium = 'cost' of hedging — needs unpacking.

===
HEDGING COST CALCULATION:
• If USD forward trading at premium relative to spot, hedger PAYS this premium.
• The 1.15% annualized 'cost' offsets ~1.15% of USD interest rate differential.
• Under CIP (Covered Interest Parity), forward premium ≈ interest rate differential.
• Reducing hedge exposes Continental to CURRENCY RISK to save the 1.15% cost.

===
EVALUATION: MISGUIDED.
• The 'cost' is essentially a REBALANCING of the fund's exposure to interest rate differentials.
• Reducing the hedge INCREASES currency volatility exposure.
• A better rationale would be based on CURRENCY OUTLOOK, not the mechanical forward premium.
• Modification 1 is a poor argument for reducing hedges.

===
BETTER APPROACH: base hedge ratios on volatility contribution and correlations.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies cost issue + evaluates correctly; 3=one aspect; 0=agrees.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Voort's Modification 2 (add 50% JPY hedge based on forward discount).

rationales.model_answer:
===
ANALYSIS:
• EUR/JPY spot 160.20, 6-month forward 157.80 → JPY forward premium (EUR discount).
• Long JPY forward: buy JPY at 6-month forward rate; receive positive carry as JPY strengthens forward.

===
CURRENT POSITION: unhedged JPY exposure means Continental IS long JPY spot (from Japanese equity investment).

===
MODIFICATION 2 (50% hedge):
• Adding hedge means SELLING JPY forward.
• Since JPY is trading at PREMIUM to EUR in forwards, selling JPY forward means Continental would receive the forward rate (higher JPY in EUR terms).
• Actually: 6-month forward 157.80 EUR/JPY (less JPY per EUR) = JPY has appreciated in forwards.
• Selling JPY forward means Continental locks in current JPY value (protecting against JPY weakening).

===
RATIONALE ASSESSMENT:
• 'protect against sudden JPY weakness while maintaining some carry benefit' — makes sense: 50% hedge preserves half of carry gain while providing partial downside protection.
• JPY historically volatile and susceptible to sudden BOJ policy shifts.
• Modification 2 IS APPROPRIATE.

PARTIAL: 5=analysis + supports Modification 2; 3=supports; 0=misunderstands.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Modification 3 (shift USD hedge from 3-month to 6-month forwards).

rationales.model_answer:
===
COST SAVINGS: LEGITIMATE.
• 6-month rolls are HALF the frequency of 3-month rolls.
• Roll costs (transaction fees, bid-ask spreads) reduced by ~50%.
• Meaningful for large institutional portfolios.

===
ADDITIONAL RISK CONSIDERATIONS:
• LONGER TERM = MORE EXPOSURE TO CURRENCY MOVEMENTS between rolls.
• If USD strengthens more than expected during 6-month window, 3-month hedges would be adjusted more nimbly.
• FORWARD RATES beyond 3 months may be less liquid, wider bid-ask spreads.
• If Continental's PORTFOLIO WEIGHTS CHANGE, 3-month rolls allow more precise hedge adjustments.
• CARRY DIFFERENCE: 6-month forward premium/discount differs from 3-month; hedging cost basis changes.
• MARK-TO-MARKET fluctuations more volatile with 6-month positions.

===
CONCLUSION: TRADEOFF.
• Cost savings legitimate; not 'minimal' additional risk as Voort claims.
• For a large institutional portfolio with stable allocation, 6-month may be reasonable.
• Consider: 4-month rolls (intermediate step) or MONTHLY re-analysis of hedge.
• Modification 3 is REASONABLE with awareness of tradeoffs.

PARTIAL: 5=cost savings + additional risks identified; 3=one aspect; 0=uncritical.

questions.stem: For the -0.35 correlation between USD returns and eurozone equity returns, DISCUSS the implication for USD hedging strategy AND for total portfolio risk.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY:

===
IMPLICATION FOR USD HEDGING:
• Negative correlation means when USD strengthens (Continental gains on USD exposure), eurozone equity typically declines.
• Currency gain PARTIALLY OFFSETS equity loss (natural hedge).
• Therefore, FULL hedging of USD would REMOVE this natural offset.
• Optimal hedge ratio is LESS THAN 100% given negative correlation.

===
OPTIMAL HEDGE RATIO (from theory):
• Portfolio variance minimizing hedge = 1 + (Correlation × σ_asset / σ_currency).
• With ρ = -0.35, hedge ratio < 100% is variance-reducing.
• Approximate: 50-75% hedge often optimal in these cases.

===
IMPLICATION FOR TOTAL PORTFOLIO RISK:
• Currency losses partly compensate equity losses → total volatility lower than sum of asset + currency variances.
• Diversification benefit from negative currency-equity correlation.
• Removing this via full hedge could INCREASE portfolio volatility.

===
CONCLUSION:
• Current 50% USD hedge is CONSISTENT with correlation analysis.
• Voort's Modification 1 (reduce to 25%) would preserve more diversification benefit, but is largely driven by correlation, not by forward premium as claimed.
• Correlation is UNSTABLE over time — monitor and adjust.

PARTIAL: 5=hedge ratio + portfolio risk implications; 3=one aspect; 0=misses point.

cases.title: Athena Investment Services Case Scenario
cases.vignette:
Caitlyn Wilson, CFA, recently started her own asset management company,
Athena Investment Services. The board of directors of Athena adopted
both the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Practice (Code
and Standards) and the CFA Institute Asset Manager Code of Professional
Conduct (Asset Manager Code) to institutionalize ethical behavior within
the firm. The board also implemented half-yearly staff performance
reviews, including an assessment of each manager's ability to ensure
their department's compliance with the both the Code and Standards and
the Asset Manager Code.

Six months into the first financial year, Wilson meets with all of the
managers to assess each department's compliance. Wilson asks the
compliance officer, Mark Zefferman, CFA, to make an opening statement to
set the right tone for the meeting. Zefferman states,

"At a minimum, we are responsible for implementing procedures addressing
the general principles embedded in the six components of the Asset
Manager Code. As stated below, we must:

Statement 1: Act with skill, competence, and diligence while
exhibiting independence and objectivity when giving investment advice,

Statement 2: Put our clients' interests above the firm's when
appropriate and act in a professional and ethical manner at all times,
and

Statement 3: Communicate with our clients in a timely and
non-misleading manner and obey all rules governing capital markets."

Zefferman adds,

"With regard to the last statement, please be aware that we must
implement the new anti-money-laundering regulations introduced by our
local regulator, effective the first quarter of next year. I have
analyzed the new regulations and have found that all of the local
requirements are part of regulations recently introduced in Europe,
where only a few of our clients reside. When we start taking on new
clients based in Singapore in the second half of next year, we will also
need to follow that country's anti-money-laundering regulations. The
local anti-money-laundering legislation appears to be embedded in the
Singapore regulations as well."

Wilson continues, "I would like each of you to explain how the
implementation of the Asset Manager Code within your department is being
supervised. Let us start with Shenal Mehta, our client service manager."

Mehta states,

"With respect to the Asset Manager Code relating to client services, we
have ensured that we enforce the following policies: All disclosures are
accurate and complete, and our calculations are shown, no matter how
complicated. We also ensure that the client sees some sort of
communication from us when they request it and that the marketing
material sent to clients is checked by the compliance department for
accuracy and completeness."

Anders Peterson, CFA, chief investment officer, states,

"In addition to what Mehta has said, I have the following comments:

Comment 1: On occasion, we are able to acquire securities we expect
will be particularly strong performers, such as oversubscribed initial
public offerings. In order to ensure that all clients are treated
fairly, each client portfolio is given the same number of shares.

Comment 2: Any communication with clients is kept confidential and
is only accessible by authorized personnel.

Comment 3: A gift and entertainment policy is in place to help
ensure our managers and analysts keep their independence and
objectivity."

Richard Gilchrist, head of portfolio administration, then adds, "Our
portfolio policies call for all assets to be valued at fair market
prices using third-party pricing services. When a security price is not
available from the service, a committee whose members have experience in
valuing illiquid assets uses the hierarchy dictated by Global Investment
Performance Standards (GIPS) to determine values."

Wilson concludes the meeting by mentioning that Athena must do even more
to ensure its clients continue to have faith in Athena's ability to
protect and grow their assets. She recommends they disclose their risk
management practices, which identify, measure, and manage the various
risk aspects of the business to clients and the regulator. She adds, "In
addition, we need to create a business continuity plan covering data
backup and recovery, alternate trading systems if the primary system
fails, and methods to communicate to employees, critical vendors, and
suppliers in case of an emergency that could disrupt normal business
functions."

questions.stem:
Which of the following anti-money-laundering laws must Athena
currently comply with to be consistent with the CFA Institute Standards
of Professional Conduct?

rationales.A: Incorrect. The new local anti-money-laundering regulations do not take effect until the first quarter of NEXT year, so they are not currently binding — and Standard I(A) requires compliance with the strictest CURRENTLY applicable law.
rationales.B: Correct. Athena currently has clients residing in Europe, and the European regulations are already in force and encompass all of the local requirements. Under Standard I(A) — Knowledge of the Law, members must comply with the strictest applicable law or regulation, which at present is the European anti-money-laundering regime.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Athena will not take on Singapore-based clients until the second half of next year, so the Singaporean regulations are not yet applicable — they become relevant only once that client relationship begins.

questions.stem: DETERMINE whether Peterson's Comment 1 (each client portfolio receives the same number of shares of oversubscribed IPOs) complies with the Asset Manager Code and Standard III(B) Fair Dealing. JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: DOES NOT COMPLY.

Accepted justifications (any ONE):
• Fair dealing requires PRO-RATA allocation based on ORDER SIZE (or account size), not identical share counts — equal numbers systematically favor small accounts and disadvantage large ones.
• Allocation must also reflect SUITABILITY — an oversubscribed IPO may be unsuitable for some accounts (e.g., conservative mandates), so blanket identical allocation ignores client-specific appropriateness.
• 'Fair' does not mean 'equal' under Standard III(B); it means equitable, disclosed, and consistent with each client's mandate.

PARTIAL: full=verdict + one accepted justification; half=verdict only; 0=complies.

questions.stem: Athena's local regulator introduces new anti-money-laundering rules; a few clients reside in Europe (whose regulations encompass the local rules) and Singapore clients arrive next year (Singapore regulations embed the local rules). DETERMINE which AML regulations Athena must follow, now and after Singapore onboarding. JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: Athena must comply with the STRICTEST APPLICABLE law in each relationship — Standard I(A).

Accepted answer structure:
• NOW: comply with local AML rules for all clients; for Europe-resident clients, the European rules apply and are the stricter, more comprehensive set (they encompass the local rules), so complying with the European requirements satisfies both.
• AFTER SINGAPORE ONBOARDING: for Singapore-based clients, comply with Singapore regulations, which embed the local rules — again the broader (stricter) set governs.
• PRINCIPLE (must appear for full credit): where laws conflict or overlap, members follow the MORE STRICT of applicable laws; adopting the most comprehensive regime firm-wide is acceptable best practice.

PARTIAL: full=principle + both time periods; half=principle only; 0="local only."

cases.title: Frank Litman Case Scenario
cases.vignette:
Frank Litman, CFA, was recently hired as a portfolio manager by Twain
Investments, a fairly small asset management firm. Since attending
graduate school 10 years ago, Litman has managed a limited number of
accounts belonging to friends. All of these accounts are currently too
small to meet Twain's minimum balance requirement of $5 million and
generate only modest fees for Litman. Litman disclosed the arrangement
to the human resource (HR) manager when he interviewed for his position
with Twain. The HR manager agreed that the accounts were too small and
would probably never be large enough to meet Twain's minimum size
requirement.

After accepting the position with Twain, Litman met with each of the
friends for whom he manages portfolios. He recommended they find another
financial adviser. Litman's friends argued that a different adviser
would undoubtedly charge higher fees and asked Litman to continue
managing their money as a personal favor. Following the meetings, Litman
sent separate letters to both the Twain HR manager and his friends
explaining his employment relationship and that he also manages some
small portfolios for a few of his friends.

The following month, Litman updated the promotional material that he
shares with all of his Twain clients and prospects. The material
summarizes the portfolio trading strategy Litman developed by analyzing
20 years of historical data. In his analysis, Litman determined that his
strategy of investing in large-capitalization US stocks would have
outperformed the S&P 500 Index over the last 20 years with an average
annual return of 8.91% versus 8.22% for the S&P 500. The concluding
paragraph of the brochure states, "We believe long-term use of this
trading strategy will lead to superior performance compared with the S&P
500." The brochure includes a footnote in small print stating, "Results
are gross before taxes and thus may be higher than actual results would
have been over the given period. Past performance cannot guarantee
future results."

At Twain, Litman has discretionary authority over 30 individual clients
who hold both stocks and bonds in their portfolios. His 10 largest
clients vary widely in age, occupation, and wealth. For a variety of
reasons, each of these accounts requires significant attention. The
remaining two-thirds of Litman's clients are stable, long-term
investors, all of whom are saving for retirement. Litman performs
comprehensive quarterly reviews with the owners of the 10 largest
accounts and similar annual reviews with the remaining clients.
Recently, he made an exception to this rule when he learned that one of
his smaller, less active clients had unexpectedly inherited $600,000
from an aunt's estate. Litman met with the client and performed a
comprehensive review of the client's financial situation even though
only three months had passed since their last meeting.

Twain hires a compliance officer and subsequently experiences
significant change during the following year. The compliance officer
immediately begins to update the firm's policies and procedures even
though Twain adheres to the Asset Manager Code of Professional Conduct.
In addition, after a thorough analysis, Twain senior management decides
to outsource its back-office operations and hires an independent
consultant to review client portfolio information. At the same time,
they add several research and investment staff members and upgrade the
information management system. They also eliminate paper records in
favor of electronic copies and develop a business-continuity plan based
on current staffing.

Eighteen months later, the compliance officer resigns. Rather than hire
an external replacement, management designates one of Twain's senior
portfolio managers as the new compliance officer. The compliance officer
reviews both firm and employee transactions and reports to the CEO
rather than to the board of directors.

questions.stem:
According to the CFA Institute *Standards of Practice Handbook*,
which of the following additional pieces of information would
Litman least likely be required to supply to Twain to comply with his
duty to employer? The:

options.A: names of his friends who are his clients.
options.B: amount and type of compensation received from friends.
options.C: duration of the investment management agreements with friends.

rationales.A: According to Standard IV(B), members should disclose the terms of any agreement under which a member will receive additional compensation. Terms include the nature of the compensation, the approximate amount of compensation, and the duration of the agreement. According to Standard III(E), members must keep information about current and prospective clients confidential. Client names would be considered confidential, particularly when tied to the other previously mentioned information to be given to the employer.
rationales.B: because the information is a part of the recommended procedures for compliance for Standard IV(B).
rationales.C: because the information is a part of the recommended procedures for compliance for Standard IV(B).

questions.stem:
With regard to managing portfolios for Twain as well as for his
friends, Litman should most likely undertake which of the following to
ensure compliance with the CFA Institute Standards of Professional
Conduct? He should:

options.C: obtain written consent from Twain and his friends.

rationales.A: because Litman must obtain written permission from all parties involved when conflicts of interest are present.
rationales.B: because Litman must obtain written permission from all parties involved when conflicts of interest are present.
rationales.C: According to Standard IV(B)--Additional Compensation Agreements Litman must obtain written permission from all parties involved when conflicts of interest are present.

questions.stem:
In the footnote of the promotional material about the performance of
his portfolio trading strategy, Litman is least likely in compliance
with the CFA Institute Standards of Professional Conduct with respect
to:

rationales.A: because recommended presentation procedures require disclosure indicating if fees are gross or net and if before or after taxes.
rationales.B: because recommended presentation procedures require disclosure indicating if fees are gross or net and if before or after taxes.
rationales.C: Standard III(D)--Performance Presentation allows the use of simulated performance analysis as long as it is clearly stated that the results are simulated. Litman uses historical data over 20 years, but he has only managed actual accounts for friends for 10 years. Consequently, he should have stated in the footnote that the results were simulated.

questions.stem:
Did Litman violate any CFA Institute Standards of Professional
Conduct in regard to his performance reviews for Twain clients?

options.B: Yes, with respect to the frequency of reviews for his 10 largest clients.
options.C: Yes, with respect to his recent review for the client with the inheritance.

rationales.A: Standard III(C)--Suitability requires that members make a reasonable inquiry into a client or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints prior to making any investment recommendations or taking investment action and must update this information regularly. Such an inquiry should be repeated at least annually and prior to material changes to specific investment recommendations or decisions on behalf of the client. The Code and Standards do not require clients to be treated the same.
rationales.B: because the Standards do not require clients to be treated equally.
rationales.C: because the Standards would require Litman to review his client's new circumstance to advise accordingly.

questions.stem: DETERMINE whether Litman's continued management of his friends' accounts complies with Standard IV (Duties to Employers). JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
VERDICT: DOES NOT FULLY COMPLY.

Accepted justifications (any ONE):
• Managing outside accounts FOR COMPENSATION (even modest fees) requires WRITTEN CONSENT from BOTH the employer and the outside clients — Standard IV(A)/IV(B). Litman only sent notification LETTERS; he did not obtain consent.
• Disclosure to HR at interview + letters afterward = notification, not permission. The Standard requires consent BEFORE continuing the compensated outside activity.
• The fact that the accounts are below Twain's minimum does not exempt him — the conflict is the compensated competing service, not account size.

PARTIAL: full=verdict + consent-vs-notification distinction; half=verdict + vague conflict reasoning; 0=complies.

questions.stem: Litman's brochure presents a backtested strategy (8.91% vs 8.22% for the S&P 500) with the claim 'We believe long-term use of this trading strategy will lead to superior performance' and a small-print gross-of-fees footnote. IDENTIFY the issues under Standard I(C) Misrepresentation and III(D) Performance Presentation.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO issues for full credit.

Accepted issues:
• SIMULATED RESULTS NOT LABELED: the 20-year record is a BACKTEST, not actual performance; it must be clearly identified as hypothetical/simulated, not buried.
• FEES: gross-of-fees presentation relegated to small print understates the difference from achievable client results; net-of-fees (or prominent disclosure) is required for fair presentation.
• 'We believe ... superior performance' is permissible ONLY as clearly framed opinion; combined with an unlabeled backtest it creates a misleading implication of achieved outperformance.
• SMALL PRINT: material disclosures cannot be minimized to the point of being overlooked.
NOTE: the 'past performance cannot guarantee future results' line is correct and compliant — candidates should not cite it as a violation.

PARTIAL: full=two issues; half=one issue; 0=none/wrong target.

cases.title: Ashraf Omar Case Scenario
cases.vignette:
Ashraf Omar, CFA, recently joined the Sahara Manufacturing Company
(Sahara) as its chief financial officer (CFO). The company is planning
an initial public offering (IPO). The proceeds of the IPO will be used
to finance the purchase of plant and machinery. Omar was recruited on
the basis of his extensive investment banking background, having
successfully supervised 10 IPOs over the last five years at Falcon
Investment Bank.

Sahara, a family-owned company, had a very good reputation until
recently when an ongoing tax dispute became public. The dispute may lead
the tax authority to impound plant assets. Furthermore, outdated plant
equipment is causing production disruption and declining profit margins.
The CEO is looking to retire because he is not able to manage the
current challenges.

Omar creates a detailed plan to help manage the IPO process. He plans on
using an extensive checklist and numerous templates that he developed
while at Falcon. Omar decides to employ the same external service
providers he used at Falcon to handle the legal, accounting, and
marketing aspects required for a successful IPO. He considers these
external providers the best in the industry, and their fees are
competitive. He will also work with his previous contacts at the
regulatory authority during the approval process.

As part of the due diligence process, Omar discovers a letter from a
well-known credit rating agency indicating an imminent downgrade of
Sahara to below investment grade. But Omar recalls that a private
placement document being used to pitch the debt issue to investors shows
a pending investment-grade rating. He notes that the outstanding debt is
being paid according to schedule. Omar also finds among the firm's
documents court filings relating to the successful defense of a wrongful
dismissal suit by a former employee fired for theft. In addition, Omar
learns Sahara had been penalized previously for harmful plant emissions
and warned about any reoccurrence.

In the "Investment Risk" section of the draft prospectus, Omar includes
Exhibit 1.

Exhibit 1 Investment Risks

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
Risk        Risk Details               **Possible Business
Impact**
--------------- ------------------------------ ---------------------------
Management      There is the possibility that  Any delay in finding a
Sahara will not find a         replacement could
suitable candidate to replace  negatively affect Sahara's
the retiring CEO in a timely   ability to implement its
fashion.                       strategy for improving
investor returns.

Corporate tax   Sahara is disputing            Sahara may be subject to
underpayment of taxes.         additional tax payments,
penalties, and fines.

Profitability   Sahara faces declining profit  New equipment may not help
margins.                       improve profit margins.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

Knowing that a third-party research firm can add value to the IPO
marketing process by giving an independent opinion, Omar hires Miriam
Halawi, CFA. She is a former colleague who started her own research firm
two years ago. Halawi allows Omar to use her research report in all
Sahara marketing material with proper acknowledgement. After extensive
research, Halawi makes a long-term buy recommendation of Sahara. But she
qualifies the recommendation with a high-risk rating, knowing the IPO
targets retail investors along with institutional investors. Omar
invites Halawi to travel across the region with him to promote the IPO.
Halawi agrees but only if she is paid a flat fee.

Omar works with the marketing specialists to create an advertisement
targeting retail investors to be published in newspapers across the
nation. Institutional investors will be invited to an investor briefing
to kick off the offer period. The final copy reads, in part, as follows:

Invest in the Sahara Manufacturing Company to be assured of a good
return. The company offers the potential for long-term growth with
reasonable levels of risk. Miriam Halawi, CFA, a third-party research
analyst, affirms that Sahara Manufacturing Company is a "long-term buy"!

One week prior to the IPO, Sahara's board of directors approves and
implements an employee share option plan (ESOP). Existing staff members
are allocated 10% of the upcoming IPO at a 25% discount to the IPO
price. Omar acquires his allocation with the intention of selling his
shares at a profit after trading commences. The details of the ESOP are
highlighted in the IPO prospectus.

questions.stem:
Omar's plan for the IPO *most likely* violates the CFA Institute
Standards of Professional Conduct because of his intended use of:
