options.C: lower interest rate volatility and decreasing default correlations.

rationales.A: because an investor buying MBS expects lower, not higher, volatility.
rationales.B: Choate expresses his belief that market expectations of interest rate volatility will decrease, so he buys agency MBS in the COF portfolio. The correlation of expected defaults on the collateral of a CDO affects the relative value between the senior and subordinated tranches; as default correlations increase, the value of mezzanine tranches usually increases relative to the value of senior tranches. Because he expects the correlation to be highly positive, he can try to profit by selling the lower yielding (or selling short) Class A and buying the higher yielding Class B
rationales.C: because an investor that expects higher correlations would short Class A and go long Class B.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Choate's characterization of the risk differences between investment-grade and high-yield bonds.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — three claims to evaluate.

===
CLAIM 1 (IG more sensitive to interest rate changes than HY): CORRECT.
• IG bonds have longer duration and their prices are dominated by rate risk.
• HY bonds trade more like equity — dominated by credit risk, less by rates.

===
CLAIM 2 (IG more sensitive to credit migration risk — spread volatility): CORRECT.
• IG bonds are more affected by rating changes since downgrades from IG to HY trigger forced selling.
• HY is already low-rated; further downgrades within HY have smaller price impact.

===
CLAIM 3 (HY: monitoring outright market value is more critical due to default risk): CORRECT.
• HY carries meaningful default risk → losses on principal — MV monitoring essential.
• Position sizing and MV-based risk metrics dominant for HY.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly; 3=two; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: IDENTIFY the two credit spread measures Choate uses (security level and portfolio level) based on his description.

rationales.model_answer:
SECURITY LEVEL: G-SPREAD (interpolated spread over two on-the-run government bonds).
• Choate specifically describes: 'spread over a linear interpolation of the yields of two on-the-run government bonds' → this is exactly G-Spread.

PORTFOLIO LEVEL: OAS (Option-Adjusted Spread).
• Choate's description: 'applies to a diversified group of securities, whether they have embedded options or not' → OAS handles callables/putables/MBS and can be aggregated to portfolio level.
• OAS strips option value, allowing comparison across bonds with different embedded options.

PARTIAL: 5=G-Spread + OAS both correct; 3=one correct; 0=neither.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Choate's Consideration 1 (liquidity management) as described.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — his approach is largely SOUND.

Positive aspects (any ONE for credit):
• Uses multiple liquidity measures (bid-ask, volumes, funds flows) — comprehensive.
• Managing liquidity through POSITION SIZING is prudent.
• Using Treasuries, ETFs, and derivatives as liquidity buffers is standard practice.
• Recognizes post-GFC deterioration in corporate bond liquidity.

Considerations (any ONE for credit):
• Should also consider average trade size in the security.
• Days-to-liquidate is another important measure.
• Wider bid-ask spreads themselves can be opportunities in stress.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies approach as sound + explains reasoning; 3=partial critique; 0=irrelevant.

questions.stem: Choate's fund objective: beat Barclays US IG Corporate Index by 100 bps annually with tracking error volatility of 200 bps. CALCULATE the implied information ratio target + DISCUSS what this implies about the fund's active risk.

rationales.model_answer:
INFORMATION RATIO = Excess Return / Tracking Error Volatility = 100/200 = 0.50

Accepted reasoning:
• IR of 0.50 is a REASONABLE but not outstanding target (top-quartile managers typically achieve IRs of 0.5-1.0 net of fees).
• 200 bps TEV indicates moderate active risk — enhanced indexing to modest active management, not aggressive.
• Implies about 68% probability of positive excess return in any given year (assuming normal distribution).
• With 100 bps target vs 200 bps TEV, the fund needs consistent alpha rather than large bets to hit the target.

PARTIAL: 5=IR = 0.50 + interpretation; 3=IR = 0.50 only; 0=wrong calc.

cases.title: Credit Strategies (PM Pathway) - Mt. Pleasant Advisers (Ashley West)
cases.vignette:
Ashley West is the Managing Director of Credit Strategies at Mt.
Pleasant Advisers (MPA). She oversees a group of strategists, analysts,
and traders who contribute to managing more than $50 billion in
fixed-income securities. She has gathered her group for their weekly
investment strategy meeting, where they are currently focused on higher
expected volatility in the markets. West makes the following comments:

Comment 1: Relative to high-yield bonds, investment-grade bonds of
the same duration are more sensitive to changes in interest rates.

Comment 2: Credit investors can use the four Cs of credit as a
framework to assess both the probability of default and the estimated
loss given default.

Comment 3: Spread duration is a measure of risk that is more useful
for high-yield bonds than for investment-grade bonds.

West begins a discussion with her trader, Daniel Island, regarding
return compensation for investing in corporate bonds. Island tells West,
"When I am evaluating the price of a corporate bond, the screen on my
Bloomberg terminal shows several spread measures. Some measures are
better than others. Dealers often will quote me a spread to Treasuries
whose maturity does not match the bond's maturity."

Charles Stone joins the conversation. He is MPA's credit strategist and
coordinates the recommendations of the analysts to portfolio managers.
The analysts have provided him with data for three corporate bonds in
the media sector that include the credit rating, spread duration,
z-spread, and expected loss severity. He asks the analysts to provide
data for three additional measures that he feels are required for the
portfolio managers to select the most attractive bond using relative
value analysis. The measures include expected probability of default,
rating agency credit outlook, and historical sector default rates.

West likes to balance the bottom-up approach for portfolio construction
with a top-down approach. She provides portfolio managers with a macro
factors report containing two sections that they use as part of their
investment process. Section 1 shows macro factors that she considers
relevant for credit investing and includes corporate profitability,
economic growth, currency movements, changes in expected market
volatility, key rate durations, and default rates. Section 2 contains
risk measurements that are used for credit portfolio management and
includes average credit rating, average spread duration, duration times
spread, average OAS, duration, and effective convexity.

West is concerned about liquidity risk in the credit markets. She
believes that since the Great Recession, liquidity has declined, and she
asks Stone for his opinion on the topic. Stone replies, "First, trading
volume has declined across credit markets, even for higher-quality
sectors. As a result, liquidity management has become less relevant to
portfolio managers as a means of adding alpha to portfolios. Second,
spread changes are more pronounced during times of outflows in
high-yield markets relative to investment-grade markets, particularly
during times of stress. Therefore, macro forecasting of the economic and
credit cycle would aid in positioning the portfolio to compensate for
liquidity risk. Third, bid--ask spreads can vary over time and are a
good indicator of liquidity. Wider bid--ask spreads in a market downturn
create opportunities for portfolio managers to add value to portfolios."

Stone fields a call from Edisto Palma, an MPA portfolio manager in the
Madrid office. Palma is frustrated with the negative interest rate
environment present in the European Union (EU) debt markets resulting
from the European Central Bank's quantitative easing programs. He tells
Stone he is considering buying securities outside of the EU market to
pick up additional yield. Stone informs Palma that he is sympathetic
with the situation but that there are implications to buying securities
outside his EU benchmark, whether they are from developed or emerging
markets. Stone outlines three suggestions for Palma. First, he should
evaluate whether the spread advantage is negated by the cost of a
currency hedge. Second, he should avoid local currency investments in
countries where the exchange rate is pegged. Third, he should ensure
that the timing of the credit cycles across markets coincides.

questions.stem:
Which of West's comments regarding risk considerations in corporate
bonds is least likely correct?

rationales.A: because Comment 1 is correct. Investment-grade bonds are quoted as a spread to Treasuries and move lockstep in price with changes in interest rates. High-yield bonds are typically quoted in dollar prices or yields and may not move with rates as frequently.
rationales.B: because Comment 2 is correct. The 4 Cs of credit are used to assess creditworthiness and, as a result, can be applied to determine the probability that a borrower will default and the severity of the loss.
rationales.C: West is incorrect in Comment 3. Spread duration is a useful measure for determining a portfolio's sensitivity to changes in credit spreads. Because credit spread volatility--as opposed to outright credit default loss--is more relevant for investment-grade bonds than for high-yield bonds, the risk in a portfolio of investment-grade bonds is typically measured in terms of spread duration.

questions.stem:
In Island's conversation with West, the spread measure that dealers
often quote Island is most likely the:

rationales.A: because the I-spread normally uses swap rates that are denominated in the same currency as the credit security.
rationales.B: The G-spread is the spread over an actual or interpolated government bond.
rationales.C: The benchmark spread is a simple way to calculate a credit spread; it subtracts the yield on a recently issued benchmark-sized security with little or no credit risk (benchmark bond) of a particular maturity from the yield on a credit security. Typically, the benchmark bond is an on-the-run government bond. A problem with benchmark spread is the potential maturity mismatch between the credit security and the benchmark bond. Unless the benchmark yield curve is perfectly flat, using different benchmark bonds will produce different measures of credit spread.

questions.stem:
Assuming no change in credit spreads, which data item that the analysts
provide Stone would portfolio managers most likely find useful in
their analysis?

rationales.A: because historical sector default rates would be useful in a top-down---not bottom-up---approach.
rationales.B: because yield and rating agency credit outlook do not provide the necessary input for the excess return formula.
rationales.C: In the case of unchanged spreads, credit relative value analysis is essentially about weighing the unknown prospect of default losses or credit rating migration against the known compensation provided by credit spreads. Excess return can be calculated as EXR ≈ (s)(t) -- (Δs)(SD) -- (t)(p)(L), where EXR = Excess return, s = Spread, t = Holding period, Δs = Change in spread, SD = Spread duration, p = Expected probability of default, and L = Expected loss severity. The data item most useful for relative value analysis is the expected probability of default.

questions.stem:
Is West's two-section report regarding the top-down approach *most
likely* correctly structured?

options.B: No. She is incorrect with respect to the macro factors.
options.C: No, she is incorrect with respect to the risk measurement factors.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The report is not correctly structured — one item is misclassified between the two sections.
rationales.B: Correct. Section 1's macro-factor list includes KEY RATE DURATIONS alongside corporate profitability, economic growth, currency movements, expected volatility, and default rates. Key rate duration is a portfolio RISK MEASUREMENT — the sensitivity of portfolio value to a shift at a specific curve point — not a macroeconomic factor, so West is incorrect with respect to the macro factors.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Section 2's contents — average credit rating, average spread duration, duration times spread, average OAS, duration, and effective convexity — are all legitimate credit portfolio risk measurements, so West is correct with respect to the risk measurements.

questions.stem:
Stone's comments to West regarding liquidity risk in credit markets
is most likely correct with regard to:

rationales.A: because his first point is incorrect. Liquidity management has become more relevant to portfolio managers as a means of adding alpha to portfolios.
rationales.B: West is incorrect with respect to the macro factors. Key rate durations do not form a macro factor used in the top-down approach to select securities or sectors using relative value. Key rate durations are used by a portfolio manager to measure a portfolio's exposures to non-parallel yield curve changes.
rationales.C: because his third point is incorrect. More volatile market conditions often have a negative effect on bid--ask spreads, and therefore, trading costs can detract from portfolio performance.

questions.stem:
Which of the suggestions Stone outlines for Palma in selecting the
proposed investments is most likely correct? The one regarding:

options.A: the credit cycle is most likely correct.
options.B: spread advantage is most likely correct.
options.C: local currency investment is most likely correct.

rationales.A: because credit cycles may vary across regions and this can be used to the advantage of the portfolio manager to diversify.
rationales.B: Investments in securities denominated in a foreign currency can often produce higher yields than domestic investments produce, particularly in emerging market countries. However, if the portfolio manager chooses to hedge the foreign exchange (FX) exposure, the cost of hedging can offset a significant amount of the yield advantage, thus reducing the relative value advantage. This may not be the case for currencies that are pegged because the portfolio manager may choose not to hedge the FX because the foreign currency will most likely be more stable, assuming there is no devaluation.
rationales.C: because investments in countries where the currency is pegged may not require FX hedging, given more stability in the exchange rate.

questions.stem: EVALUATE each of West's three comments about credit portfolio management.

rationales.model_answer:
===
COMMENT 1 (IG bonds more sensitive to rates than HY of same duration): CORRECT.
• Rate risk dominates IG returns; HY dominated by credit risk.

===
COMMENT 2 (four Cs of credit for both PD AND LGD): INCORRECT.
• The four Cs (Capacity, Collateral, Covenants, Character) primarily assess PROBABILITY OF DEFAULT and creditworthiness.
• LOSS GIVEN DEFAULT (severity) is assessed separately using recovery rates, seniority, collateral value.
• Comment conflates two distinct analyses.

===
COMMENT 3 (spread duration more useful for HY than IG): INCORRECT.
• Spread duration IS important for HY, BUT it is ALSO important for IG.
• For IG bonds, spread duration measures sensitivity to spread changes — critical given IG's larger portfolio weight in most bond funds.
• Not 'more useful' for HY specifically.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: Island describes 'spread to Treasuries whose maturity does not match the bond's maturity.' IDENTIFY the correct measure to use INSTEAD + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
ANSWER: G-SPREAD (interpolated spread) or Z-SPREAD.

Accepted reasoning:
• G-SPREAD: linearly interpolates yields of two on-the-run Treasuries to match the bond's exact maturity — better than a mismatched benchmark.
• Z-SPREAD: constant spread added to the risk-free spot curve; correctly accounts for the term structure across all cash flows.
• Both are superior to a simple 'spread to Treasury' when maturities don't match.

PARTIAL: 5=G-Spread OR Z-Spread + rationale; 3=names one without explanation; 0=other.

questions.stem: Stone's Macro Factors Report Section 1 and Section 2 each list metrics. IDENTIFY any metrics in Section 1 that are MISCATEGORIZED (belong in Section 2) or vice versa.

rationales.model_answer:
MISCATEGORIZED METRICS:

SECTION 1 (macro factors) contains:
• KEY RATE DURATIONS — this is a PORTFOLIO RISK METRIC, belongs in Section 2.
• DEFAULT RATES — this is DEBATABLE: macro factor for overall economy is reasonable, but rating-agency default rate as a metric for a specific portfolio would be Section 2. Accept either interpretation.

SECTION 2 (portfolio risk metrics) contains:
• EFFECTIVE CONVEXITY — correctly placed as portfolio risk metric.
• AVERAGE OAS — correctly placed.
• All others correctly placed.

Correct classification:
• MACRO FACTORS: corporate profitability, GDP/growth, currency, expected market volatility, default rates (economy-wide).
• PORTFOLIO METRICS: KRDs, average rating, spread duration, DTS, OAS, duration, effective convexity.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies KRD miscategorization + reasoning; 3=identifies KRD without full logic; 0=all classifications accepted as correct.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Stone's three claims about liquidity in credit markets.

rationales.model_answer:
===
CLAIM 1 (trading volume declined; liquidity management LESS relevant for adding alpha): INCORRECT.
• Trading volume decline is factual (post-GFC).
• BUT: reduced liquidity makes liquidity management MORE important for alpha, not less. Skilled managers extract value from liquidity dislocations.

===
CLAIM 2 (spread changes more pronounced during outflows in HY vs IG, particularly in stress): CORRECT.
• HY is more sensitive to fund flows and market sentiment.
• IG has broader institutional demand base providing more stability.

===
CLAIM 3 (wider bid-ask in downturns create opportunities to add value): CORRECT.
• Dislocation-driven wide spreads offer entry opportunities for patient buyers.
• Consistent with liquidity-provisioning alpha strategies.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

cases.title: Index-Based Equity Strategies - Sapphire Bay (Edward Cullen)
cases.vignette:
-   6 questions in 2024. 1 question may have been removed for 2025 and
beyond.

Edward Cullen advises the board of directors of the Sapphire Bay
Foundation (Sapphire) regarding all aspects of the investment portfolio
of Sapphire's endowment fund. Traditionally, Cullen drove the selection
of active investment managers for the various asset classes. Despite
historically ranking well among peers, several of the managers have
performed below the level of their respective benchmarks in the past few
years. Cullen's colleague Paige Stapleton recommends that some passive
management should be introduced into Sapphire's investment mix using
pooled investments. They agree to introduce the idea to Sapphire's board
at its next meeting.

At the next board meeting, Cullen begins by introducing passive
investing to Sapphire's board. He states that open-end mutual funds and
exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are appropriate approaches. Both
alternatives are readily available, offer a broad spectrum of investment
choices, and are easy to buy and sell. He makes the following comments
comparing the two alternatives.

1.  Both mutual funds and ETFs can be purchased on margin.

2.  Investors can take short positions in ETFs but not in mutual funds.

3.  Both mutual funds and ETFs have the same degree of liquidity.

Stapleton then begins a description of factor-based strategies. These
include common equity factors, such as value, size, and quality, and
they can be used either in place of or to complement market-cap-weighted
indexing. She points out that relative to market-cap weighting,
factor-based strategies tend to diversify risk exposures; are
transparent in terms of factor selection, weighting, and rebalancing;
but can be copied by other investors, which can reduce the advantages of
a strategy.

Cullen provides Sapphire's board with an example comparing the
performance of the River Valley Fund, a factor-based fund, with its
benchmark portfolio (Exhibit 1). The fund uses benchmark segments of
four mutually exclusive sub-categories. Cullen calculates the percentage
of River Valley's excess return that resulted from active
factor-weighting decisions.

Exhibit 1 Attribution Data for River Valley Fund and Benchmark

| Total  | 1.00   | 5.84   | 94     | 1.00   | 6.06   | 100    |

For the large-cap US equity portion of Sapphire's investment portfolio,
Cullen believes that there are some existing passive indexed-based funds
that track the S&P 500 Index that the foundation should consider. Cullen
presents Exhibit 2 to Sapphire's board.

Exhibit 2 S&P 500 Index Funds

fund/index**

Dividends reinvested          Next day    Same day    Next day

**Management fee (in basis        12          15          10
points)**

Rebalance                     Quarterly   Quarterly   Quarterly

Reconstitution                Quarterly   Quarterly   Semi-Annually
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

For the international portion of the investment portfolio, Stapleton
suggests that Sapphire invest in an MSCI EAFE index portfolio
specifically tailored for the foundation rather than investing in an
existing index fund. Anne Rowland, Sapphire's board chair, asks her how
this could be accomplished, given that the initial allocation is only
$15 million. Stapleton suggests that Sapphire hire a manager to
purchase a portfolio of securities that are a mutually exclusive yet
comprehensive subgroup of the index designed to track the index return
and risk characteristics.

questions.stem: In comparing mutual funds and ETFs, Cullen is *most* accurate in:

rationales.A: Only ETF investors can purchase shares on margin.
rationales.B: Only ETF investors can take short positions.
rationales.C: Mutual funds and ETFs do not have the same degree of liquidity. Although ETFs allow for inter-day trading they could experience periods of market illiquidity. Mutual fund units are redeemed directly from the mutual fund.

questions.stem:
When comparing factor-based strategies relative to the market-cap
weighting of an index, Stapleton's comments are most likely:

rationales.A: Stapleton's comment is correct regarding transparency but incorrect regarding risk exposure. Passive factor-based strategies tend to be transparent in terms of factor selection, weighting, and rebalancing. The strategies can be easily replicated by other investors which can produce overcrowding and reduce the realized advantages of a strategy. Relative to broad-market-cap-weighting, passive factor-based strategies tend to concentrate risk exposures, leaving investors exposed during periods when a chosen risk factor is out of favor.
rationales.B: Stapleton's comment is correct regarding transparency. Passive factor-based strategies tend to be transparent in terms of factor selection, weighting, and rebalancing. The strategies can be easily replicated by other investors which can produce overcrowding and reduce the realized advantages of a strategy.
rationales.C: Stapleton's comment is incorrect regarding risk exposure. Relative to broad-market-cap-weighting, passive factor-based strategies tend to concentrate risk exposures, leaving investors exposed during periods when a chosen risk factor is out of favor.

questions.stem:
In Exhibit 1, the percentage of the excess return of the River
Valley Fund arising from active factor weighting is closest to:

rationales.A: This is the value of the total effect (--0.22%).
rationales.B: The candidate did not divide the sum of the difference due to factor weights (--0.04%) by the total effect (--0.22%).
rationales.C: The percentage of excess return arising from active factor weighting is 18.18%. In comparing the weights between the fund and the benchmark, the factors with different weights are Growth and Quality. The total contribution to the return caused by active factor weighting is:      The fund's holding of Momentum securities was less than the benchmark's (24 versus 30), and thus, the fund incurred active security selection risk. But it did not incur active factor risk, since the factor weight is the same as that of the benchmark.    ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- River               Benchmark              Return                       Attribution   Difference Valley                                           Contribution                 Analysis      Due to: Fund ------------ ---------- ---------- --------------- ---------- ---------------- ------------- --------------- -------------- ------------- Factor   Weight   Return   Weight (C)  Return   River Valley   Benchmark   Total Effect  Factor       Security (A)      (B)                      (D)      (A) × (B) =      (C) × (D) =   (E) - (F) =     Weight       Selection (E)            (F)         (G)  Growth       0.22       7.9%       0.25            7.9%       1.74%            1.98%         --0.24%         --0.24%  Value        0.19       5.2%       0.19            5.2%       0.99%            0.99%         0.00%  Quality      0.29       6.7%       0.26            6.7%       1.94%            1.74%         0.20%           0.20%  Momentum     0.30       3.9%       0.30            4.5%       1.17%            1.35%         --0.18%                        --0.18%  Total    1.00                  1.00                       5.84%            6.06%         --0.22%         --0.04%        --0.18% -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 2, the portfolio manager *most likely* to have the
largest tracking error is:

rationales.A: Incorrect. Manager A holds 498 of 500 index names (near-full replication) with quarterly reconstitution — small sampling gap and timely index maintenance keep tracking error low despite next-day dividend reinvestment.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Manager B holds 504 names against a 500-stock index and reinvests dividends same-day — the closest replication and most tracking-friendly dividend treatment, despite the highest fee.
rationales.C: Correct. Manager C holds only 475 of 500 index names (the largest sampling gap), reinvests dividends next day, and reconstitutes only SEMI-ANNUALLY while the index reconstitutes more frequently — allowing the portfolio to drift from the index between updates. These three features combine to produce the largest expected tracking error.

questions.stem:
The portfolio method suggested by Stapleton to replicate the MSCI
EAFE Index is best described as:

rationales.A: Incorrect. Optimization selects holdings by minimizing predicted tracking error through a risk model, explicitly accounting for covariances among securities, typically subject to constraints. Stapleton describes representative cell-based selection, not an optimizer-driven construction.
rationales.B: Correct. Stapleton proposes purchasing securities that form a mutually exclusive yet comprehensive subgroup of the index, designed to track the index's return and risk characteristics — the definition of stratified sampling: divide the index into mutually exclusive, exhaustive cells and hold representative securities from each. It is the standard method for tracking a large-constituent index (MSCI EAFE) with a modest ($15 million) allocation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. A blended approach combines methods — for example, stratified sampling plus optimization. Stapleton describes a single sampling technique with no optimization component.

questions.stem: Based on Exhibit 2, the manager whose portfolio management practices are *most* supportive of minimizing tracking error is:

rationales.A: Incorrect. Manager A replicates closely (498 of 500 names) and reconstitutes quarterly, but reinvests dividends the NEXT day. The index assumes same-day reinvestment, so the one-day lag introduces a small recurring source of return deviation that Manager B avoids.
rationales.B: Correct. Manager B's practices are the most tracking-friendly in Exhibit 2: full index coverage (504 holdings against the 500-name index), SAME-DAY dividend reinvestment matching the index's total-return assumption, and quarterly rebalancing and reconstitution aligned with index maintenance. The higher 15 bp fee lowers net return by a constant, but the return PATH deviates least from the index.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Manager C combines the largest sampling gap (475 of 500 names), next-day dividend reinvestment, and only SEMI-ANNUAL reconstitution — allowing membership drift between index updates. These are the practices most likely to WIDEN tracking error, not minimize it.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Cullen's three statements comparing ETFs and open-end mutual funds.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STMT 1 (both can be purchased on margin): INCORRECT.
• ETFs can be purchased on margin (trade like stocks).
• Mutual funds CANNOT be purchased on margin (only end-of-day NAV pricing).

===
STMT 2 (short positions in ETFs but not mutual funds): CORRECT.
• ETF shares can be shorted; MF shares cannot.

===
STMT 3 (both have same degree of liquidity): INCORRECT.
• ETFs trade continuously intraday with real-time liquidity.
• Mutual funds settle at end-of-day NAV only.
• Liquidity structures differ materially.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Stapleton's three claims about factor-based strategies relative to market-cap-weighted indexing.

rationales.model_answer:
===
CLAIM 1 (factor-based diversifies risk exposures): CORRECT.
• Well-designed factor strategies target specific risk premia and can reduce concentration in mega-cap stocks.

===
CLAIM 2 (transparent in factor selection, weighting, rebalancing): CORRECT.
• Rules-based factor construction is publicly documented.

===
CLAIM 3 (can be copied by other investors → reduces advantages): CORRECT.
• Widespread adoption of a factor can drive up prices of favored stocks, compressing the factor premium (crowding).
• All three statements are correct.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed correctly; 3=two correct; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: Using Exhibit 1, CALCULATE the portion of River Valley's excess return attributable to active FACTOR-WEIGHTING decisions and the portion attributable to security selection within factors. Show your work.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — full decomposition from Exhibit 1 (fund 5.84% vs benchmark 6.06%; excess = −0.22%).

===
ACTIVE FACTOR-WEIGHTING (allocation) = Σ (fund weight − benchmark weight) × benchmark factor return:
• Growth: (0.22 − 0.25) × 7.9 = −0.237%
• Value: (0.19 − 0.19) × 5.2 = 0
• Quality: (0.29 − 0.26) × 6.7 = +0.201%
• Momentum: (0.30 − 0.30) × 4.5 = 0
• TOTAL ALLOCATION ≈ −0.036% (accept −0.03% to −0.04%)

===
SECURITY SELECTION = Σ fund weight × (fund factor return − benchmark factor return):
• Only Momentum differs: 0.30 × (3.9 − 4.5) = −0.18%
• TOTAL SELECTION = −0.18%

===
CHECK: −0.036% + (−0.18%) = −0.216% ≈ −0.22% excess ✓

===
SHARE OF EXCESS from factor-weighting = −0.036 / −0.216 ≈ 17% (selection ≈ 83%). Accept 15–20%.

A bare correct pair of numbers (allocation ≈ −0.04%, selection ≈ −0.18%, or ~17%/83% split) earns full credit without prose.

PARTIAL: full=both components (or the split) with method; half=correct method, arithmetic slip; low=one component; 0=wrong framework.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two key differences between using pooled investments (mutual funds/ETFs) versus a separately-managed account for passive equity exposure at Sapphire's scale.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted differences:
• COST: SMAs may have lower total expense ratios at scale; pooled vehicles have fund-level fees applied to all shareholders.
• CUSTOMIZATION: SMAs allow tax-loss harvesting, ESG customization, and exclusions; pooled vehicles offer standard portfolios.
• TAX EFFICIENCY: SMAs enable direct tax-loss harvesting at security level; pooled vehicles distribute gains to all holders.
• TRANSPARENCY: SMAs give full position transparency; pooled vehicles typically quarterly.
• MINIMUM INVESTMENT: SMAs require larger minimums (usually $1M+); pooled vehicles accessible at any size.
• GOVERNANCE: SMAs allow custom risk controls; pooled vehicles use fund rules.

PARTIAL: 5=two distinct differences + reasoning; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Index-Based Equity Strategies - Arthur Camme (Arthur Camme)
cases.vignette:
Arthur Camme, founder of Camme Consulting, Inc., advises endowment funds
and private foundations regarding equity manager selection. Because of
the poor relative performance and high fees of active management over
the past several years, Camme has been fielding an increasing number of
inquiries from clients about the best ways to use passive solutions for
equity exposure.

Camme meets with client Sylvia Parker, who seeks guidance in
establishing passive exposure for her $400 million family foundation.
For a portion of equities, Parker wants to use an index approach
augmented by factor-based solutions. She comments, "I hear that
factor-based approaches can be used when pursuing risk-adjusted returns
superior to that of a comparable market cap-weighted index. In addition,

-   fundamental weighting can enhance return, but when compared with
intrinsic values, it has the disadvantages of overweighting
overpriced stocks and underweighting underpriced stocks;

-   value factor funds seek to lower downside risk; and

-   relative to cap weighting, single-factor funds tend to concentrate
risk exposure."

Camme suggests to Parker that the first priority in moving to a passive
solution is to consider the choices available for index exposure. Owing
to the large size of the fund, Camme recommends choosing a replication
manager in order to minimize total costs. He explains the differences
between full replication, stratified sampling, and optimization when
constructing the portfolio. Parker likes the idea of blending stratified
sampling with optimization and asks Camme to identify the appropriate
manager based on the data in Exhibit 1.

Exhibit 1 Equity Replication Managers

-------------------------------------------------------------------------

B**

Benchmark                    S&P 500       MSCI EAFE    Russell 3000

**Number of holdings:            498/500       800/928      1,200/3,000
Fund/index**

Usage of futures             For cash drag For currency No
overlay

Accounts for covariances     No            No           Yes

Tracking error               0.06%         1.97%        0.34%

Excess return                0.10%         2.10%        --0.15%
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

In reviewing the relative performance of Manager B from Exhibit 1,
Parker makes the following statements:

-   He faced more volatile markets than the others did, based on the
tracking errors.

-   He used currency overlays to lever the returns of securities held in
foreign currency.

-   His excess return looks like it is more a matter of luck than skill.

Another client is Ken Dashe, the new chairman of the investment
committee of the Daventown Arts Endowment. He wants Camme to help him
understand the primary investment strategy being followed by the
MultiFAK fund, which uses several factors to structure and maintain its
large-cap active portfolio. The fund uses benchmark segments of four
mutually exclusive sub-categories as shown in Exhibit 2.

Exhibit 2 Attribution Data for MultiFAK Fund and Benchmark

Due to a recent fund raising campaign, Daventown experienced sizable
cash inflows and the funds were applied in accordance with policy

**Exhibit 3 Excerpts from Daventown Arts Endowment Investment Policy

**Active       Seek excess returns through the use of active equity and

guideline**    of 2%.
Fixed income is to consist of 50% US Treasuries and 50%

**Derivative   1. Cash drag can be addressed through derivatives.
usage**        2. Allocation guidelines can be achieved with
