questions.stem:
The investment process indicated for the Fraser Fund is *most
likely* designed around which of the following?

options.A: A balanced exposure to known rewarded factors
options.B: Research-based rules across a broad universe of securities
options.C: The inclusion of non-financial variables, such as pricing power

rationales.A: A systematic investment approach is likely to be designed around extracting premiums from a balanced exposure to known, rewarded factors, whereas a discretionary approach usually searches for active return from firm-specific factors.
rationales.B: A systematic investment approach is likely to be designed around research-based rules across a broad universe of securities, whereas a discretionary approach will involve managerial judgments on a smaller subset of securities.
rationales.C: The discretionary investment process searches for active returns from firm-specific factors, such as pricing power and the competitive landscape. This process results in more concentrated portfolios reflecting the depth of the manager's insights on firm characteristics and the competitive landscape. A systematic investment approach is likely to be designed around extracting premiums from a balanced exposure to known, rewarded factors and typically incorporates research-based rules across a broad universe of securities.

questions.stem:
Using Exhibit 1, the average monthly return of the Fraser Fund that
is unexplained by rewarded factors is closest to:

rationales.A: Correct. The return unexplained by rewarded factors ('alpha') = actual excess return − Σβ_pk × F_k. Rewarded-factor return = (0.91 × 0.61%) + (0.15 × 0.17%) + (0.60 × 0.18%) + (0.08 × 0.72%) = 0.555% + 0.026% + 0.108% + 0.058% ≈ 0.75%. Unexplained = 0.55% − 0.75% = −0.20% per month.
rationales.B: Incorrect. −0.17% is the fund's shortfall versus its BENCHMARK (0.55% − 0.72%) — the active return relative to the Russell 1000 Value Index, not the portion of the fund's return unexplained by its own rewarded-factor exposures.
rationales.C: Incorrect. 0.13% results from adding the 0.33% monthly risk-free rate back to the −0.20% unexplained return (−0.20% + 0.33% = 0.13%). The computation must be done consistently in excess-of-risk-free terms; the risk-free rate does not belong in the alpha.

questions.stem:
The Mattley Fund manager is *most likely* following which investment
style?

rationales.A: A sector rotator typically has high active risk and could have either high or low Active Share, depending on whether a concentrated or diversified portfolio approach was followed.
rationales.B: A closet index would exhibit both low Active Share and low active risk, because such funds make few active bets.
rationales.C: The Mattley Fund exhibited relatively high Active Share but relatively low active risk. High Active Share indicates that a manager's holdings differ substantially from the benchmark, and low active risk indicates low idiosyncratic risk resulting from diversification: This combination indicates that the manager most likely follows a diversified stock picking strategy. A sector rotator typically has high active risk and could have either high or low Active Share, depending on whether a concentrated or diversified portfolio approach was followed. A closet index would exhibit both low Active Share and low active risk, because such funds make few active bets.

questions.stem:
In Lazare and Warrack's comments about Active Share and active risk,
the comment that is least accurate is the one concerning:

rationales.A: The active risk attributed to Active Share will be smaller for more diversified portfolios with lower idiosyncratic risk.
rationales.B: The comment concerning neutralizing factor exposure is incorrect: In a single-factor model, if the factor exposure is neutralized, the active risk will be entirely attributable to the Active Share--a consequence of the manager deviating from benchmark weights. The active risk attributed to Active Share will be smaller for more diversified portfolios with lower idiosyncratic risk. Active risk does rise with an increase in factor and idiosyncratic volatility.
rationales.C: Active risk does rise with an increase in factor and idiosyncratic volatility.

questions.stem:
If the Western Fund manager is hired, which constraint
is most restrictive with respect to average position size? The
constraint related to:

rationales.A: The most restrictive constraint on position size for the Western Fund manager is the liquidity constraint. It arises from the restriction on average daily trading volume: The average position size cannot exceed $1.13 million.  Average daily trading for the representative company size: 0.90% × $1.8 billion = $16.2 million.  -   Restriction on trading volume: 7% × Average daily trading = 7% × $16.2 million = $1.13 million.  -   Restriction on allocation: 1.75% × $100 million = $1.75 million.  -   Restriction on diversification: $100 million/60 securities = $1.67 million.  Position size is restricted by trading volume, and the fund could hold up to $100 million/$1.13 million = 89 securities.
rationales.B: With a requirement of at least 60 securities, an investment of up to $100 million/60 = $1.67 million is allowed in any security. The restriction on daily trading ($1.13 million) prevents that much from being acquired.
rationales.C: The allocation restriction is 1.75% of funds under management = 1.75% × $100 million = $1.75 million. However, the restriction on daily trading ($1.13 million) prevents this amount from being acquired.

questions.stem:
The proportion of the Herrick Fund's portfolio variance contributed
by Country A is closest to:

rationales.A: Incorrect. 20.9% results from dividing Country A's covariance with the portfolio by the portfolio's standard deviation (0.0276/0.132) while omitting the 30% country weight — a units error mixing variance and standard deviation and dropping the weight entirely.
rationales.B: Correct. Country A's contribution to portfolio variance = weight × covariance with the portfolio = 0.30 × 0.0276 = 0.00828. As a proportion of total portfolio variance (0.132² = 0.017424): 0.00828 / 0.017424 ≈ 47.5%.
rationales.C: Incorrect. 56.8% comes from weight × standalone standard deviation over portfolio standard deviation (0.30 × 25/13.2). Using Country A's standalone volatility ignores its correlations with Countries B and C — the covariance with the portfolio, not the standalone risk, determines the variance contribution.

questions.stem: Using Fama-French factors and Exhibit 1, CALCULATE the alpha (non-factor return) of the Fraser Fund. Show work.

rationales.model_answer:
APPROACH:
Alpha = Fund excess return − Σ(Fund β × Factor performance)

Fund excess return over risk-free = 0.55%

Factor-attributed returns:
• Market: 0.91 × 0.61% = 0.5551%
• Size: 0.15 × 0.17% = 0.0255%
• Value: 0.60 × 0.18% = 0.108%
• Momentum: 0.08 × 0.72% = 0.0576%
Sum of factor returns = 0.5551 + 0.0255 + 0.108 + 0.0576 = 0.7462%

Alpha = 0.55% − 0.7462% = -0.196% (approximately -0.20%)

INTERPRETATION: Fraser Fund's non-factor alpha is NEGATIVE (~-20 bps monthly) — fund is underperforming what its factor exposures would predict.

PARTIAL: 5=~-0.20% + work; 4=method correct, arithmetic off; 3=negative alpha identified without full work; 0=positive alpha.

questions.stem: EVALUATE Lazare and Warrack's three statements about Active Share, active risk, and idiosyncratic volatility.

rationales.model_answer:
===
STMT 1 (active risk rises with idiosyncratic volatility): CORRECT.
• Higher idiosyncratic risk in the securities increases active risk from unhedged security-specific bets.

===
STMT 2 (active risk from Active Share is SMALLER in more diversified portfolios): CORRECT.
• Diversification averages out idiosyncratic risks across many holdings.
• The idiosyncratic component of active risk shrinks as N grows.

===
STMT 3 (if factor exposure is fully neutralized, all Active Share attributes to active risk): INCORRECT (subtle).
• If factor exposures are neutralized, Active Share is FULLY attributed to IDIOSYNCRATIC RISK, not necessarily to active risk in a mechanical sense.
• Active risk = idiosyncratic + factor components. Neutralizing factor removes factor component; the remaining IS idiosyncratic, which IS active risk. 
• The statement is technically correct but the wording 'entirely attributed' conflates concepts. Accept 'CORRECT' if candidate reasons carefully.

PARTIAL: 5=all three assessed with reasoning; 3=two clearly; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: DISCUSS whether the Mattley Fund's profile (high Active Share, low active risk) indicates skilled management.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — assessment is NUANCED.

Accepted reasoning:
• HIGH ACTIVE SHARE + LOW ACTIVE RISK indicates the manager holds DIFFERENT SECURITIES than the benchmark but avoids concentrated factor bets.
• This profile is consistent with DIVERSIFIED STOCK-SELECTION strategy.
• It suggests the manager is:
  - Not chasing beta (low active risk)
  - Not making concentrated sector/factor bets
  - Genuinely picking stocks based on fundamentals
• SKILL VERIFICATION requires:
  - Track record of positive alpha AFTER fees
  - Consistency of alpha across market regimes
  - Reasonable factor-neutral returns over long periods
• The PROFILE ALONE doesn't prove skill; it's a positive PREDICTOR of active management value if alpha is present.

PARTIAL: 5=nuanced discussion + skill verification framework; 3=one-sided claim; 0=vague.

questions.stem: DISCUSS how the Western Fund's constraints (7% ADV limit, 1.75% position limit) affect its ability to add alpha in small-cap value.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — constraints REDUCE alpha potential.

Accepted reasoning:
• The 7% ADV constraint limits POSITION SIZE — even with high-conviction ideas, the fund cannot fully express its view.
• The 1.75% AUM position limit similarly caps concentration.
• Small-cap securities have lower liquidity — the fund needs to accumulate/exit positions over many days, creating implementation costs.
• At $1.8B average market cap and 0.90% daily turnover: daily volume = $16.2M. 7% = $1.13M per day. Total position of 1.75% of AUM (assuming $750M) = $13.1M → takes 12 days to build/exit.
• Constraint reduces active weight per position → dilutes potential alpha.
• Higher position count required for diversification, which further dilutes concentration.

PARTIAL: 5=identifies constraints + explains impact + quantifies; 3=identifies impact without numbers; 0=vague.

cases.title: Inflection Capital Case Scenario (Derek Mulaney)
cases.vignette:
Derek Mulaney is the head trader at Inflection Capital, a Boston-based
investment management firm offering traditional and alternative
investment strategies. Mulaney leads a three-person trading team
consisting of himself, Feng Shui, and Abagail Morrison. The trading team
works closely with Inflection's portfolio managers, analysts, and
compliance team in the execution of the firm's investment strategies.

As part of his morning team meeting, Mulaney often reviews trading
situations with Shui and Morrison. He brings up an example that recently
occurred while trading for Inflection's hedge fund. The portfolio
manager, Chris Largent, was listening to a 10:00 a.m. earnings call for
NeuvoGen (NGEN), a midsize biotech company, during which management
announced additional unfavorable news. Largent determined the market had
significantly underreacted to the news and wanted to set up a trade to
exploit this potential alpha opportunity. Largent communicated a sell
order to Mulaney to establish a short position for the fund representing
approximately 3% of expected trading volume for the stock that day.
Mulaney describes how he used a trading algorithm to sell the shares,
adding that some algorithms are more suitable to use than others,
depending on the situation.

Morrison has been working on executing a sell order to liquidate shares
in Acme Industrials (ACME). The closing price on Tuesday was $79.90.
Morrison received the order from Wright before the market open on
Wednesday morning to sell 50,000 ACME shares with a day limit of
$80.00. No part of the order is filled on Wednesday, and the stock
closes the day at $79.60. On Thursday morning, Morrison instructed a
new day order to sell the 50,000 shares at $79.50; 30,000 shares were
sold at $79.50 per share, with resulting commissions and fees for the
trade of $0.02 per share. Shares for ACME closed the day Thursday at a
price of $79.40 per share. No further attempts were made to sell the
unfilled 20,000 shares, which were canceled.

Shui is instructed to execute a buy order of TeXelence (TEXX), a
small-cap technology stock. Shui is able to execute the order over the
course of 30 minutes and received an average price of $50.55. The price
at the time the order was placed is $50.45. The volume-weighted average
price (VWAP) over the trading horizon was $50.52, and the time-weighted
average price (TWAP) over the horizon was $50.57, with a closing price
later that day of $50.60. The market rose during the trading horizon,
with an index arrival price of 1,010 and a VWAP of 1,015. Shui
calculates that TEXX has a beta to the index of 1.30.

At the end of each trading week, Mulaney holds a strategy meeting with
Shui and Morrison to evaluate trade execution and to discuss strategies
for improvement in the future. They are discussing trade implementation
in different markets as they begin to support a new macro-hedge fund
that frequently trades in emerging market equities and often uses
derivatives to implement its strategy.

Morrison is discussing trading trends and makes the following
observation: "In recent years, average trade sizes appear to be
increasing, and in emerging markets, a similar volume of equity trading
is now taking place on dark pools as on 'lit' exchanges."

Shui comments, "For exchange-traded derivatives, market transparency is
similar to that of exchange-traded equities, with trade price, size,
quote, and depth of book all publicly available. I believe we should
explore algorithmic trading in futures markets, where it is more evolved
than it is for options markets."

Mulaney adds, "We can also explore the use of over-the-counter (OTC)
derivatives. Liquidity has increased for OTC instruments, even for those
not suited to central clearing, and average trade size is still
relatively small, which could align well with the needs of the fund."

questions.stem:
Which of the following trading algorithms did Mulaney *most
likely* use when trading NGEN?

rationales.A: Arrival price algorithms are used for orders in which the portfolio manager or trader believes prices are likely to move unfavorably during the trade horizon. In this case, the portfolio manager wishes to trade more aggressively to capture alpha ahead of the price decline expected later in the trade horizon. These algorithms are used when the security is relatively liquid or the order is not outsized (less than 15% of expected volume), which is the case here.
rationales.B: Dark aggregator algorithms are used in trading when portfolio managers and traders are concerned with information leakage that may occur from posting limit orders. These algorithms are used when the order size is large relative to the market (i.e., a large percentage of expected volume) and are appropriate for trades in which the trader or portfolio manager does not need to execute the order in its entirety.
rationales.C: TWAP algorithms slice the order into smaller amounts to send to the market following an equal-weighted time schedule. TWAP algorithms are appropriate for orders in which portfolio managers or traders do not have expectations of adverse price movement during the trade horizon. These algorithms are also used by portfolio managers and traders who have a greater risk tolerance for longer execution time periods, which is not the case here.

questions.stem:
The TEXX trade *most likely *looks best relative to which trading
benchmark?

rationales.A: Correct. Arrival cost = (50.55 − 50.45)/50.45 ≈ +19.8 bps. The market rose over the horizon: index cost = (1,015 − 1,010)/1,010 ≈ +49.5 bps, and with TEXX's beta of 1.30 the expected stock move is 1.30 × 49.5 ≈ 64.4 bps. Market-adjusted cost = 19.8 − 64.4 ≈ −44.6 bps — the buy was executed well BELOW what the market move alone would have implied, making this the benchmark against which the trade looks best.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Versus TWAP: (50.55 − 50.57)/50.57 ≈ −4.0 bps — favorable, but far smaller than the −44.6 bps market-adjusted result.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Versus VWAP: (50.55 − 50.52)/50.52 ≈ +5.9 bps — a COST relative to the volume-weighted benchmark, the worst of the three comparisons.

questions.stem:
Whose comments at the weekend strategy meeting are *most
likely* accurate?

rationales.A: Shui is correct in her comments; for exchange-traded derivatives, market transparency is similar to that of exchange-traded equities, with trade price, size, quote, and depth of book all publicly available. In addition, algorithmic trading is more evolved in futures markets than in options markets.
rationales.B: Mulaney indicates that liquidity has increased for OTC instruments not suited for central clearing, whereas it has actually decreased. In addition, trade sizes for OTC derivatives are relatively large.
rationales.C: Morrison indicates that average trade sizes have increased, whereas they have generally decreased as participants break down their trades into smaller pieces that they either trade sequentially or simultaneously across different venues. In addition, Morrison indicates that a similar volume of equity trading in emerging markets is taking place in dark pools as on traditional exchanges, whereas the volume in dark pools is actually minimal in comparison.

questions.stem: For Largent's alpha-opportunity SELL order (3% of daily volume, market has underreacted), IDENTIFY the most appropriate trading algorithm + JUSTIFY.

rationales.model_answer:
ANSWER: LIQUIDITY-SEEKING or IMPLEMENTATION SHORTFALL algorithm (or PERCENTAGE-OF-VOLUME with aggressive parameters).

Accepted reasoning (any ONE):
• Alpha decay: the trade must execute QUICKLY before the market catches up to the news — VWAP or scheduled algorithms are too slow.
• LIQUIDITY-SEEKING algorithms hunt for available liquidity across venues to complete the order fastest.
• Implementation shortfall algorithms MINIMIZE arrival-price slippage — appropriate when the market is likely to move against the trader.
• 3% of daily volume is manageable; aggressive execution is warranted given the alpha signal decay.

REJECTED:
• VWAP (scheduled, slow — misses alpha).
• TWAP (time-based, doesn't respond to liquidity).
• Dark aggregator alone (may not fill fast enough).

PARTIAL: 5=liquidity-seeking/IS + rationale; 3=names appropriate algo without reasoning; 1=names slow algo; 0=irrelevant.

questions.stem: For Morrison's ACME order (Wednesday $80 limit, Thursday $79.50 limit, 30k/50k filled at $79.50), CALCULATE the implementation shortfall components (delay cost, trading cost, missed trade opportunity cost, commissions).

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — full IS decomposition:

Decision price (Tuesday close): $79.90; Order size: 50,000 shares.
Paper portfolio value at decision = 50,000 × $79.90 = $3,995,000.

1. DELAY COST: from decision to arrival (Wed morning): market moved to $80 range at open; assume arrival price ≈ Wed open. Delay cost negligible if arrival ≈ decision (but shares unfilled Wed at $79.60 close indicates market fell).

2. EXECUTION COST (30,000 shares filled at $79.50 vs decision $79.90):
   30,000 × ($79.50 − $79.90) = -$12,000 (loss from decision price).

3. MISSED TRADE OPPORTUNITY COST (20,000 shares unfilled):
   20,000 × ($79.40 − $79.90) = -$10,000 (opportunity cost measured at cancel).

4. COMMISSIONS + FEES: 30,000 × $0.02 = $600.

TOTAL IMPLEMENTATION SHORTFALL (LOSS from decision):
   $12,000 + $10,000 + $600 = $22,600.
   As % of paper value: $22,600 / $3,995,000 ≈ 56.6 bps.

PARTIAL: 5=all four components + total; 4=three components; 3=two; 0=fewer.

questions.stem: For Shui's TEXX buy order (avg $50.55, arrival $50.45, VWAP $50.52, TWAP $50.57, beta 1.30, index arrival 1010, index VWAP 1015), EVALUATE the trade's performance using arrival-price basis and market-adjusted basis.

rationales.model_answer:
===
ARRIVAL PRICE COST (in-line vs arrival):
• Cost = ($50.55 − $50.45) / $50.45 = 0.198% = ~19.8 bps unfavorable to trader.

===
MARKET-ADJUSTED (subtract market drift):
• Market moved from index 1010 to 1015 during trading horizon → +0.495% index return.
• Expected TEXX move due to market = beta × index return = 1.30 × 0.495% = 0.644% price move upward.
• Market-adjusted trade cost = 19.8 bps − 64.4 bps = -44.6 bps (i.e., trader OUTPERFORMED the market-adjusted arrival price by ~44.6 bps).

===
COMPARISON TO VWAP AND TWAP:
• Avg price $50.55 vs VWAP $50.52 → 5.9 bps unfavorable to VWAP benchmark.
• Avg price $50.55 vs TWAP $50.57 → 3.9 bps favorable to TWAP.
• Mixed relative-benchmark results.

PARTIAL: 5=arrival + market-adjusted analysis + benchmarks; 3=partial analysis; 0=incorrect direction.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two ways Mulaney's trading team can measure and improve trade execution quality over time.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted approaches:
• Systematically track IMPLEMENTATION SHORTFALL across trades and analyze the components (delay, execution, missed opportunity, commission).
• Benchmark performance vs multiple standards (arrival price, VWAP, TWAP, market-adjusted) to identify strengths and weaknesses.
• Analyze performance by algorithm type — identify which algorithms work best for what market conditions.
• Track BROKER performance separately and rotate work to best performers.
• Post-trade analysis / TCA (Transaction Cost Analysis) reports.
• Real-time monitoring of active orders vs benchmarks.
• Alpha decay analysis — measure how quickly information advantages disappear.
• Review venue analysis (which exchanges/dark pools provided best fills).

PARTIAL: 5=two clear approaches + implementation notes; 3=one; 0=vague.

cases.title: Quagmire Case (Steve Holt)
cases.vignette:
Steve Holt is the manager for Quagmire Funds. Quagmire has a number of
funds under management to meet different client needs.

In the UPX Fund, an investment in the new technology sector has
performed well and is expected to continue to do so. However, the
proportion of the fund invested in the new technology sector is now
larger than what is permitted in the fund prospectus. Holt intends to
reduce the holdings in the sector to comply with the prospectus over the
next two months before the quarter ends. The volume of shares that need
to be sold is approximately 20% of the daily volume in their respective
individual markets.

To execute the trades in the new technology sector, Holt asks his team
to consider three possible options:

Option 1: Have a broker act as a principal for the entire amount
that needs to be sold.

Option 2: Use a scheduled algorithm to execute the trade based on
available daily liquidity over eight weeks.

Option 3: Trade in a "dark pool" so as not to reveal any information
about the amount to be traded.

Upon completion of the trades, Holt calculates an expanded
implementation shortfall to evaluate trade costs. The data are provided
in Exhibit 1.

**Exhibit 1 Expanded Implementation Shortfall Information ($) for the
Trades in the New Technology Sector**

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Value of shares sold based on:
--------------------------------------------- -------------------------
decision price                                5,124,300

arrival price                                 5,175,800

actual trade execution prices                 5,545,600

Note: All shares were sold
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Holt asks his team to determine whether trading with a new securities
brokerage, Zavier Brokerage (ZB), appears to be viable. The team sent ZB
a buy order for 5,000 shares of XTV stock to test the brokerage's
trading abilities. The anticipated trade execution cost was 110.00 basis
points (bps), based on previous trading with other brokerages. Exhibit 2
displays ZB's execution performance.

Exhibit 2 ZB Trade Execution Performance

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
XTV ($)          Index ($)
----------------------------- --------------------- -------------------
Decision price                25.45                 12,750

Arrival price                 25.50                 12,740

Average trade price           25.75                 12,775

CAPM beta                     0.85                  1.00
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

questions.stem:
The primary reason for the planned trades of shares in the new
technology sector of the UPX Fund over the next few months *is most
likely* to:

rationales.A: The limits for particular fund holdings within a prospectus are to prevent unintended risk taking by not having the fund sufficiently diversified. Consequently, the reduction of holdings in the new technology sector is for risk management purposes as detailed by the prospectus.
rationales.B: Although a profit may be recognized by selling stocks in the new technology sector, risk management is the primary motive for planned trading because the proportion invested in this sector exceeds the limits imposed by the fund prospectus.
rationales.C: Although the selling of securities will produce cash, there is no indication that the fund requires cash for a redemption or to cover any immediate trading costs.

questions.stem:
Which trading option is *most *appropriate for selling securities
from the new technology sector of the UPX Fund?

rationales.A: Given that the trade can be executed over a two-month period, there is no reason to incur the expense of having a broker act as a principal trader for all the shares. If there were a need to trade the shares quickly, this kind of strategy could be warranted.
rationales.B: A scheduled algorithm over the eight-week period is the best option for selling the securities. There is sufficient liquidity in the market, because on any given day, the amount sold will be approximately 0.5% of the daily volume: 20% daily volume ÷ (5 days × 8 weeks) = 0.5%. The price impact of trading will be minimal, and there is no trade urgency given the time horizon for liquidating the shares.
rationales.C: A dark pool is more appropriate when a given trade can reveal valuable information about the security being traded. In this case, the trading has no information value and needs to eventually be fully executed; in addition, a dark pool does not guarantee execution.

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 1, the component of the expanded implementation
shortfall for the trading of the securities from the UPX Fund that
is most favorable to Quagmire Funds is the:

rationales.A: The delay cost component has less impact on the expanded IS than the trade cost component.
rationales.B: Expanded implementation shortfall (IS) = Delay cost + Trading cost + Opportunity cost + Fees. Note that to reflect that shares are being sold rather than purchased, the "number of shares sold" is a negative value.    > Delay cost = (Number of shares sold × Arrival price) -- (Number of > shares sold × Decision price) > >   = Value of shares sold at arrival price -- Value of shares sold > decision price > > Delay cost = (--$5,175,800) -- (--$5,124,300) = --$51,500, > >  which reduces the expanded IS.    > Trading cost = (Total shares sold × Execution price) -- (Number of > shares sold × Arrival price) > >   = Value of shares sold at execution price -- Value of shares sold at > arrival price > > Trading cost = (--$5,545,600) -- (--$5,175,800) = --$369,800, > >  which reduces the expanded IS.    Opportunity cost: Based on not selling the desired number of shares. All shares were sold, which makes this value zero. The trading cost component is the most favorable to Quagmire Funds because it reduces the expanded IS the most.
rationales.C: Because all the shares were sold, the opportunity cost is zero. The delay cost and trading cost components both reduced the expanded IS.

questions.stem:
Based on Exhibit 2, the market-adjusted cost of trade execution by
ZB is closest to:

rationales.A: It is the arrival cost less the index cost (98.04 -- 27.47 = 70.57), which is not adjusted for beta.
rationales.B: Market-adjusted cost (bps) = Arrival cost (bps) -- Beta × Index cost (bps).    > Arrival cost (in bps) = [(Average execution price -- Arrival price) ÷ > Arrival price] × 10,000 bps > > = [($25.75 -- $25.50) ÷ $25.50] × 10,000 bps = 98.04 bps. > > > > Index cost (in bps) = [(Average execution index price -- Arrival > index price) ÷ Arrival index price] × 10,000 bps > >   = [($12,775 -- $12,740) ÷ $12,740] × 10,000 bps = 27.47 bps. > > > > Market-adjusted cost = Arrival cost --Beta × Index cost > >   = 98.04 -- 0.85 × 27.47 = 74.69 bps.
rationales.C: The decision price, instead of the arrival price, is used for the arrival cost calculation.  [($25.75 -- $25.45) ÷ $25.45] × 10,000 bps = 117.88 bps.  ($12,775 -- $12,750) ÷ $12,750] × 10,000 bps = 19.61 bps.  The final calculation becomes 117.88 -- 0.85 × 19.61 = 101.21 bps.

questions.stem: For Holt's technology-sector reduction (20% of daily volume, two-month window), EVALUATE each of the three trade execution options: (1) broker as principal for entire amount, (2) scheduled algorithm over 8 weeks, (3) dark pool.

rationales.model_answer:
===
OPTION 1 (Broker as principal for entire amount): NOT APPROPRIATE.
• Principal risk transfer for 20% of daily volume requires broker to accept large inventory risk → very wide spread charged.
• Expensive way to execute a size that could be traded gradually.
• Information leakage: broker knows entire position.

===
OPTION 2 (Scheduled algorithm over 8 weeks): MOST APPROPRIATE.
• 20% of daily volume over 8 weeks = manageable participation rate.
• Minimizes market impact through gradual execution.
• Retains anonymity vs. principal trade.
• Aligned with Holt's two-month timeline.

===
OPTION 3 (Dark pool alone): PARTIALLY APPROPRIATE but INSUFFICIENT.
• Dark pools provide anonymity but limited liquidity for 20% of daily volume of technology names.
• Cannot complete the entire trade in dark pools alone.
• Best used as SUPPLEMENTAL execution alongside algorithm.

PARTIAL: 5=all three evaluated + Option 2 selected; 3=Option 2 identified + one other correctly evaluated; 0=chooses Option 1.

questions.stem: From Exhibit 1 IS data, CALCULATE the total implementation shortfall for Holt's trades and DECOMPOSE into delay cost and execution cost. Show work.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — full IS decomposition:

Paper portfolio value at DECISION price: $5,124,300 (sold based on decision price).
Portfolio value at ARRIVAL price: $5,175,800.
Actual proceeds from execution: $5,545,600.

1. DELAY COST (decision to arrival):
   For a SELL, favorable delay if arrival price > decision price.
   $5,175,800 − $5,124,300 = +$51,500 = FAVORABLE delay of ~1.00%.
   NOTE: this is a GAIN for the seller (price went up).

2. EXECUTION COST (arrival to actual execution):
   For a SELL, execution cost = arrival value − actual proceeds.
   $5,175,800 − $5,545,600 = -$369,800 = FAVORABLE execution of -7.15% (executed at HIGHER prices than arrival).
   Or: proceeds exceeded arrival value by $369,800.

3. TOTAL IMPLEMENTATION SHORTFALL:
   For sell: paper portfolio value − actual proceeds.
   $5,124,300 − $5,545,600 = -$421,300 = -8.22% (FAVORABLE — gained vs decision).

PARTIAL: 5=all components with correct signs; 4=arithmetic minor; 3=direction correct; 0=wrong sign.

questions.stem: Using Exhibit 2, EVALUATE whether Zavier Brokerage (ZB) is a viable trading partner. Show market-adjusted analysis.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — full analysis:

Trade: BUY 5,000 XTV. Decision $25.45; Arrival $25.50; Average trade $25.75.
Index: Decision 12,750; Arrival 12,740; Trade avg 12,775.
XTV Beta to index: 0.85.

===
RAW COST vs ARRIVAL:
($25.75 − $25.50) / $25.50 = 0.980% = 98 bps trade cost.

===
MARKET-ADJUSTED:
Index moved from 12,740 (arrival) to 12,775 (VWAP): +0.275%.
Expected XTV move due to index = 0.85 × 0.275% = 0.234%.
Market-adjusted cost = 0.980% − 0.234% = 0.746% = 74.6 bps.

===
VS EXPECTATION (110 bps):
Market-adjusted cost 74.6 bps < 110 bps expected → ZB OUTPERFORMED by ~35 bps.

===
CONCLUSION: YES, ZB is VIABLE.
• Beat expected cost by 35 bps on market-adjusted basis.
• Reasonable performance for initial test trade.

PARTIAL: 5=YES + full market-adjusted work; 4=YES + partial work; 3=YES without market-adjusted; 1=NO; 0=confused.

questions.stem: DISCUSS two considerations Holt should evaluate when deciding whether to use dark pools versus lit markets for large orders.

rationales.model_answer:
GRADING KEY — any TWO of the following.

Accepted considerations:
• INFORMATION LEAKAGE: dark pools reduce information leakage but may have front-running / fishing by pool participants.
• AVAILABLE LIQUIDITY: dark pools have limited depth; large orders may only partially fill.
• PRICE IMPROVEMENT: dark pools often execute at midpoint of NBBO — better than lit spreads.
• COUNTERPARTY RISK: dark pool counterparties may be adverse (e.g., HFTs detecting patterns).
• REGULATORY REPORTING: some dark pool trades have delayed public reporting.
• PRICE DISCOVERY: dark trades don't contribute to price discovery — market impact when eventually reported.
• EXECUTION SPEED: lit markets guarantee execution at posted prices; dark pools do not.
• FILLS QUALITY: dark pool fill rates can vary widely by venue.

PARTIAL: 5=two clear considerations; 3=one; 0=vague.
